title: Data Analysis in Business (First edition)
text:
Welcome
If you have ever stared at a spreadsheet and wondered, “What is this data actually telling me?” — then this book is for you.
Welcome to the ETF2121 / ETF5921 Handbook (1st Edition): a practical, hands-on guide to the data analysis skills that matter most in the real world. Whether you are a student stepping into data for the very first time, a working professional looking to sharpen your toolkit, or simply a curious mind who wants to make sense of numbers, you are in the right place.

title: Data Analysis in Business (First edition)
text:
What this book is about
Data is everywhere — in the businesses we run, the decisions we make, and the stories we tell. But raw data, on its own, says very little. The real magic happens when we learn how to ask the right questions, pull out the right information, and communicate what we find in a way that helps others make better decisions.
This handbook is built around that journey. Together, we will:

Query and shape data using SQL — the language of databases.
Explore and visualise patterns using R, a free and powerful tool used by analysts and researchers worldwide.
Build dashboards in Power BI that turn raw numbers into clear, compelling stories.
Apply modelling techniques to real-world problems — from forecasting demand to understanding customer behaviour.

Every chapter is written with one promise in mind: no prior experience required. If you can read, click, and copy a line of code, you can do this.

title: Data Analysis in Business (First edition)
text:
How to read this book
This is not a textbook you read once and shelve. It is designed as a companion: short enough to follow during a busy semester, deep enough to return to long after the course is over. Each chapter offers:

A clear walkthrough of the key ideas, in plain language.
Worked examples using real datasets — not toy problems.
Hands-on exercises that mirror the kinds of questions you will face at work.
A summary and further reading for those who want to go deeper.

Every tool used in this book is free and open-source. You will never need to buy a licence to follow along. All you need is a laptop, an internet connection, and a willingness to experiment.

title: Data Analysis in Business (First edition)
text:
A note from the author
I wrote this book for the student I once was — curious, a little overwhelmed, and unsure where to start. My hope is that by the final page, you will not only have the technical skills to analyse data confidently, but also the mindset to keep learning long after this course ends.
Mistakes are part of the journey, both yours and mine. If you spot one in these pages, or have an idea for how to make the book better, I would love to hear from you. Please reach out at joan.tan@monash.edu.
Thank you for reading, and welcome aboard.
Yours sincerely,
Dr. Joan Tan Senior Lecturer, Monash University

title: Course Structure
text:
Learning Objectives
This unit provides an overview of fundamental tools of data and statistical analysis used in the Business and Economics disciplines. The methods covered are widely used in industry and academia, providing the necessary foundation for more advanced approaches as you advance your training and career.
This course delves deeper into basic statistical concepts, with a focus on their application in finance, accounting, and other sectors. It introduces cutting-edge software and programming languages such as R, Power-BI, SQL and Excel for robust data analysis and predictive modelling. Students will engage with modern data sources and advanced sampling techniques, learn to perform hypothesis testing, and apply modelling techniques such as regression and time series analysis in business contexts. The course places a strong emphasis on practical applications, aiming to equip students with the skills to interpret and leverage statistical data for solving business problems and making informed decisions in industry.
This unit equips students with statistical techniques and the use of modern business software, including R, Excel, Power BI, for business applications. Students will learn to process and analyse extensive datasets using methods such as regression, time series analysis and classification. Key topics include modeling, regression analysis, and time series analysis in the business context.
On successful completion of this unit, you should be able to:

title: Course Structure
text:
Gain proficiency in business reporting tools and data analysis tool.
Learn to perform complex statistical analyses, including hypothesis testing and confidence intervals.
Use various predictive modeling technique to enhance forecasting and decision-making in business context.
Acquire skills to analyze and interpret real-time data for better business strategic planning and decision.

title: Course Structure
text:
Assessment
The assessment for this unit is designed to evaluate your understanding of the course material and your ability to apply the concepts learned in practical scenarios. The assessment consists of the following components: - Quizzes: There will be a series of quizzes throughout the semester to test your understanding of the material covered in the lectures and readings. These quizzes will be conducted online and will consist of multiple-choice questions, short answer questions, and problem-solving exercises. The quizzes will be timed and will be available for a limited period. - Individual Assignments: You will be required to complete a series of individual assignments that will involve applying the concepts learned in the course to real-world scenarios. These assignments will require you to analyze data, interpret results, and present your findings in a clear and concise manner. The assignments will be submitted online and will be graded based on the quality of your analysis and presentation. - Group Project: You will be required to work in groups to complete a project that involves analyzing a dataset and presenting your findings. The project will require you to apply the concepts learned in the course to a real-world problem and will involve data collection, analysis, and presentation. The project will be graded based on the quality of your analysis, the effectiveness of your presentation, and your ability to work as a team. - Final Exam: The final exam will be a comprehensive assessment of the material covered in the course. The exam will consist of multiple-choice questions, short answer questions, and problem-solving exercises. The exam will be conducted in person and will be timed.

title: 1  Database Management System
text:
1.1 A day in the life of a data analyst
Imagine it is your first day as a junior data analyst at a coffee chain with 200 stores across Australia. Your manager walks over with a friendly smile and asks:
Where do you even begin?
Every store records every transaction. Every transaction has a time, a barista, a customer, a drink, a size, a price, and a payment method. Multiply that by 200 stores, by tens of thousands of customers a day, by 30 days, and you are looking at millions of rows of data.
If somebody hands you a spreadsheet, your laptop will probably freeze before you can open it. And even if it did open, how would you find just the Melbourne stores, just the large oat-milk lattes, just the past 30 days — without scrolling for hours?
This is the moment a data analyst reaches for a database.

title: 1  Database Management System
text: “Hey, can you tell me how many large oat-milk lattes we sold in Melbourne last month, broken down by store and by day of the week? Oh, and our regional manager needs it before lunch.”

title: 1  Database Management System
section: 1.2 So… what is a database, really?
text:
1.2 So… what is a database, really?
A database is just an organised collection of data — but designed so that finding, updating, and protecting that data is fast, safe, and reliable, even when there are millions of records and hundreds of people using it at once.
You already use databases every day, even if you do not realise it:

When you log into Instagram, a database checks your password and pulls up your feed.
When you tap your Myki card, a database deducts the fare and updates your balance.
When Spotify recommends a song, it is querying a database of your listening history.
When your bank app shows your latest transaction, that record came from a database — within milliseconds.

A database is a quiet engine running in the background of almost every digital service we touch.

title: 1  Database Management System
section: 1.3 What is a Database Management System (DBMS)?
text:
1.3 What is a Database Management System (DBMS)?
A database is the data. A Database Management System (DBMS) is the software that lets people and applications interact with that data — to store it, retrieve it, change it, secure it, and back it up.
Think of it like this:

The database is the library — rows of shelves full of books.
The DBMS is the librarian — the one who knows where every book lives, lends them out, keeps the catalogue tidy, and stops you from walking off with the rare collection.

Without a librarian, even the best library is just a pile of books.
A DBMS quietly handles a lot of jobs that you would otherwise have to do by hand:

Storing data efficiently so it does not take up unnecessary space.
Finding data quickly when you ask a question, even across millions of rows.
Protecting data integrity so that, for example, a bank balance can never be in two contradictory states at once.
Controlling who can see and change what — the intern should not be able to delete payroll records.
Letting many people work at once without overwriting each other’s changes.
Backing up and recovering data if something goes wrong.

title: 1  Database Management System
text:
1.4 “But can’t I just use Excel?”
Excel is wonderful. It really is. For small datasets, quick calculations, and simple charts, it is hard to beat. Most data analysts open Excel several times a day.
But Excel was never designed to be a database. The cracks start to show the moment you push past a few thousand rows or need more than one person editing at once.
Picture a real-world example. A bank stores its customer records in Excel. What happens when:

Two staff members open the file at the same time and both make edits — whose changes are kept?
The dataset grows to 50 million rows — Excel itself caps out at around one million.
The file gets emailed around — which copy is the real one?
A staff member accidentally sorts only one column, leaving every row attached to the wrong customer.
A regulator asks: “Who changed this customer’s address last Tuesday at 3:14pm?”

Each of these is a nightmare in Excel. Each is a routine, solved problem in a DBMS.
Here is the key shift in thinking: a spreadsheet is a document; a database is a system. A document lives on someone’s laptop. A system serves many people, protects the data, and can be trusted as the single source of truth.

1.4.1 Spreadsheet vs. database at a glance








Feature
Database (DBMS)
Spreadsheet (Excel)

Up to ~1 million rows, slow well before that

A good rule of thumb: if more than one person depends on the data, or if the data is too big for your laptop to enjoy, it belongs in a database.

title: 1  Database Management System
section: 1.5 The most common kind: a Relational Database (RDBMS)
text:
1.5 The most common kind: a Relational Database (RDBMS)
There are several styles of database, but the one a data analyst encounters most often — by a wide margin — is the relational database, managed by a Relational Database Management System (RDBMS).
A relational database organises information into tables — and that one word is the key to everything.
A table is exactly what it sounds like:

Each column describes a property (e.g. customer_id, first_name, email).
Each row is one record — one customer, one order, one transaction.

Here is a tiny customers table:

So far this could be a spreadsheet. The magic of a relational database is that tables can be linked to each other.
Imagine our coffee chain has three tables:

customers — who the customer is.
orders — what they ordered, when, and at which store.
stores — where each store is located.

Each table on its own only tells part of the story. But because the orders table contains a customer_id (which links back to customers) and a store_id (which links back to stores), we can answer rich questions like “How many lattes did Melbourne customers buy on Saturdays?” by combining the tables.





erDiagram

    STORES ||--o{ ORDERS : "is sold at"
    CUSTOMERS {
        int customer_id PK
        string first_name
        string last_name

    }
    ORDERS {
        int order_id PK
        int customer_id FK
        int store_id FK
        datetime order_time
        string drink
        decimal price
    }
    STORES {
        int store_id PK
        string store_name
        string city
        string state
    }






The links between tables are made by special columns called keys:

A primary key is a column that uniquely identifies each row in a table — like customer_id in customers. No two customers can share one.
A foreign key is a column in one table that points to the primary key of another. In orders, customer_id is a foreign key — it tells us which customer placed the order.

This simple idea — unique IDs that connect tables — is the backbone of almost every business system you will ever work with. Once it clicks, a huge amount of the messy real world becomes tidy.

title: 1  Database Management System
section: 1.6 Where does SQL come into all this?
text:
1.6 Where does SQL come into all this?
You now understand the what (a database) and the who (the DBMS). The missing piece is the how: how do you actually ask a database a question?
The answer is SQL — short for Structured Query Language, and usually pronounced “sequel.”
SQL is the language we use to talk to a relational database. You write a sentence in SQL, the DBMS understands what you want, and it returns the answer. For example, to answer your manager’s coffee question, you might write:
SELECT store_name, COUNT(*) AS num_lattes
FROM orders
JOIN stores ON orders.store_id = stores.store_id
WHERE drink = 'Large Oat Latte'
  AND stores.city = 'Melbourne'
  AND order_time >= '2026-03-01'
GROUP BY store_name;
Do not worry if that looks like wizardry right now — by the end of this section, it will read almost like English. The point for now is: that one short query can sift through millions of rows in seconds. Try doing that in Excel.

1.6.1 Why every data analyst learns SQL
If there is one technical skill that appears in nearly every data-analyst job ad on the planet, it is SQL. Here is why it matters so much:

It is the universal language of data. Whether the data lives in PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, Snowflake, or BigQuery, the core syntax is almost identical. Learn it once, use it anywhere.
It is where the data lives. Most companies store their important data in relational databases. If you cannot query the database, you cannot do the analysis.
It is fast. A well-written SQL query can summarise hundreds of millions of rows in seconds — work that would crash any spreadsheet.
It is the entry point to everything else. Power BI dashboards, R analyses, Python models — they all begin with pulling data, and pulling data usually means SQL.

The good news? SQL is also one of the easiest programming languages to start with. It is declarative, which means you describe what you want, not how to get it. You say “give me all customers in Melbourne” and the database figures out the most efficient way to find them.

title: 1  Database Management System
section: 1.7 A first look at SQL commands
text:
1.7 A first look at SQL commands
SQL has a small vocabulary, but its words fall into three useful families. You do not need to memorise these now — just notice the shape of the language.
Data Definition Language (DDL) — for defining the structure of the database itself.

CREATE — make a new table, view, or index.
ALTER — change an existing table’s structure.
DROP — delete a table or other object permanently.
TRUNCATE — empty a table without deleting the table itself.

Data Manipulation Language (DML) — for changing the data inside the tables.

INSERT — add new rows.
UPDATE — change existing rows.
DELETE — remove rows.

Data Query Language (DQL) — for asking questions of the data. As an analyst, this is where you will spend almost all of your time.

SELECT — pick the columns you want to see.
WHERE — filter to just the rows you care about.
ORDER BY — sort the result.
GROUP BY and HAVING — summarise the data into groups.
JOIN — combine information across multiple tables.
DISTINCT — get rid of duplicates.
LIMIT — return only the first few rows.

Most data analysts write SELECT queries every single day, and rarely touch CREATE or DROP. Building tables is more often the job of a database administrator or data engineer. Still, it is useful to recognise these commands when you see them — and we will cover the essentials in the next chapter.

title: 1  Database Management System
section: 1.8 Getting your hands dirty: practising SQL for free
text:
1.8 Getting your hands dirty: practising SQL for free
Theory only takes you so far. The fastest way to get comfortable with SQL is to start writing it. Best of all, you do not need to install anything heavy on your laptop.
In this course, we will use FreeSQL — a free, browser-based SQL editor. You sign up once, log in, and can start writing real queries against a real database immediately.






TipTry it later: open-source alternatives



If you want to keep learning beyond this course, two excellent free, open-source databases to install on your own machine are:

SQLite — the lightest, simplest database in the world. A whole database lives in a single file. Perfect for personal projects.
PostgreSQL — the same industrial-strength database used by companies like Apple, Instagram, and Reddit. Free forever.

The SQL skills you build here will work, almost unchanged, on either.



1.8.1 Setting up FreeSQL (5 minutes)

Go to freesql.com.
Click Sign Up in the top-right corner.
Enter your name, email, and a password, then click Create Account.
Log in. You will land in an editor with a sample database ready to query.
Type a query, click Run (or press Ctrl + Enter), and watch the results appear.

That is it. You now have a real SQL playground.

title: 1  Database Management System
section: 1.9 Quick check: are you ready for the next chapter?
text:
1.9 Quick check: are you ready for the next chapter?
Before we move on, take a moment to make sure these ideas feel solid. If any of them are still fuzzy, give the chapter another quick skim — that is completely normal.

In your own words, why might a coffee chain prefer a database over a giant Excel file?
What is the difference between a database and a DBMS?
What makes a database “relational”? What does a primary key do, and what does a foreign key do?
Why is SQL a useful skill for a data analyst, even one who works mostly in Power BI or R?
Name one DDL command, one DML command, and one DQL command.







NoteIn a nutshell



A database is a structured store of information. A DBMS is the software that manages it. The most common kind, the relational database, organises data into tables linked by keys. To talk to it, we use SQL — the language at the heart of nearly every data analyst’s job.

title: 1  Database Management System
text:
1.10 What’s next
In the next chapter, we will roll up our sleeves and write our first real SQL queries — pulling data, filtering it, sorting it, and starting to answer questions like the one your manager threw at you on day one. By the end of the section, that coffee question will not feel scary at all.

title: 2  SQL Basics
text:
2.1 The FreshMart Dataset
Welcome to SQL Basics — the chapter where you learn to talk to databases, and databases (mostly) talk back.
SQL (Structured Query Language) is the universal language of data. It doesn’t matter if you’re working at a tiny startup or inside a corporation with more data than common sense — SQL is the tool that gets you to the numbers. Every data analyst uses it. Every data scientist knows it. Even Excel users secretly wish they had it.
To add a comment in SQL, use two dashes (--). SQL will politely ignore everything that follows — which is more than can be said for most group project feedback:
The basic building block of SQL is the query:
SQL keywords are case-insensitive — select and SELECT both work — but writing them in uppercase is convention because we’re professionals (or at least pretending to be). Always end your statement with a semicolon (;). Think of it as the full stop at the end of your sentence. SQL insists.
Throughout this chapter, we’ll work with data from FreshMart — a fictional Australian supermarket chain with a loyalty program, a suspiciously wide product range, and at least one customer who has never returned after signing up for the membership card.
We’ll be working with two tables. First, the Customers table, which tracks FreshMart loyalty members:
Churned = 1 means the customer has left FreshMart for a competitor. Gary McSnarl, for instance, found a 10-cent discount on milk elsewhere and was gone by Tuesday. Marcus Lee, on the other hand, has spent $12,300 — he is clearly doing the shopping for an entire extended family. Nina Patel signed up for the loyalty card at the checkout in 2024 and has not been back since. Jake Nguyen’s $340 annual spend is deeply relatable if you are a 22-year-old student.
Second, the Products table, which catalogues items sold at FreshMart:
IsOrganic = 1 means the product is certified organic. IsOrganic = 0 means it is not — but nobody is judging you for buying the Cola.

title: 2  SQL Basics
text:
2.2 Table Creation
It is not common for a data analyst to build database structures from scratch, but understanding the basics helps you read and work with existing databases confidently.

2.2.1 Creating a Table
Use CREATE TABLE to define a new table. You specify the table name, each column’s name, and its data type.
CREATE TABLE Customers (
    CustomerID     INT PRIMARY KEY,
    FirstName      VARCHAR(50),
    LastName       VARCHAR(50),
    Age            INT,
    Gender         CHAR(1),
    Suburb         VARCHAR(100),
    State          CHAR(3),
    MembershipTier VARCHAR(10),
    YearJoined     INT,
    TotalSpend     DECIMAL(10, 2),
    Churned        INT
);
INT stores whole numbers. VARCHAR(50) stores text up to 50 characters long. DECIMAL(10, 2) stores numbers with two decimal places — useful for money, because nobody wants to round Gary’s $520.00 to the nearest integer. PRIMARY KEY means this column uniquely identifies each row — no two customers can share a CustomerID.


2.2.2 Inserting Data
Use INSERT INTO to add a new row. Here is how FreshMart registered their most recent loyalty member:
INSERT INTO Customers (CustomerID, FirstName, LastName, Age, Gender, Suburb, State, MembershipTier, YearJoined, TotalSpend, Churned)
VALUES (7, 'Nina', 'Patel', 19, 'F', 'Brunswick', 'VIC', 'Bronze', 2024, 0.00, 0);
Nina has spent $0.00 so far. In her defence, she only signed up for the loyalty card because the checkout operator asked nicely.


2.2.3 Querying Data
Use SELECT to retrieve rows from a table. The asterisk * means “all columns”:
SELECT * FROM Customers;
This returns every column and every row in the table. Useful for a quick look, but in real analysis you will usually name the specific columns you need.


2.2.4 Updating Data
Use UPDATE to modify existing rows. Priya Sharma has just crossed into a new spending bracket:
UPDATE Customers
SET TotalSpend = 9000.00
WHERE CustomerID = 1;






WarningNever forget the WHERE clause in UPDATE!



Without it, you will update every single row in the table. Every customer’s TotalSpend becomes $9,000.00. FreshMart’s finance team will not be pleased, and neither will your exam marker.




2.2.5 Deleting Data
Use DELETE to remove rows. Gary McSnarl has officially churned and asked to be removed from the loyalty database:
DELETE FROM Customers
WHERE CustomerID = 6;






WarningNever forget the WHERE clause in DELETE either!



Without it, you delete every customer. Every single one. Marcus, Priya, even Nina who hasn’t bought anything yet — all gone. Always double-check your WHERE clause before running a delete.



2.2.5.1 Exercises

Create a table called Transactions with columns for TransactionID, CustomerID, ProductID, Quantity, and TransactionDate.







NoteSolution





CREATE TABLE Transactions (
    TransactionID   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    CustomerID      INT,
    ProductID       INT,
    Quantity        INT,
    TransactionDate DATE
);




Insert two rows into the Transactions table — one for Marcus buying Chicken Breast, and one for Jake buying Cola.







NoteSolution





INSERT INTO Transactions (TransactionID, CustomerID, ProductID, Quantity, TransactionDate)
VALUES
(1, 4, 6, 3, '2024-06-15'),
(2, 2, 5, 2, '2024-07-01');




Update Bev O’Brien’s Churned status from 1 to 0 — she came back, apparently the competitor was out of Sourdough.







NoteSolution





UPDATE Customers
SET Churned = 0
WHERE CustomerID = 3;




Delete the product with ProductID equal to 5.







NoteSolution





DELETE FROM Products
WHERE ProductID = 5;
-- Goodbye, Cola. You will be missed by Jake Nguyen.




Write a query to select all data from the Products table.







NoteSolution





SELECT * FROM Products;




You now know how to create and manage tables. But as a data analyst, you will spend almost all your time querying data that already exists. That is where the real action is.

title: 2  SQL Basics
section: 2.3 SQL for Data Querying and Manipulation
text:
2.3 SQL for Data Querying and Manipulation

2.3.1 Selecting Data
The SELECT statement retrieves data from a table. You list the columns you want, separated by commas:
SELECT column1, column2, ...
FROM table_name;
To see just the names and membership tiers of FreshMart’s customers — the useful columns, not the internal IDs:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, MembershipTier
FROM Customers;



FirstName
LastName
MembershipTier




Priya
Sharma
Gold


Jake
Nguyen
Bronze


Bev
O’Brien
Silver


Marcus
Lee
Gold


Amara
Diallo
Silver


Gary
McSnarl
Bronze


Nina
Patel
Bronze



To see just the product names and prices from the Products table:
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products;


2.3.2 Filtering Data
The WHERE clause filters rows based on a condition. Only rows that satisfy the condition are returned — everything else is quietly discarded.
SELECT column1, column2, ...
FROM table_name
WHERE condition;
Show only customers who have spent more than $1,000 at FreshMart:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
WHERE TotalSpend > 1000;



FirstName
LastName
TotalSpend




Priya
Sharma
8420.50


Bev
O’Brien
3150.75


Marcus
Lee
12300.00


Amara
Diallo
1890.25



Jake and Nina did not make the cut. Jake is not surprised — he is 22. Nina is technically surprised because she forgot she had a loyalty card.


2.3.3 Sorting Data
Use ORDER BY to sort your results. ASC is ascending (A–Z, smallest–largest) and DESC is descending (Z–A, largest–smallest). The default is ascending.
Let’s rank customers from highest to lowest total spend:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC;



FirstName
LastName
TotalSpend




Marcus
Lee
12300.00


Priya
Sharma
8420.50


Bev
O’Brien
3150.75


Amara
Diallo

Gary
McSnarl
520.00


Jake
Nguyen
340.00


Nina
Patel
0.00



Marcus is comfortably in first place. Nina is in last place. Nina is unbothered.


2.3.4 Distinct Values
The DISTINCT keyword returns only unique values — duplicates are removed. Useful when you want a list of categories, not a full row-by-row count.
Which product categories does FreshMart stock?
SELECT DISTINCT Category
FROM Products;
This returns each category name exactly once, even if multiple products share the same category (as is the case with Dairy and Beverages).
Which states are FreshMart customers from?
SELECT DISTINCT State
FROM Customers;


2.3.5 Multiple Conditions
You can combine conditions with logical operators: AND, OR, and NOT.
Customers who joined after 2020 and have spent more than $500 — your recent-but-engaged members:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, YearJoined, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
WHERE YearJoined > 2020 AND TotalSpend > 500;
Customers who are either churned or have spent nothing — potential re-engagement targets:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend, Churned
FROM Customers
WHERE Churned = 1 OR TotalSpend = 0;
Products that are not organic:
SELECT ProductName, IsOrganic
FROM Products
WHERE NOT IsOrganic = 1;

2.3.5.1 Exercises

Write a query to select all customers who joined after 2020.
Write a query to select distinct states from the Customers table.
Write a query to select customers with a TotalSpend between $300 and $2,000.
Write a query to find products that are organic and cost less than $7.00.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Customers who joined after 2020
SELECT FirstName, LastName, YearJoined
FROM Customers
WHERE YearJoined > 2020;

-- 2. Distinct states
SELECT DISTINCT State
FROM Customers;

-- 3. Customers with TotalSpend between $300 and $2,000
SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
WHERE TotalSpend BETWEEN 300 AND 2000;

-- 4. Organic products under $7.00
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice, IsOrganic
FROM Products
WHERE IsOrganic = 1 AND UnitPrice < 7.00;






2.3.6 Aggregating Data
Aggregate functions summarise many rows into a single number. These are the workhorses of data analysis — you will use them constantly.



Function
What it does




COUNT()
Counts the number of rows


SUM()
Adds up all values


AVG()
Calculates the average


MIN()
Finds the smallest value


MAX()
Finds the largest value



How many customers does FreshMart have on its loyalty database?
SELECT COUNT(*) AS TotalCustomers
FROM Customers;
The result is 7. Seven customers, one of whom has spent nothing and one of whom has spent enough to feed a village.
What is the total lifetime spend across all customers?
SELECT SUM(TotalSpend) AS TotalRevenue
FROM Customers;
What is the average unit price of products in the store?
SELECT AVG(UnitPrice) AS AvgUnitPrice
FROM Products;
What is the price range — cheapest to most expensive?
SELECT MIN(UnitPrice) AS CheapestProduct, MAX(UnitPrice) AS MostExpensiveProduct
FROM Products;
The Cola at $2.80 is the cheapest. The Almond Butter at $9.99 is the most expensive. Whether that is fair is a matter of personal philosophy.

2.3.6.1 Exercises

Write a query to count the total number of products in the Products table.
Write a query to find the average TotalSpend across all customers.
Write a query to find the product with the highest UnitPrice.
Write a query to find the total TotalSpend across all customers who have not churned.
Write a query to find the earliest year a customer joined FreshMart.
Write a query to find the minimum and maximum UnitPrice among organic products only.
Write a query to count the number of customers who have spent more than $0.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Total number of products
SELECT COUNT(*) AS TotalProducts
FROM Products;

-- 2. Average customer spend
SELECT AVG(TotalSpend) AS AvgTotalSpend
FROM Customers;

-- 3. Most expensive product
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
WHERE UnitPrice = (SELECT MAX(UnitPrice) FROM Products);

-- 4. Total spend among non-churned customers
SELECT SUM(TotalSpend) AS ActiveCustomerRevenue
FROM Customers
WHERE Churned = 0;

-- 5. Earliest year joined
SELECT MIN(YearJoined) AS EarliestJoinYear
FROM Customers;

-- 6. Price range for organic products
SELECT MIN(UnitPrice) AS CheapestOrganic, MAX(UnitPrice) AS MostExpensiveOrganic
FROM Products
WHERE IsOrganic = 1;

-- 7. Customers who have actually spent something
SELECT COUNT(*) AS SpendingCustomers
FROM Customers
WHERE TotalSpend > 0;






2.3.7 WHERE Clause (More Detail)
The WHERE clause supports a full range of comparison operators:



Operator
Meaning




=
Equal to


!= or <>
Not equal to


>
Greater than


<
Less than


>=
Greater than or equal to


<=
Less than or equal to



Customers with a TotalSpend strictly greater than $1,000:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
WHERE TotalSpend > 1000;
Products whose price is not equal to $3.49 (i.e., not the Full Cream Milk):
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
WHERE UnitPrice != 3.49;

2.3.7.1 Exercises

Write a query to find all products with a UnitPrice less than $5.00.
Write a query to find customers who joined before 2022 and have spent more than $1,000.
Write a query to find customers who are from QLD or have a TotalSpend of exactly $0.00.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Budget-friendly products
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
WHERE UnitPrice < 5.00;

-- 2. Long-term, high-value customers
SELECT FirstName, LastName, YearJoined, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
WHERE YearJoined < 2022 AND TotalSpend > 1000;

-- 3. QLD customers or zero-spend members
SELECT FirstName, LastName, State, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
WHERE State = 'QLD' OR TotalSpend = 0.00;






2.3.8 GROUP BY Clause
GROUP BY groups rows that share the same value in a column, then lets you apply aggregate functions to each group separately. Think of it as sorting your data into buckets and doing maths on each bucket.
SELECT column1, aggregate_function(column2)
FROM table_name
GROUP BY column1;
How many customers does FreshMart have in each state?
SELECT State, COUNT(*) AS NumberOfCustomers
FROM Customers
GROUP BY State;
This groups the Customers rows by state and counts how many are in each. VIC has a strong showing.



State
NumberOfCustomers




NSW
2


QLD
1


VIC
4



What is the average TotalSpend by membership tier?
SELECT MembershipTier, AVG(TotalSpend) AS AvgSpend
FROM Customers
GROUP BY MembershipTier;
This tells you, at a glance, how much each tier tends to spend — useful for understanding whether the loyalty tiers are doing their job.

2.3.8.1 Exercises

Write a query to find the total TotalSpend grouped by State in the Customers table.
Write a query to count the number of customers in each MembershipTier.
Write a query to find the maximum TotalSpend by MembershipTier.
Write a query to find the average UnitPrice by Category in the Products table.
Write a query to count the number of products in each Category.
Write a query to find the total TotalSpend for churned vs non-churned customers (group by Churned).
Write a query to find the number of customers who joined in each year, grouped by YearJoined.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Total spend by state
SELECT State, SUM(TotalSpend) AS TotalStateSpend
FROM Customers
GROUP BY State;

-- 2. Customer count by membership tier
SELECT MembershipTier, COUNT(*) AS CustomerCount
FROM Customers
GROUP BY MembershipTier;

-- 3. Highest spender by membership tier
SELECT MembershipTier, MAX(TotalSpend) AS MaxSpend
FROM Customers
GROUP BY MembershipTier;

-- 4. Average unit price by product category
SELECT Category, AVG(UnitPrice) AS AvgPrice
FROM Products
GROUP BY Category;

-- 5. Number of products per category
SELECT Category, COUNT(*) AS ProductCount
FROM Products
GROUP BY Category;

-- 6. Total spend by churn status
SELECT Churned, SUM(TotalSpend) AS TotalSpend
FROM Customers
GROUP BY Churned;

-- 7. Customers joining each year
SELECT YearJoined, COUNT(*) AS NewMembers
FROM Customers
GROUP BY YearJoined;






2.3.9 HAVING Clause
HAVING is like WHERE for groups — it filters the results after GROUP BY has been applied. Use it when your condition involves an aggregate function like COUNT() or AVG().
SELECT column1, aggregate_function(column2)
FROM table_name
GROUP BY column1
HAVING condition;
Find membership tiers where the average TotalSpend exceeds $3,000:
SELECT MembershipTier, AVG(TotalSpend) AS AvgSpend
FROM Customers
GROUP BY MembershipTier
HAVING AVG(TotalSpend) > 3000;
Only the Gold tier qualifies — which is exactly what you’d hope for from your Gold members.
Find product categories where the average unit price is above $6.00:
SELECT Category, AVG(UnitPrice) AS AvgPrice
FROM Products
GROUP BY Category
HAVING AVG(UnitPrice) > 6.00;






TipWHERE vs HAVING — Know the Difference




WHERE filters individual rows before grouping. Use it on regular column values.
HAVING filters groups after grouping. Use it on the result of aggregate functions.

This query will throw an error because you cannot use an aggregate function inside WHERE:
-- ❌ This does NOT work
SELECT MembershipTier, AVG(TotalSpend)
FROM Customers
WHERE AVG(TotalSpend) > 3000
GROUP BY MembershipTier;
This is the correct version:
-- ✅ This works
SELECT MembershipTier, AVG(TotalSpend)
FROM Customers
GROUP BY MembershipTier
HAVING AVG(TotalSpend) > 3000;



2.3.9.1 Exercises

Write a query to find states where the total TotalSpend of all customers exceeds $5,000.
Write a query to find product categories that contain more than 1 product.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. States with combined customer spend above $5,000
SELECT State, SUM(TotalSpend) AS TotalStateSpend
FROM Customers
GROUP BY State
HAVING SUM(TotalSpend) > 5000;

-- 2. Categories with more than one product
SELECT Category, COUNT(*) AS ProductCount
FROM Products
GROUP BY Category
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;






2.3.10 NULL Values
NULL in SQL means unknown or missing. It is not zero, it is not an empty string, and it is not false. It is the database equivalent of a friend saying “I’ll let you know” and then never letting you know.
Note that Nina Patel’s TotalSpend is 0.00, not NULL — she has a recorded value of zero. A NULL would mean the data is entirely absent, which is a different situation. In a larger database, you might encounter NULL in columns like LastName (for a customer who only provided a first name) or State (if the address was not captured).
You cannot compare NULL with =. This will silently return nothing:
-- ❌ Does NOT work as expected
SELECT FirstName FROM Customers WHERE State = NULL;
Instead, use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL:
-- Customers with no state on record
SELECT FirstName, LastName, State
FROM Customers
WHERE State IS NULL;

-- Customers who definitely have a state recorded
SELECT FirstName, LastName, State
FROM Customers
WHERE State IS NOT NULL;

2.3.10.1 Exercises

Write a query to find all products whose Brand is NULL.
Write a query to find all customers whose Suburb is not NULL.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Products with no brand on file
SELECT ProductName
FROM Products
WHERE Brand IS NULL;

-- 2. Customers with a recorded suburb
SELECT FirstName, LastName, Suburb
FROM Customers
WHERE Suburb IS NOT NULL;






2.3.11 LIMIT Clause
The LIMIT clause caps the number of rows returned. Useful when you want a quick peek at a large table, or when you are hunting for the top or bottom performers.
SELECT *
FROM Products
LIMIT 3;
This returns only the first 3 rows. To find the 3 most expensive products:
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
ORDER BY UnitPrice DESC
LIMIT 3;
That would be Almond Butter ($9.99), Chicken Breast ($8.50), and Free Range Eggs ($7.99). Healthy eating is not cheap.






TipSQL Dialect Note



LIMIT works in MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite. In Microsoft SQL Server, use SELECT TOP n instead:
SELECT TOP 3 ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
ORDER BY UnitPrice DESC;
When in doubt, ask your AI assistant which variant your database system uses.



2.3.11.1 Exercises

Write a query to find the 3 customers with the highest TotalSpend.
Write a query to find the cheapest product in the store — return just 1 row.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Top 3 spenders
SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC
LIMIT 3;

-- 2. The cheapest product
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
ORDER BY UnitPrice ASC
LIMIT 1;
-- That's the Cola at $2.80. Jake approves.






2.3.12 LIKE Operator
LIKE lets you search for text patterns inside a column. It is used inside the WHERE clause and works with two wildcards:

% — matches any sequence of characters (including no characters at all)
_ — matches exactly one character

Products whose name starts with “Organic”:
SELECT ProductName
FROM Products
WHERE ProductName LIKE 'Organic%';
This returns Organic Oats 500g. Clean eating, found efficiently.
Products whose name contains the word “Butter”:
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
WHERE ProductName LIKE '%Butter%';
Customers whose last name starts with “P”:
SELECT FirstName, LastName
FROM Customers
WHERE LastName LIKE 'P%';

2.3.12.1 Exercises

Write a query to find all products whose ProductName contains the word “Chocolate”.
Write a query to find products whose ProductName ends with “6pk”.
Write a query to find customers whose FirstName starts with “A”.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Chocolate products (there is one, and it is important)
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
WHERE ProductName LIKE '%Chocolate%';

-- 2. Products sold in six-packs
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
WHERE ProductName LIKE '%6pk';

-- 3. Customers whose first name starts with A
SELECT FirstName, LastName
FROM Customers
WHERE FirstName LIKE 'A%';
-- That's Amara Diallo.






2.3.13 IN Operator
The IN operator checks if a value matches any value in a list. It is a clean alternative to chaining multiple OR conditions.
SELECT column1, column2
FROM table_name
WHERE column_name IN (value1, value2, value3);
Find customers who are Gold or Silver members:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, MembershipTier
FROM Customers
WHERE MembershipTier IN ('Gold', 'Silver');
Much neater than writing:
WHERE MembershipTier = 'Gold' OR MembershipTier = 'Silver'
You can also use NOT IN to exclude specific values:
-- Products NOT in the Dairy or Beverages categories
SELECT ProductName, Category
FROM Products
WHERE Category NOT IN ('Dairy', 'Beverages');

2.3.13.1 Exercises

Write a query to find customers from VIC, NSW, or QLD.
Write a query to find products that are not in the Bakery or Snacks categories.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Customers from major eastern states
SELECT FirstName, LastName, State
FROM Customers
WHERE State IN ('VIC', 'NSW', 'QLD');

-- 2. Products outside Bakery and Snacks
SELECT ProductName, Category
FROM Products
WHERE Category NOT IN ('Bakery', 'Snacks');






2.3.14 BETWEEN Operator
BETWEEN filters for values within a range. It is inclusive — both the lower and upper bounds are included in the results. It works with numbers, dates, and text.
SELECT column1, column2
FROM table_name
WHERE column_name BETWEEN value1 AND value2;
Products priced between $3.00 and $7.00:
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
WHERE UnitPrice BETWEEN 3.00 AND 7.00;
Customers who joined between 2019 and 2022 (inclusive):
SELECT FirstName, LastName, YearJoined
FROM Customers
WHERE YearJoined BETWEEN 2019 AND 2022;

2.3.14.1 Exercises

Write a query to find products with a UnitPrice between $5.00 and $9.00.
Write a query to find customers with a TotalSpend between $1,000 and $10,000.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Mid-range products
SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
WHERE UnitPrice BETWEEN 5.00 AND 9.00;

-- 2. Mid-range spenders
SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
WHERE TotalSpend BETWEEN 1000 AND 10000;






2.3.15 Aliases with AS
Aliases give a column or table a temporary name in your query results. They do not change the actual database — they just make the output easier to read and share.
SELECT column_name AS alias_name
FROM table_name AS table_alias;
Let’s produce a readable customer report instead of raw column names:
SELECT FirstName AS First, LastName AS Last, TotalSpend AS LifetimeValue, MembershipTier AS Tier
FROM Customers AS C;
Aliases are especially useful with aggregate functions, which otherwise produce awkward output names like COUNT(*) or AVG(TotalSpend):
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS TotalCustomers,
    AVG(TotalSpend) AS AvgLifetimeValue,
    SUM(TotalSpend) AS TotalRevenue
FROM Customers;
Without aliases, your column header would literally say COUNT(*). Not a great look in a client dashboard.

2.3.15.1 Exercises

Write a query to select ProductName and UnitPrice from Products, renaming them as Product and Price.
Write a query that counts all products and calculates the average UnitPrice, with clear aliases on both output columns.







NoteSolutions





-- 1. Rename product columns
SELECT ProductName AS Product, UnitPrice AS Price
FROM Products;

-- 2. Product summary with clean aliases
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS TotalProducts,
    AVG(UnitPrice) AS AvgPrice
FROM Products;




You have now covered the essential SQL toolkit for data analysis: selecting, filtering, sorting, aggregating, grouping, and pattern matching. In the next chapter, we’ll level up with SQL joins — combining multiple tables to answer bigger questions, like which customers bought which products, and whether Marcus Lee is single-handedly keeping the Meat & Seafood category profitable. Stay tuned.

title: 3  Advanced SQL
text:
3.1 The FreshMart Database
In the previous chapter, you learned how to query a single table — filtering, sorting, grouping, and summarising data. That is a solid foundation. But in the real world, data is never conveniently packed into one table. A company’s database might have dozens of tables, each storing a different piece of the puzzle. Advanced SQL is how you put those pieces together.
In this chapter, we will cover:
All examples use the FreshMart database — a fictional Australian supermarket chain with customers, products, stores, and transaction records.
We are working with four tables.
Customers:
Products:
Stores:
Transactions:

title: 3  Advanced SQL
text:
3.2 JOINs
A JOIN combines rows from two tables based on a related column. The Transactions table stores CustomerID and StoreID — numbers that point to records in the Customers and Stores tables. On its own, a table full of ID numbers is not very useful. JOINs let you pull in the actual names and details from the other tables.
The general syntax is:
SELECT t1.column1, t2.column2, ...
FROM table1 AS t1
JOIN table2 AS t2
  ON t1.shared_column = t2.shared_column;
The ON clause specifies the condition that links the two tables — usually a foreign key in one table matched to a primary key in another. You must qualify column names with the table alias (e.g., T.CustomerID instead of just CustomerID) when the same column name exists in multiple tables.


3.2.1 INNER JOIN
An INNER JOIN returns only the rows that have a matching value in both tables. If a row has no match in the other table, it is excluded entirely.
SELECT T.TransactionID,
       C.FirstName,
       C.LastName,
       S.StoreName,
       T.TransactionDate,
       T.TotalAmount
FROM Transactions AS T
INNER JOIN Customers AS C
  ON T.CustomerID = C.CustomerID
INNER JOIN Stores AS S
  ON T.StoreID = S.StoreID;
This query joins all three tables at once. The result replaces the ID numbers with actual names:

3.2.2 LEFT JOIN
A LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table, plus the matching rows from the right table. Where there is no match, the right table’s columns come back as NULL.
This is the most useful JOIN for analysts. Use it when you want to see everything from one table regardless of whether it has matching records in another — for example, to find customers who have never made a purchase.

3.2.3 RIGHT JOIN
A RIGHT JOIN is the mirror image of LEFT JOIN: it returns all rows from the right table, plus matching rows from the left table. Unmatched left-table rows become NULL.

TipLEFT JOIN vs RIGHT JOIN — A Practical Tip



Most analysts write everything as a LEFT JOIN and simply swap the table order when needed. A RIGHT JOIN of A → B returns the same result as a LEFT JOIN of B → A. Sticking to LEFT JOIN throughout makes queries easier to read.
-- These two queries return the same result:

-- RIGHT JOIN version
SELECT S.StoreName, T.TransactionID
FROM Transactions AS T
RIGHT JOIN Stores AS S
  ON T.StoreID = S.StoreID;

-- Equivalent LEFT JOIN version (just swap the tables)
SELECT S.StoreName, T.TransactionID
FROM Stores AS S
LEFT JOIN Transactions AS T
  ON S.StoreID = T.StoreID;

3.2.4.1 Exercises

Write a query to show each transaction’s ID, the customer’s first and last name, the store name, and the total amount.







NoteSolution





SELECT T.TransactionID,
       C.FirstName,
       C.LastName,
       S.StoreName,
       T.TotalAmount
FROM Transactions AS T
INNER JOIN Customers AS C
  ON T.CustomerID = C.CustomerID
INNER JOIN Stores AS S
  ON T.StoreID = S.StoreID;




Write a query to show all stores and the total number of transactions recorded at each. Include stores with zero transactions, showing 0 rather than NULL.







NoteSolution





SELECT S.StoreName, COUNT(T.TransactionID) AS TotalTransactions
FROM Stores AS S
LEFT JOIN Transactions AS T
  ON S.StoreID = T.StoreID
GROUP BY S.StoreName;
-- COUNT(T.TransactionID) counts only non-NULL values,
-- so stores with no transactions correctly get 0.




Write a query to show all customers who have made at least one transaction, along with the transaction date and amount.







NoteSolution





SELECT C.FirstName, C.LastName, T.TransactionDate, T.TotalAmount
FROM Customers AS C
INNER JOIN Transactions AS T
  ON C.CustomerID = T.CustomerID;
-- INNER JOIN automatically excludes customers with no transactions.




Write a query to find which customers have never made a transaction.







NoteSolution





SELECT C.FirstName, C.LastName
FROM Customers AS C
LEFT JOIN Transactions AS T
  ON C.CustomerID = T.CustomerID
WHERE T.TransactionID IS NULL;
-- Only Nina Patel returns.




Write a query to list all transactions made at stores in VIC, showing the customer name, store name, transaction date, and total amount.







NoteSolution





SELECT C.FirstName, C.LastName, S.StoreName, T.TransactionDate, T.TotalAmount
FROM Transactions AS T
INNER JOIN Customers AS C
  ON T.CustomerID = C.CustomerID
INNER JOIN Stores AS S
  ON T.StoreID = S.StoreID
WHERE S.State = 'VIC';

title: 3  Advanced SQL
text:
3.3 CASE WHEN
CASE WHEN is SQL’s version of if-else logic. It lets you create new calculated columns based on conditions — classifying values, creating labels, flagging rows — all inside a single query without touching the raw data.
SELECT column1,
       CASE
           WHEN condition1 THEN result1
           WHEN condition2 THEN result2
           ELSE default_result
       END AS new_column_name
FROM table_name;
SQL checks each WHEN condition from top to bottom and returns the first one that is true. If none match, the ELSE value is used. If there is no ELSE and nothing matches, the result is NULL.
Classify customers into spend tiers based on their lifetime TotalSpend:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend,
       CASE
           WHEN TotalSpend >= 5000 THEN 'High Spender'
           WHEN TotalSpend >= 1000 THEN 'Mid Spender'
           ELSE 'Light Spender'
       END AS SpendTier
FROM Customers;

Nina Patel is officially a Light Spender with zero dollars spent. Her classification is technically accurate. Gary McSnarl spent $520 before churning — apparently he found cheaper milk elsewhere and never looked back.
You can also combine MembershipTier and TotalSpend for a more nuanced classification:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, MembershipTier, TotalSpend,
       CASE
           WHEN MembershipTier = 'Gold'   AND TotalSpend >= 5000 THEN 'Loyal VIP'
           WHEN MembershipTier = 'Gold'   AND TotalSpend <  5000 THEN 'Gold Underperformer'
           WHEN MembershipTier = 'Silver'                        THEN 'Growing'
           WHEN MembershipTier = 'Bronze' AND Churned = 1        THEN 'Lost'
           ELSE 'New'
       END AS CustomerSegment
FROM Customers;
You can also use CASE WHEN inside aggregate functions. This pattern is used constantly for pivot-style summaries:
-- Count churned vs active customers in a single query
SELECT
    SUM(CASE WHEN Churned = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS ChurnedCount,
    SUM(CASE WHEN Churned = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS ActiveCount
FROM Customers;

3.3.0.1 Exercises

Write a query to classify each customer by MembershipTier into a descriptive label:

'Gold' → 'Premium Member'
'Silver' → 'Standard Member'
'Bronze' → 'Entry Member'








NoteSolution





SELECT FirstName, LastName, MembershipTier,
       CASE
           WHEN MembershipTier = 'Gold'   THEN 'Premium Member'
           WHEN MembershipTier = 'Silver' THEN 'Standard Member'
           ELSE 'Entry Member'
       END AS MemberLabel
FROM Customers;




Write a query to classify each product by UnitPrice:

Under $4.00 → 'Budget'
$4.00 to $7.99 → 'Mid-Range'
$8.00 or above → 'Premium'








NoteSolution





SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice,
       CASE
           WHEN UnitPrice < 4.00  THEN 'Budget'
           WHEN UnitPrice < 8.00  THEN 'Mid-Range'
           ELSE 'Premium'
       END AS PriceCategory
FROM Products;




Write a query using the Transactions table to add a BasketSize column:

NumItems of 5 or fewer → 'Small'
6 to 14 → 'Medium'
15 or more → 'Large'








NoteSolution





SELECT TransactionID, NumItems,
       CASE
           WHEN NumItems <= 5  THEN 'Small'
           WHEN NumItems <= 14 THEN 'Medium'
           ELSE 'Large'
       END AS BasketSize
FROM Transactions;




Write a query to count how many customers fall into each spend tier (High Spender / Mid Spender / Light Spender).







NoteSolution





SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN TotalSpend >= 5000 THEN 'High Spender'
        WHEN TotalSpend >= 1000 THEN 'Mid Spender'
        ELSE 'Light Spender'
    END AS SpendTier,
    COUNT(*) AS NumberOfCustomers
FROM Customers
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN TotalSpend >= 5000 THEN 'High Spender'
        WHEN TotalSpend >= 1000 THEN 'Mid Spender'
        ELSE 'Light Spender'
    END;

title: 3  Advanced SQL
text:
3.4 Subqueries
A subquery is a query nested inside another query. The inner query runs first, and its result is handed to the outer query to use. This lets you answer two-step questions: “first calculate this, then use that result to filter or compute something else.”
Subqueries appear in three places:

Inside WHERE — to filter using a calculated value
Inside FROM — to create a temporary derived table
Inside SELECT — to add a per-row calculated column


3.4.1 Subqueries in WHERE
Find customers whose lifetime TotalSpend is above the average:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend
FROM Customers
WHERE TotalSpend > (SELECT AVG(TotalSpend) FROM Customers);
The inner query runs first and returns the average spend (approximately $3,803). The outer query then uses that value to filter rows.



FirstName
LastName
TotalSpend




Priya
Sharma
8420.50


Marcus
Lee
12300.00



Marcus Lee and Priya Sharma are the only two customers above average — which is less surprising when you notice that Marcus spent $210 and $190 in a single month. He is clearly shopping for a village.
Find transactions where the basket total exceeds the average transaction amount:
SELECT TransactionID, TotalAmount
FROM Transactions
WHERE TotalAmount > (SELECT AVG(TotalAmount) FROM Transactions);


3.4.2 Subqueries in FROM
A subquery in the FROM clause creates a temporary derived table that the outer query treats like a real table. You must give it an alias.
Calculate the average transaction amount per state by first joining transactions to stores, then grouping in the outer query:
SELECT State, AVG(TotalAmount) AS AvgTransactionAmount
FROM (
    SELECT T.TotalAmount, S.State
    FROM Transactions AS T
    INNER JOIN Stores AS S
      ON T.StoreID = S.StoreID
) AS TransactionsByState
GROUP BY State;
The inner query builds a table that links each transaction to the store’s state. The outer query then groups and aggregates it. In SQL Server, you cannot reference a column alias created in SELECT directly inside GROUP BY — wrapping it in a subquery solves this cleanly.


3.4.3 Subqueries in SELECT
A subquery in the SELECT clause adds a calculated value to every row. It must return exactly one value (a scalar).
Show each transaction alongside the overall average transaction amount and the difference:
SELECT TransactionID, TotalAmount,
       (SELECT AVG(TotalAmount) FROM Transactions) AS AvgAmount,
       TotalAmount - (SELECT AVG(TotalAmount) FROM Transactions) AS DifferenceFromAvg
FROM Transactions;

3.4.3.1 Exercises

Write a query to find all products whose UnitPrice is above the average unit price.

SELECT ProductName, UnitPrice
FROM Products
WHERE UnitPrice > (SELECT AVG(UnitPrice) FROM Products);




Write a query to find all transactions where TotalAmount is below the average transaction amount.







NoteSolution





SELECT TransactionID, TotalAmount
FROM Transactions
WHERE TotalAmount < (SELECT AVG(TotalAmount) FROM Transactions);




Write a query to show each customer’s name, their TotalSpend, the maximum TotalSpend across all customers, and the gap between their spend and the top spender.







NoteSolution





SELECT FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend,
       (SELECT MAX(TotalSpend) FROM Customers) AS TopSpend,
       (SELECT MAX(TotalSpend) FROM Customers) - TotalSpend AS GapFromTop
FROM Customers;




Using a subquery in FROM, write a query to find the average TotalAmount per membership tier, but only include tiers where the average transaction exceeds $60.







NoteSolution





SELECT MembershipTier, AvgTransaction
FROM (
    SELECT C.MembershipTier, AVG(T.TotalAmount) AS AvgTransaction
    FROM Transactions AS T
    INNER JOIN Customers AS C
      ON T.CustomerID = C.CustomerID
    GROUP BY C.MembershipTier
) AS TierSummary
WHERE AvgTransaction > 60;

title: 3  Advanced SQL
text:
3.5 String Functions
Real data is messy. Names have inconsistent capitalisation. Text fields have trailing spaces. Values need reformatting before they are useful. String functions let you clean and reshape text directly inside SQL.
All examples use SQL Server syntax.

3.5.1 UPPER() and LOWER()
Convert text to all uppercase or all lowercase. This is important for consistent comparisons — 'Victoria' and 'VICTORIA' are treated as different values by SQL unless you normalise first.
SELECT FirstName, LastName,
       UPPER(LastName)  AS LastNameUpper,
       LOWER(FirstName) AS FirstNameLower
FROM Customers;
A common use: case-insensitive search by converting both the column and the search term to the same case.
SELECT ProductName
FROM Products
WHERE LOWER(Category) LIKE '%dairy%';


3.5.2 LEN()
Returns the number of characters in a string. SQL Server uses LEN() — note this is different from LENGTH() used in MySQL and SQLite.
SELECT ProductName, LEN(ProductName) AS NameLength
FROM Products;
Find products with unusually long names:
SELECT ProductName, LEN(ProductName) AS NameLength
FROM Products
WHERE LEN(ProductName) > 15;


3.5.3 CONCAT()
Joins two or more strings together into one.
SELECT CONCAT(FirstName, ' ', LastName) AS FullName,
       Suburb,
       State
FROM Customers;



FullName
Suburb
State




Priya Sharma
Fitzroy
VIC


Jake Nguyen
Parramatta
NSW


Bev O’Brien
Toowoomba
QLD



In SQL Server you can also use the + operator to concatenate. However, you cannot use + to join a number directly to a string — you must convert the number to text first using CAST():
SELECT FirstName + ' ' + LastName + ' has spent $' + CAST(TotalSpend AS VARCHAR) AS SpendSummary
FROM Customers;


3.5.4 SUBSTRING(), LEFT(), RIGHT()
SUBSTRING(string, start, length) extracts part of a string. Positions are 1-indexed — the first character is at position 1.
SELECT ProductName, SUBSTRING(ProductName, 1, 10) AS FirstTenChars
FROM Products;
LEFT(string, n) and RIGHT(string, n) are convenient shortcuts for extracting from the beginning or end:
SELECT FirstName,
       LEFT(FirstName, 3)  AS FirstThree,
       RIGHT(LastName, 3)  AS LastThree
FROM Customers;


3.5.5 TRIM(), LTRIM(), RTRIM()
Removes leading and/or trailing spaces. Invisible extra spaces are a common source of silent bugs — a value that looks like 'Fitzroy' in a report might actually be ' Fitzroy' in the database, causing WHERE Suburb = 'Fitzroy' to silently return no rows.
SELECT TRIM(StoreName)   AS BothSidesTrimmed   -- removes spaces on both sides
FROM Stores;

SELECT LTRIM(StoreName)  AS LeftTrimmed         -- removes leading spaces only
FROM Stores;

SELECT RTRIM(StoreName)  AS RightTrimmed        -- removes trailing spaces only
FROM Stores;


3.5.6 REPLACE()
Replaces every occurrence of a substring with another string. Useful for fixing consistent data entry errors or abbreviating labels.
-- Abbreviate store names in a summary report
SELECT StoreName,
       REPLACE(StoreName, 'FreshMart', 'FM') AS ShortName
FROM Stores;



StoreName
ShortName

FM Fitzroy


FreshMart Parramatta
FM Parramatta


FreshMart Toowoomba
FM Toowoomba


FreshMart Carlton
FM Carlton


FreshMart Adelaide CBD
FM Adelaide CBD


FreshMart Darwin
FM Darwin



The rebranding team is going to love this.

3.5.6.1 Exercises

Write a query to display each customer’s full name in uppercase and the character length of their last name.







NoteSolution





SELECT UPPER(CONCAT(FirstName, ' ', LastName)) AS FullNameUpper,
       LEN(LastName) AS LastNameLength
FROM Customers;




Write a query to create a CustomerProfile column that combines FirstName, LastName, and Suburb in the format 'FirstName LastName — Suburb' (e.g., 'Priya Sharma — Fitzroy').







NoteSolution





SELECT CONCAT(FirstName, ' ', LastName, ' — ', Suburb) AS CustomerProfile
FROM Customers;

-- Equivalent using + operator:
SELECT FirstName + ' ' + LastName + ' — ' + Suburb AS CustomerProfile
FROM Customers;




Write a query to find all products whose ProductName contains the word 'organic', regardless of how it was capitalised in the database. Use LOWER() to make the search case-insensitive.







NoteSolution





SELECT ProductName, Category
FROM Products
WHERE LOWER(ProductName) LIKE '%organic%';




Write a query to display each customer’s first name and the first 3 characters of their suburb as SuburbCode.







NoteSolution





SELECT FirstName, LEFT(Suburb, 3) AS SuburbCode
FROM Customers;

title: 3  Advanced SQL
text:
3.6 Date Functions
Almost every real-world dataset has dates — join dates, transaction records, store opening dates. Being able to extract parts of dates, calculate durations, and filter by time period is an essential analyst skill. All examples use SQL Server syntax.

3.6.1 GETDATE()
Returns the current date and time from the database server.
SELECT GETDATE() AS CurrentDateTime;
Useful as a reference point for “how long ago” calculations.


3.6.2 YEAR(), MONTH(), DAY()
Extract individual components from a date column.
SELECT TransactionID,
       YEAR(TransactionDate)  AS TxnYear,
       MONTH(TransactionDate) AS TxnMonth,
       DAY(TransactionDate)   AS TxnDay
FROM Transactions;
Filter to transactions from February 2024 only:
SELECT TransactionID, TotalAmount
FROM Transactions
WHERE YEAR(TransactionDate) = 2024
  AND MONTH(TransactionDate) = 2;
Calculate approximate years since each customer joined:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, YearJoined,
       YEAR(GETDATE()) - YearJoined AS YearsAsMember
FROM Customers;

3.6.3 DATEDIFF()
Calculates the difference between two dates. Syntax: DATEDIFF(unit, start_date, end_date).
Common units: DAY, MONTH, YEAR.
How many days ago did each transaction take place?
SELECT TransactionID, TransactionDate,
       DATEDIFF(DAY, TransactionDate, GETDATE()) AS DaysSinceTransaction
FROM Transactions;
How many months have passed since FreshMart Darwin opened?
SELECT DATEDIFF(MONTH, '2024-01-01', GETDATE()) AS MonthsSinceOpening;






TipStoring Years as Integers vs Dates



In the Customers table, YearJoined is stored as an INT (e.g., 2018) rather than a full DATE. This works fine for year-level calculations using YEAR(GETDATE()) - YearJoined, but it does not work with DATEDIFF() directly. In a production database, you would store join dates as a proper DATE column for full date arithmetic.




3.6.4 DATEADD()
Adds (or subtracts) a time interval from a date. Syntax: DATEADD(unit, number, date). Use a negative number to subtract.
What would each transaction date be if a promotional reminder was sent 14 days later?
SELECT TransactionID, TransactionDate,
       DATEADD(DAY, 14, TransactionDate) AS ReminderDate
FROM Transactions;
What was the date 3 months before each transaction (to identify pre-transaction browsing windows)?
SELECT TransactionID, TransactionDate,
       DATEADD(MONTH, -3, TransactionDate) AS ThreeMonthsBefore
FROM Transactions;


3.6.5 FORMAT()
Formats a date as a human-readable string. Useful for reports and dashboards where raw date formats look unfriendly.
SELECT TransactionID,
       FORMAT(TransactionDate, 'dd MMMM yyyy') AS FormattedDate
FROM Transactions;

FROM Transactions
WHERE YEAR(TransactionDate) = 2024
  AND MONTH(TransactionDate) <= 3;




Write a query to show each transaction’s ID, date, and the number of days between the transaction date and today.







NoteSolution





SELECT TransactionID, TransactionDate,
       DATEDIFF(DAY, TransactionDate, GETDATE()) AS DaysAgo
FROM Transactions;




Write a query to count the number of transactions per month in 2024, ordered by month.







NoteSolution





SELECT MONTH(TransactionDate) AS TxnMonth,
       COUNT(*) AS NumberOfTransactions
FROM Transactions
WHERE YEAR(TransactionDate) = 2024
GROUP BY MONTH(TransactionDate)
ORDER BY TxnMonth;




Write a query to show customer names and a column LongTermMember — 'Yes' if they joined before 2020, 'No' otherwise. Combine CASE WHEN with YearJoined.







NoteSolution





SELECT FirstName, LastName, YearJoined,
       CASE
           WHEN YearJoined < 2020 THEN 'Yes'
           ELSE 'No'
       END AS LongTermMember
FROM Customers;




Write a query to show all transactions with the date formatted as 'Month YYYY' (e.g., 'January 2024') and the total amount.







NoteSolution





SELECT TransactionID,
       FORMAT(TransactionDate, 'MMMM yyyy') AS TransactionPeriod,
       TotalAmount
FROM Transactions;

title: 3  Advanced SQL
text:
3.7 UNION
UNION stacks the results of two separate SELECT queries into one combined result set by appending the rows of the second query below the rows of the first.
Three rules must be followed:

Both queries must return the same number of columns.
The columns must have compatible data types, in the same order.
The column names in the final output come from the first query.


3.7.1 UNION vs UNION ALL









UNION
UNION ALL




Duplicates
Removed
Kept


Performance
Slower (must scan for duplicates)
Faster


Use when
You need a deduplicated list
You want all rows including repeats



Default to UNION ALL unless you specifically need duplicate removal.


3.7.2 Example: Active and At-Risk Customers
The marketing team wants a single report showing both active and churned customers, labelled clearly. Two separate pools of data, same structure — a perfect job for UNION ALL.
SELECT FirstName, LastName, MembershipTier, TotalSpend, 'Active' AS Status
FROM Customers
WHERE Churned = 0

UNION ALL

SELECT FirstName, LastName, MembershipTier, TotalSpend, 'Churned' AS Status
FROM Customers
WHERE Churned = 1;
