Both queries return five columns with compatible types. You can add ORDER BY at the very end to sort the full combined result:
SELECT FirstName, LastName, MembershipTier, TotalSpend, 'Active' AS Status
FROM Customers
WHERE Churned = 0

UNION ALL

SELECT FirstName, LastName, MembershipTier, TotalSpend, 'Churned' AS Status
FROM Customers
WHERE Churned = 1

ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC;


3.7.3 Example: Deduplication with UNION
UNION (without ALL) removes duplicates across both result sets. For example, to get a deduplicated list of all states that have either a FreshMart store or a VIC/NSW customer:
SELECT State FROM Stores
UNION
SELECT State FROM Customers WHERE State IN ('VIC', 'NSW');
Each state appears exactly once, even if it appears in both tables. In practice, SELECT DISTINCT is more natural for deduplication within a single table, but UNION is essential when deduplicating across separate queries or separate tables.






TipWhen to Use UNION



Use UNION when you have two separate pools of data with the same structure that you want to analyse together:

Combining transaction data from two financial years stored in separate tables
Merging customer records from an old system and a new system during a migration
Building a report that shows “top 3 biggest spenders” and “bottom 3 spenders” in one list




3.7.3.1 Exercises

Write a query to create a combined list of all suburb names — one column from Customers and one from Stores — with a column called Source set to 'Customer' or 'Store'. Use UNION ALL.







NoteSolution





SELECT Suburb, 'Customer' AS Source
FROM Customers

UNION ALL

SELECT Suburb, 'Store' AS Source
FROM Stores;




Write a query using UNION ALL to show the top 2 highest-spending customers and the top 2 largest stores (by SquareMetres) in a single result set. Include a Category column.







NoteSolution





SELECT TOP 2
    CONCAT(FirstName, ' ', LastName) AS Name,
    TotalSpend AS KeyMetric,
    'Customer' AS Category
FROM Customers
ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC

UNION ALL

SELECT TOP 2
    StoreName AS Name,
    CAST(SquareMetres AS DECIMAL(10,2)) AS KeyMetric,
    'Store' AS Category
FROM Stores
ORDER BY SquareMetres DESC;




Write a query to produce a combined list of all VIC customers and all VIC stores, showing the name and suburb, sorted alphabetically by suburb. Use UNION ALL.







NoteSolution





SELECT CONCAT(FirstName, ' ', LastName) AS Name, Suburb
FROM Customers
WHERE State = 'VIC'

UNION ALL

SELECT StoreName AS Name, Suburb
FROM Stores
WHERE State = 'VIC'

ORDER BY Suburb;

title: 3  Advanced SQL
text:
3.8 Putting It All Together
Real-world queries rarely use just one technique. Here is a query that combines several tools from this chapter to answer a practical analytical question.
Question: For each customer who has made at least one transaction, show their full name, state, total amount spent across all transactions, number of transactions, spend tier, and whether their total transaction spend is above or below the average.
SELECT
    CONCAT(C.FirstName, ' ', C.LastName) AS CustomerName,
    C.State,
    SUM(T.TotalAmount)                   AS TotalTransactionSpend,
    COUNT(T.TransactionID)               AS NumberOfTransactions,
    CASE
        WHEN SUM(T.TotalAmount) >= 200 THEN 'High Spender'
        WHEN SUM(T.TotalAmount) >= 80  THEN 'Mid Spender'
        ELSE 'Light Spender'
    END AS SpendTier,
    CASE
        WHEN SUM(T.TotalAmount) > (SELECT AVG(sub.CustomerTotal)
                                   FROM (SELECT CustomerID, SUM(TotalAmount) AS CustomerTotal
                                         FROM Transactions
                                         GROUP BY CustomerID) AS sub)
        THEN 'Above Average'
        ELSE 'Below Average'
    END AS SpendVsAverage
FROM Customers AS C
INNER JOIN Transactions AS T
  ON C.CustomerID = T.CustomerID
GROUP BY C.CustomerID, C.FirstName, C.LastName, C.State
ORDER BY TotalTransactionSpend DESC;
This single query uses INNER JOIN, CASE WHEN, a subquery, aggregate functions, GROUP BY, and ORDER BY. If you can read and write a query like this, you are thinking like a data analyst.

You have now covered the full essential SQL toolkit for business data analysis. JOINs connect your tables. CASE WHEN classifies and labels your data. Subqueries let you compute in layers. String and date functions clean and enrich what you retrieve. UNION combines result sets from multiple directions.
The next chapter takes things further with CTEs (Common Table Expressions) and window functions — tools that make complex multi-step queries far more readable, and that will become your best friends for any serious analytical work.

title: 4  Advanced SQL II
text:
4.1 Common Table Expressions (CTEs)
Congratulations — you have made it through SQL Basics and Advanced SQL. You can query a single table, join multiple tables, classify data with CASE WHEN, nest queries inside queries, and work with strings and dates. That is the toolkit of a working data analyst.
This bonus chapter covers two more powerful techniques that you will encounter as your SQL skills grow:
These topics are optional for the core course but are widely used in industry and will make you significantly more effective when queries start getting complex.
We continue with the same FreshMart database: Customers, Products, Stores, and Transactions.

title: 4  Advanced SQL II
text:
4.1.1 The Problem with Nested Subqueries
In the Advanced SQL chapter, we used subqueries in FROM to build temporary derived tables. They work, but they get hard to read fast. Here is a moderately complex subquery example:
SELECT SpendTier, COUNT(*) AS CustomerCount, AVG(TotalSpend) AS AvgSpend
FROM (
    SELECT CustomerID, TotalSpend,
           CASE
               WHEN TotalSpend = 0        THEN 'No Activity'
               WHEN TotalSpend < 500      THEN 'Low Spender'
               WHEN TotalSpend < 5000     THEN 'Mid Spender'
               ELSE 'High Spender'
           END AS SpendTier
    FROM Customers
) AS CustomerTiers
GROUP BY SpendTier;
This is fine. But imagine five of these nested inside each other. You would need a map, a torch, and emotional support.
A CTE lets you give the inner query a name and write the outer query against it cleanly. The query above becomes:
WITH CustomerTiers AS (
    SELECT CustomerID, TotalSpend,
           CASE
               WHEN TotalSpend = 0        THEN 'No Activity'
               WHEN TotalSpend < 500      THEN 'Low Spender'
               WHEN TotalSpend < 5000     THEN 'Mid Spender'
               ELSE 'High Spender'
           END AS SpendTier
    FROM Customers
)
SELECT SpendTier, COUNT(*) AS CustomerCount, AVG(TotalSpend) AS AvgSpend
FROM CustomerTiers
GROUP BY SpendTier;
Same result. Much easier to read — you define what CustomerTiers is first, then use it as if it were a real table.


4.1.2 CTE Syntax
WITH cte_name AS (
    -- your inner query goes here
    SELECT ...
    FROM ...
    WHERE ...
)
SELECT ...
FROM cte_name
WHERE ...;
The CTE is defined once with WITH, given a name, and then referenced by that name in the main query that follows. The CTE only exists for the duration of that one query — it is not saved anywhere permanently.


4.1.3 Example: Enriched Transaction Report
Let us build a readable transaction report that shows the customer name, store name, transaction date, and total amount — without burying the join logic inside the main SELECT.
WITH TransactionDetails AS (
    SELECT T.TransactionID,
           C.FirstName + ' ' + C.LastName AS CustomerName,
           S.StoreName,
           T.TransactionDate,
           T.TotalAmount,
           T.NumItems
    FROM Transactions AS T
    INNER JOIN Customers AS C
      ON T.CustomerID = C.CustomerID
    INNER JOIN Stores AS S
      ON T.StoreID = S.StoreID
)
SELECT CustomerName, StoreName, TransactionDate, TotalAmount
FROM TransactionDetails
WHERE TotalAmount > 100
ORDER BY TotalAmount DESC;
The CTE handles the joins. The main query focuses on filtering and ordering. Each part does one job.


4.1.4 Multiple CTEs
You can chain multiple CTEs together, separating them with commas. Each CTE can reference the ones defined before it.
WITH
-- Step 1: enrich transactions with customer and store details
TransactionDetails AS (
    SELECT T.TransactionID,
           C.CustomerID,
           C.FirstName + ' ' + C.LastName AS CustomerName,
           C.MembershipTier,
           S.State,
           T.TotalAmount,
           T.NumItems
    FROM Transactions AS T
    INNER JOIN Customers AS C ON T.CustomerID = C.CustomerID
    INNER JOIN Stores    AS S ON T.StoreID    = S.StoreID
),
-- Step 2: summarise by customer
CustomerSummary AS (
    SELECT CustomerID,
           CustomerName,
           MembershipTier,
           COUNT(*)          AS TotalTransactions,
           SUM(TotalAmount)  AS TotalSpentInPeriod,
           AVG(TotalAmount)  AS AvgBasketSize
    FROM TransactionDetails
    GROUP BY CustomerID, CustomerName, MembershipTier
)
-- Final query: find high-value customers
SELECT CustomerName, MembershipTier, TotalTransactions,
       TotalSpentInPeriod, AvgBasketSize
FROM CustomerSummary
WHERE TotalSpentInPeriod > 100
ORDER BY TotalSpentInPeriod DESC;
Read this query like a recipe: 1. First, I build TransactionDetails — all transactions with real customer and store names. 2. Then I build CustomerSummary — aggregate stats per customer using TransactionDetails. 3. Finally, I query CustomerSummary for the customers who have spent more than $100 in the period.
Each step is named, readable, and testable on its own. This is the main reason analysts love CTEs.






TipCTE vs Subquery — When to Use Which



Both CTEs and subqueries in FROM produce the same result. Choose based on readability:

Use a subquery when the inner query is short and self-contained (one or two lines).
Use a CTE when the inner query is long, or when you need to reference the same derived table more than once in a query.
Use multiple CTEs when you are building up a result in logical steps — think of it like writing a calculation step by step rather than all at once.




4.1.4.1 Exercises

Write a CTE called StoreTransactions that joins Transactions and Stores to show each transaction’s store name, state, total amount, and number of items. Then query it to find all transactions from VIC stores.







NoteSolution





WITH StoreTransactions AS (
    SELECT T.TransactionID,
           S.StoreName,
           S.State,
           T.TotalAmount,
           T.NumItems
    FROM Transactions AS T
    INNER JOIN Stores AS S
      ON T.StoreID = S.StoreID
)
SELECT StoreName, TotalAmount, NumItems
FROM StoreTransactions
WHERE State = 'VIC';




Write a CTE called CustomerStats that calculates the total number of transactions and total amount spent for each customer. Then query it to return only customers with more than one transaction.







NoteSolution





WITH CustomerStats AS (
    SELECT C.CustomerID,
           C.FirstName + ' ' + C.LastName AS CustomerName,
           COUNT(T.TransactionID)   AS TotalTransactions,
           SUM(T.TotalAmount)       AS TotalSpent
    FROM Customers AS C
    LEFT JOIN Transactions AS T
      ON C.CustomerID = T.CustomerID
    GROUP BY C.CustomerID, C.FirstName, C.LastName
)
SELECT CustomerName, TotalTransactions, TotalSpent
FROM CustomerStats
WHERE TotalTransactions > 1;




Write two chained CTEs:

FullTransactions: enriches transactions with customer name and store state
StateSummary: counts the number of transactions and total revenue for each state

Then query StateSummary to find states with more than two transactions.







NoteSolution





WITH
FullTransactions AS (
    SELECT T.TransactionID,
           C.FirstName + ' ' + C.LastName AS CustomerName,
           S.State,
           T.TotalAmount
    FROM Transactions AS T
    INNER JOIN Customers AS C ON T.CustomerID = C.CustomerID
    INNER JOIN Stores    AS S ON T.StoreID    = S.StoreID
),
StateSummary AS (
    SELECT State,
           COUNT(TransactionID)  AS NumTransactions,
           SUM(TotalAmount)      AS TotalRevenue
    FROM FullTransactions
    GROUP BY State
)
SELECT State, NumTransactions, TotalRevenue
FROM StateSummary
WHERE NumTransactions > 2
ORDER BY TotalRevenue DESC;

title: 4  Advanced SQL II
text:
4.2 Window Functions

4.2.1 What Makes Window Functions Different
Aggregate functions like SUM() and COUNT() collapse many rows into a single result. GROUP BY forces you to choose: either keep the detail rows, or see the aggregated numbers — not both at once.
Window functions break this trade-off. They perform calculations across a set of rows and return a value for each row individually, without collapsing anything. The detail rows stay intact; the calculated value is simply added alongside them.
The key ingredient is the OVER() clause, which defines the “window” — the set of rows the function should operate on for each row.
SELECT column1, column2,
       some_function() OVER (ORDER BY column3) AS new_column
FROM table_name;
The best way to understand this is through examples.



4.2.2 ROW_NUMBER()
Assigns a unique sequential number to each row based on an ordering. No ties — every row gets a distinct number.
Rank every customer from highest to lowest total spend, with a row number:
SELECT FirstName + ' ' + LastName AS CustomerName,
       TotalSpend,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC) AS SpendRank
FROM Customers;

Every customer still appears as their own row. No collapsing. The spend amount is still there alongside the rank. This is the fundamental difference from GROUP BY.
Use ROW_NUMBER() to retrieve the top N rows without using SELECT TOP. A common pattern is to wrap it in a CTE:
WITH RankedCustomers AS (
    SELECT FirstName + ' ' + LastName AS CustomerName,
           TotalSpend,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC) AS SpendRank
    FROM Customers
)
SELECT CustomerName, TotalSpend
FROM RankedCustomers
WHERE SpendRank <= 3;
This is functionally equivalent to SELECT TOP 3 ... ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC, but the CTE approach is more flexible — you can filter on rank, combine it with other conditions, or reuse the ranked table multiple times.



4.2.3 RANK() and DENSE_RANK()
These are like ROW_NUMBER() but handle ties differently.







Function
Behaviour on ties




ROW_NUMBER()
Always unique — tied rows get different numbers (arbitrary order between them)


RANK()
Tied rows share the same rank; the next rank is skipped


DENSE_RANK()
Tied rows share the same rank; the next rank is not skipped



To illustrate, suppose Bev O’Brien and Amara Diallo had the same TotalSpend of $3,150.75:
SELECT FirstName + ' ' + LastName AS CustomerName,
       TotalSpend,
       ROW_NUMBER()  OVER (ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC) AS RowNum,
       RANK()        OVER (ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC) AS Rnk,
       DENSE_RANK()  OVER (ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC) AS DenseRnk
FROM Customers;
If Bev O’Brien and Amara Diallo both had a TotalSpend of $3,150.75, the output would look like:

In practice, use DENSE_RANK() when you want clean consecutive rankings (e.g., “2nd highest, 3rd highest”) and RANK() when you want to reflect that two people share a position (e.g., “joint 3rd, so no one is 4th”).



4.2.4 PARTITION BY
PARTITION BY inside OVER() divides the rows into groups and restarts the window function calculation within each group — similar to GROUP BY but without collapsing the rows.
Rank transactions by TotalAmount within each state (joining Transactions to Customers to get the customer’s state):
SELECT T.TransactionID,
       C.FirstName + ' ' + C.LastName AS CustomerName,
       C.State,
       T.TotalAmount,
       RANK() OVER (
           PARTITION BY C.State
           ORDER BY T.TotalAmount DESC
       ) AS RankWithinState
FROM Transactions AS T
INNER JOIN Customers AS C
  ON T.CustomerID = C.CustomerID;

4.2.5 LAG() and LEAD()
LAG() retrieves the value from the previous row. LEAD() retrieves the value from the next row. Both are defined relative to the ordering you specify in OVER().
These are used to calculate changes between consecutive records — month-on-month growth, differences between successive transactions, comparing a current row to the one before it.

4.2.6 Running Totals with SUM() OVER()
Any aggregate function can be used as a window function by adding OVER(). The most common use is a running total — a cumulative sum that grows with each successive row.
Calculate the running total of transaction amounts across all transactions in chronological order:

       TransactionDate,

       SUM(TotalAmount) OVER (ORDER BY TransactionDate) AS CumulativeRevenue

Each row still shows its individual TotalAmount value. The CumulativeRevenue column simply adds up all amounts up to and including that row. This is something GROUP BY cannot produce while keeping the individual row detail.
You can combine PARTITION BY with SUM() OVER() to get a running total that resets for each group. For example, a running total of spend per customer (joining Transactions to Customers):
SELECT T.TransactionID,
       C.FirstName + ' ' + C.LastName AS CustomerName,
       T.TransactionDate,
       T.TotalAmount,
       SUM(T.TotalAmount) OVER (
           PARTITION BY T.CustomerID
           ORDER BY T.TransactionDate
       ) AS CumulativeSpendByCustomer
FROM Transactions AS T
INNER JOIN Customers AS C
  ON T.CustomerID = C.CustomerID;
The running total resets to zero when the customer changes — you get a separate running spend total for Priya Sharma, a separate one for Jake Nguyen, and so on.



4.2.7 Window Function Summary



Function
What it does




ROW_NUMBER()
Unique sequential number per row


RANK()
Rank with gaps after ties


DENSE_RANK()
Rank without gaps after ties


LAG(col, n)
Value from n rows before the current row


LEAD(col, n)
Value from n rows after the current row


SUM(col) OVER()
Running or partitioned cumulative sum


AVG(col) OVER()
Running or partitioned moving average



All window functions follow the same pattern:
function_name() OVER (
    PARTITION BY grouping_column   -- optional: restart within each group
    ORDER BY ordering_column       -- defines the row sequence
)

4.2.7.1 Exercises

Write a query to rank all customers by TotalSpend in descending order using DENSE_RANK(). Include the customer’s full name, their total spend, and their rank.







NoteSolution





SELECT FirstName + ' ' + LastName AS CustomerName,
       TotalSpend,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY TotalSpend DESC) AS SpendRank
FROM Customers;




Write a query to show each transaction in chronological order, along with the TotalAmount from the previous transaction and the difference. Name the columns clearly.







NoteSolution





SELECT TransactionID,
       TransactionDate,
       TotalAmount,
       LAG(TotalAmount) OVER (ORDER BY TransactionDate) AS PrevAmount,
       TotalAmount - LAG(TotalAmount) OVER (ORDER BY TransactionDate) AS AmountChange
FROM Transactions;




Write a query to show each transaction with a running total of TotalAmount ordered by TransactionDate. Call the running total column CumulativeRevenue.







NoteSolution





SELECT TransactionID,
       TransactionDate,
       TotalAmount,
       SUM(TotalAmount) OVER (ORDER BY TransactionDate) AS CumulativeRevenue
FROM Transactions;




Write a query to rank transactions by TotalAmount within each StoreID using RANK(). Show the transaction ID, store ID, total amount, and the within-store rank.







NoteSolution





SELECT TransactionID,
       StoreID,
       TotalAmount,
       RANK() OVER (
           PARTITION BY StoreID
           ORDER BY TotalAmount DESC
       ) AS AmountRankByStore
FROM Transactions;




Using a CTE and ROW_NUMBER(), write a query to return the single transaction with the highest TotalAmount. (Do not use SELECT TOP.)







NoteSolution





WITH RankedTransactions AS (
    SELECT TransactionID, CustomerID, TotalAmount,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY TotalAmount DESC) AS AmountRank
    FROM Transactions
)
SELECT TransactionID, CustomerID, TotalAmount
FROM RankedTransactions
WHERE AmountRank = 1;
-- Transaction 1002: Marcus Lee at FreshMart Carlton. $210.30 and 28 items.
-- Someone did a very thorough shop.




Write a query that uses LEAD() to show each transaction alongside the date of the next transaction, ordered chronologically. Name the column NextTransactionDate.







NoteSolution





SELECT TransactionID,
       TransactionDate,
       TotalAmount,
       LEAD(TransactionDate) OVER (ORDER BY TransactionDate) AS NextTransactionDate
FROM Transactions;

title: 4  Advanced SQL II
text:
4.3 Putting It All Together
Here is a query that combines CTEs, window functions, and techniques from previous chapters to produce a comprehensive customer performance report.
Question: For each customer who has made at least one transaction, show their name, membership tier, total transactions, total amount spent in the period, their spend tier classification, their rank by total spend (1 = highest), and whether their lifetime TotalSpend is above or below the average across all customers.
WITH
-- Step 1: aggregate transactions per customer
TransactionSummary AS (
    SELECT C.CustomerID,
           C.FirstName + ' ' + C.LastName AS CustomerName,
           C.MembershipTier,
           C.TotalSpend,
           COUNT(T.TransactionID)    AS TotalTransactions,
           SUM(T.TotalAmount)        AS PeriodSpend,
           AVG(T.TotalAmount)        AS AvgBasketSize
    FROM Customers AS C
    INNER JOIN Transactions AS T ON C.CustomerID = T.CustomerID
    GROUP BY C.CustomerID, C.FirstName, C.LastName,
             C.MembershipTier, C.TotalSpend
),
-- Step 2: add spend tier classification and rankings
CustomerReport AS (
    SELECT CustomerName,
           MembershipTier,
           TotalTransactions,
           PeriodSpend,
           AvgBasketSize,
           CASE
               WHEN TotalSpend = 0       THEN 'No Activity'
               WHEN TotalSpend < 500     THEN 'Low Spender'
               WHEN TotalSpend < 5000    THEN 'Mid Spender'
               ELSE 'High Spender'
           END AS SpendTier,
           RANK() OVER (ORDER BY PeriodSpend DESC) AS PeriodSpendRank,
           CASE
               WHEN TotalSpend > (SELECT AVG(TotalSpend) FROM Customers)
               THEN 'Above Average'
               ELSE 'Below Average'
           END AS LifetimeSpendBand
    FROM TransactionSummary
)
SELECT CustomerName, MembershipTier, SpendTier,
       TotalTransactions, PeriodSpend, PeriodSpendRank,
       AvgBasketSize, LifetimeSpendBand
FROM CustomerReport
ORDER BY PeriodSpendRank;
This query builds the result in two named steps. Anyone reading it can follow the logic clearly — no six-layer nesting, no wondering what the anonymous inner query on line 14 is supposed to be doing.

You now have the full toolkit. CTEs keep complex queries readable. Window functions unlock calculations — rankings, running totals, row-to-row comparisons — that are impossible to express cleanly with standard aggregation. Between SQL Basics, Advanced SQL, and this chapter, you have everything you need to work confidently with real business data.
Next up: Power BI, where we take this data out of the query editor and start turning it into dashboards and reports that stakeholders can actually look at without their eyes glazing over.

title: 5  Power BI
text:
5.1 Why Power BI?
Power BI is a powerful business intelligence tool that can handle large datasets and perform complex calculations quickly. It can connect to a wide variety of data sources, including databases, cloud services, and Excel files. The user-friendly interface allows users to create interactive reports and dashboards with ease. Additionally, Power BI enables the creation of custom visualizations and reports using DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) and M (Power Query Formula Language). With built-in collaboration features, users can easily share reports and dashboards with others, making it an ideal choice for organizations looking to enhance their data analysis capabilities.

title: 5  Power BI
text:
5.2 Power BI vs. Excel
Power BI and Excel are both powerful tools for data analysis, but they serve different purposes and have different strengths. Here are some key differences:

Data Handling: Power BI is designed to handle large datasets and can connect to a wide variety of data sources, while Excel is more suited for smaller datasets and manual data entry.
Data Visualization: Power BI offers a wide range of interactive visualizations and dashboards, while Excel has more limited charting capabilities.
Collaboration: Power BI allows for easy sharing and collaboration on reports and dashboards, while Excel requires more manual effort to share files.
Data Modeling: Power BI has advanced data modeling capabilities, including relationships between tables and DAX calculations, while Excel is more focused on spreadsheet calculations.
Real-time Data: Power BI can connect to real-time data sources, allowing for up-to-date reporting, while Excel requires manual updates.
Cost: Power BI has a subscription-based pricing model, while Excel is typically purchased as part of the Microsoft Office suite.




Power BI vs Excel

title: 5  Power BI
text:
5.3 Power BI vs. Tableau
Power BI and Tableau are both powerful data visualization tools, but they have some key differences:

Ease of Use: Power BI is generally considered more user-friendly, especially for those already familiar with Microsoft products. Tableau has a steeper learning curve but offers more advanced features for experienced users.
Data Handling: Power BI is better suited for handling large datasets and can connect to a wider variety of data sources, while Tableau is more focused on visualizations and may struggle with very large datasets.
Cost: Power BI is typically more affordable, with a subscription-based pricing model, while Tableau can be more expensive, especially for larger teams.
Collaboration: Power BI has built-in collaboration features that make it easy to share reports and dashboards, while Tableau requires more manual effort to share visualizations.
Customization: Tableau offers more customization options for visualizations, while Power BI has a more standardized set of visualizations.
Integration: Power BI integrates seamlessly with other Microsoft products, while Tableau has a wider range of third-party integrations.
Community and Support: Both tools have strong user communities and support resources, but Power BI has a larger user base due to its integration with Microsoft products.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
text:
6.1 What is Power BI?
Power BI is Microsoft’s free business intelligence tool, and it is one of the most in-demand skills on any data analyst job listing in Australia. By the end of this chapter, you will have Power BI installed, data loaded, tables properly connected, and your first three visuals on the canvas — all built from the FreshMart dataset you already know from the SQL chapters.
This chapter deliberately goes slowly. Every click is described. If you have never opened Power BI before, that is completely fine — that is exactly who this chapter is written for.
Power BI Desktop is a free application from Microsoft that lets you:
The end product is a .pbix file — a Power BI report file — that can be published online and shared with colleagues, embedded in a website, or presented to stakeholders.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
text:
Connect to data sources (CSV files, Excel, databases, cloud services)
Transform messy data into analysis-ready shapes
Model relationships between multiple tables
Visualise your data with interactive charts, maps, and tables
Build dashboards that update automatically as your data changes



6.1.1 Is it really free?
Power BI Desktop — the application you install on your computer — is completely free. You can download it, build reports, and save .pbix files without spending a cent. A paid subscription (Power BI Pro or Premium) is only required if you want to publish reports to the cloud and share them with a team. For this course, the free Desktop version is everything you need.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
text:
6.2 Step 1: Installing Power BI Desktop
Power BI Desktop runs on Windows only. If you are on a Mac, see the note below.






NoteMac Users



Power BI Desktop does not have a native macOS version. Mac users have two options:

Microsoft Remote Desktop — Access a Windows virtual machine through your university. Check with the IT department for access details.
Windows via Parallels or VMWare — If you have a virtualisation tool installed, you can run Windows inside macOS and install Power BI Desktop there.

The university computer labs all have Power BI Desktop installed. If you are stuck, head to a lab for this chapter.



6.2.1 Download and Install (Windows)
Step 1. Open your web browser and go to:

https://powerbi.microsoft.com/desktop







Note📸 What you should see



The Microsoft Power BI website, with a large blue “Download free” button in the centre of the page.


Step 2. Click Download free. You will be taken to the Microsoft Store.
Step 3. In the Microsoft Store, click Get (or Install). Windows will download and install Power BI Desktop automatically. This takes about 2–5 minutes depending on your connection.






Note📸 What you should see



The Microsoft Store page showing “Power BI Desktop” with a blue “Get” button.


Step 4. Once installed, search for Power BI Desktop in your Start menu and open it.






TipAlternative: Download the installer directly



If the Microsoft Store is not available on your machine, you can download the .exe installer directly from: > https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=58494

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
text:
6.3 Step 2: The Power BI Interface
When you first open Power BI Desktop, you will see a splash screen and then the main interface. Close the welcome dialog that appears.






Note📸 What you should see



The Power BI Desktop main window. It has a dark ribbon toolbar at the top, a blank white canvas in the centre, and three icons on the left sidebar.


The interface has five main areas. Take a moment to find each one:
① The Ribbon (top) — Like the ribbon in Microsoft Word. The tabs are: Home, Insert, Modeling, View, Optimize, Help. You will use Home most often.
② The Left Sidebar — Three icons that switch between:

📊 Report view (the default) — where you build visuals
🗃 Data view — where you inspect your raw table data
🔀 Model view — where you define relationships between tables

③ The Canvas (centre) — The blank page where you place your charts and visuals. Think of it as a slide in PowerPoint.
④ Visualizations Pane (right side) — A gallery of chart types to choose from.
⑤ Data/Fields Pane (right side, below Visualizations) — Once data is loaded, your table columns appear here. You drag columns from here onto the canvas to create visuals.






Note📸 What you should see



The five areas labelled above visible on screen. The canvas is blank. The Visualizations pane shows small icons for chart types (bar chart, line chart, map, etc.). The Data pane shows “No data loaded yet.”
