title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
section: 6.5 Step 4: Load the Data into Power BI
text:
6.5 Step 4: Load the Data into Power BI
Step 1. In Power BI, click Home in the ribbon. Find the Get data button (it has a small cylinder icon).






Note📸 What you should see



The Home tab is selected. “Get data” appears in the top-left area of the ribbon, with a small dropdown arrow beside it.


Step 2. Click the dropdown arrow next to Get data and choose Text/CSV.






Note📸 What you should see



A dropdown menu with options including “Excel workbook”, “Text/CSV”, “Web”, “More…”. “Text/CSV” is highlighted.


Step 3. A file browser opens. Navigate to your FreshMart folder on the Desktop. Select customers.csv and click Open.
Step 4. Power BI reads the file and shows you a preview. Check that the columns look correct — you should see CustomerID, FirstName, LastName, and the rest. Then click Load.






Note📸 What you should see



A preview dialog showing the first few rows of the customers data. Column headers are correctly detected. A “Load” button and a “Transform Data” button appear at the bottom.








TipLoad vs Transform Data



Load imports the data as-is. Transform Data opens Power Query Editor, where you can clean and reshape the data before importing. For now, click Load — we will visit Power Query in the next step.


Step 5. Repeat for the remaining three files. Each time: Get data → Text/CSV → select file → Load.

Load products.csv
Load stores.csv
Load transactions.csv

After loading all four files, check the Data pane on the right side of the screen. You should now see four tables listed: customers, products, stores, and transactions.






Note📸 What you should see



The Data pane on the right shows four table names. Each table has an expand arrow (▶) next to it. Clicking the arrow reveals the column names inside.








TipLoaded all four?



Click the Data view icon on the left sidebar (the table icon). Select each table and scroll through the rows to confirm the data loaded correctly. The transactions table should have 60 rows.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
section: 6.6 Step 5: Clean the Data with Power Query
text:
6.6 Step 5: Clean the Data with Power Query
Power Query is Power BI’s built-in data transformation tool. It lets you rename columns, change data types, remove blank rows, split text, and much more — all without touching the original CSV files.
We need to fix one thing in the transactions table: the TransactionDate column was loaded as text. We need to convert it to a proper date type.
Step 1. In the Home ribbon, click Transform data. Power Query Editor opens in a new window.






Note📸 What you should see



A new window opens — Power Query Editor. On the left is a list of your four queries (one per table). In the centre is a data grid. On the right is the “Query Settings” panel showing “Applied Steps.”


Step 2. In the left pane, click transactions to select it.
Step 3. Find the TransactionDate column in the grid. Look at the icon to the left of the column header — it probably shows ABC (text) instead of a calendar icon. Right-click the column header → Change Type → Date.






Note📸 What you should see



After changing the type, the icon next to “TransactionDate” becomes a small calendar icon. The dates in the column change from text like “2024-01-05” to a date format like “05/01/2024”.


Step 4. Power Query may ask whether to replace the existing step. Click Replace current.
Step 5. Now do the same for the customers table. Select customers in the left pane. Look for any columns that have wrong types. The TotalSpend column should be Decimal Number (a currency icon). If it shows as text, right-click → Change Type → Decimal Number.
Step 6. When you are satisfied, click Close & Apply (top-left of the Power Query window).






Note📸 What you should see



Power Query closes and you return to Power BI Desktop. A loading bar briefly appears while your changes are applied. Your data is now clean and typed correctly.








TipApplied Steps — your undo history



Notice the “Applied Steps” panel on the right in Power Query. Every transformation you make is recorded as a step. You can click any step to undo back to that point — it is a non-destructive workflow. Your original CSV files are never modified.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
text:
6.7 Step 6: Create Relationships Between Tables
Right now Power BI has four separate tables. It does not yet know that CustomerID in transactions links to CustomerID in customers. We need to define these relationships explicitly — this is the same concept as foreign keys in SQL.
Step 1. Click the Model view icon on the left sidebar (the diamond/relationship icon).






Note📸 What you should see



The four tables appear as boxes in the Model view. They may be scattered around — drag them to arrange them neatly. No lines connect them yet.


Power BI may have auto-detected some relationships already. If you see lines between the tables, great — but verify them. If not, you will create them now.
Step 2. Arrange the tables in a star pattern: transactions in the centre, with customers, products, and stores around it. This makes the diagram easy to read.
Step 3. To create the first relationship: click and drag from CustomerID in the transactions table and drop it onto CustomerID in the customers table. A line appears connecting the two tables.






Note📸 What you should see



A line appears between the transactions box and the customers box. The line has a “1” on the customers end and a “*” (asterisk) on the transactions end — this means one customer can have many transactions (a one-to-many relationship).


Step 4. Repeat for ProductID: drag from transactions.ProductID → products.ProductID.
Step 5. Repeat for StoreID: drag from transactions.StoreID → stores.StoreID.
After creating all three relationships, your model should look like this:






Note📸 What you should see



The Model view shows four table boxes. The transactions box in the centre has three lines connecting it to customers (via CustomerID), products (via ProductID), and stores (via StoreID). Each line shows “1” on the dimension table side and “*” on the transactions side.








TipWhy relationships matter



Once the relationships are set, Power BI can automatically combine information from all four tables when you build visuals. When you drag CustomerName from customers and TotalAmount from transactions onto the canvas, Power BI knows to join them via CustomerID — just like an INNER JOIN in SQL, happening invisibly in the background.








WarningCheck the direction of your lines



Double-click any relationship line to inspect it. The “Cardinality” should be **Many to one (*:1) — many transaction rows per one customer/product/store. The “Cross filter direction” should be Single**. If something looks wrong, delete the line and redraw it.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
section: 6.8 Step 7: Your First Visual — A Table
text:
6.8 Step 7: Your First Visual — A Table
Now the fun begins. Switch back to Report view (click the bar chart icon on the left sidebar). The canvas is blank and ready.

6.8.1 Creating a Table Visual
A Table visual in Power BI is like a formatted spreadsheet. It is the quickest way to verify your data is connected correctly.
Step 1. In the Visualizations pane on the right, click the Table icon (it looks like a grid of rows and columns).






Note📸 What you should see



An empty table visual appears in the centre of the canvas — it shows “Add data fields here.”


Step 2. In the Data pane (bottom-right), expand the customers table by clicking the ▶ arrow.
Step 3. Drag FirstName from the Data pane and drop it into the Columns section of the Visualizations pane (the area that says “Add data fields here”). A column of customer names appears in the table.
Step 4. Now expand the transactions table in the Data pane. Drag TotalAmount into the Columns section. Power BI automatically sums the TotalAmount for each customer.






Note📸 What you should see



The table visual now shows two columns: FirstName and a Sum of TotalAmount. Each customer’s name appears with their total spending summed across all transactions. Marcus Lee should show the highest value.


Step 5. Add LastName from the customers table to the Columns section as well. The table now shows full names.
Step 6. To sort the table by total spending, click the TotalAmount column header in the visual. An arrow appears — click it again to sort descending (highest first).






Note📸 What you should see



The table is now sorted by spending: Marcus Lee at the top, followed by Priya Sharma, and Nina Patel at the bottom with $0.00.








TipResize and reposition



Click and drag the corners of the table visual to resize it. Click and drag the centre to move it. Think of each visual as an object on a PowerPoint slide.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
section: 6.9 Step 8: Your Second Visual — KPI Cards
text:
6.9 Step 8: Your Second Visual — KPI Cards
A Card visual displays a single number prominently — perfect for headline metrics like “Total Revenue”, “Number of Transactions”, or “Average Order Value”. These are the big bold numbers you see at the top of every executive dashboard.
Step 1. Click on a blank area of the canvas to deselect the table. Then click the Card icon in the Visualizations pane (it looks like a single rectangle with a number inside).






Note📸 What you should see



A blank Card visual appears on the canvas, showing “Add data fields here.”


Step 2. From the transactions table in the Data pane, drag TotalAmount into the Fields section of the Card visual. The card now shows the sum of all transactions — the total revenue for FreshMart in 2024.






Note📸 What you should see



The Card visual displays a large number — the sum of TotalAmount across all 60 transactions. It should be around $974. This is FreshMart’s total revenue in the dataset.


Step 3. Let us add a second Card showing the total number of transactions. Click a blank area to deselect. Add another Card visual.
Step 4. Drag TransactionID from the transactions table. But instead of summing, we want to count the rows. In the Visualizations pane, click the dropdown arrow next to TransactionID in the Fields well → change Sum to Count.






Note📸 What you should see



The second Card now shows “60” — the number of transactions in the dataset.


Step 5. Add a third Card: drag CustomerID from customers into a new Card visual, and change the aggregation to Count (Distinct). This shows the number of unique customers.
Arrange the three cards in a row at the top of the canvas. You now have a simple KPI row that immediately tells the reader three things: total revenue, transaction count, and customer count.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
section: 6.10 Step 9: Your Third Visual — A Column Chart
text:
6.10 Step 9: Your Third Visual — A Column Chart
A Column Chart is one of the most used visuals in business reporting. Let us build one showing total revenue by product category.
Step 1. Click a blank area of the canvas. Click the Clustered column chart icon in the Visualizations pane (it is the bar chart icon — vertical bars side by side).






Note📸 What you should see



A blank column chart visual appears on the canvas with placeholder axis labels.


Step 2. In the Data pane, expand the products table. Drag Category into the X-axis field well in the Visualizations pane.
Step 3. Expand the transactions table. Drag TotalAmount into the Y-axis field well.






Note📸 What you should see



A column chart appears with product categories on the horizontal axis (Bakery, Beverages, Dairy, etc.) and the sum of revenue on the vertical axis. Each bar represents one category’s total sales.


Step 4. To sort the bars from highest to lowest revenue: click the three dots (…) in the top-right corner of the visual → Sort axis → Sum of TotalAmount → Sort descending.






Note📸 What you should see



The bars re-order so the highest-revenue category appears on the left. Dairy or Meat & Seafood should be near the top — Marcus Lee’s chicken purchases are doing a lot of heavy lifting.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
text:
6.11 Step 10: Basic Formatting
A chart with the default blue colour and no title is functional but not professional. Let us apply some basic formatting.
Step 1. Click on the column chart to select it. In the Visualizations pane, click the Format visual icon (the paint roller icon, second icon from the left in the Visualizations pane header).






Note📸 What you should see



The Visualizations pane switches to formatting options. You see sections like “Visual” and “General”, each with expandable sub-sections: Title, Colors, Data labels, etc.


Step 2. Expand Title. Change the title text to: Revenue by Product Category. Change the font size to 14. Turn on Bold.
Step 3. Expand Columns → change the colour to a dark blue or your preferred brand colour using the colour picker.
Step 4. Expand Data labels and toggle them On. Each bar now shows its exact value — useful for stakeholders who do not want to read the axis.






Note📸 What you should see



The chart now has a bold title, coloured bars, and small data labels above each bar showing the exact revenue value.


Step 5. To format the Y-axis values as currency: click the Y-axis section → expand Display units → change to None (so numbers show in full, not abbreviated as “1K”). To add a dollar sign, you would typically set this up in the Data Model as a currency column — we will cover that in the next chapter.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
text:
6.12 Step 11: Saving Your Work
Step 1. Press Ctrl + S (or File → Save).
Step 2. Choose a location — save it as FreshMart_Dashboard.pbix in your FreshMart folder on the Desktop.






TipSave often



Power BI does not auto-save. Press Ctrl+S regularly. A .pbix file saves everything: the data, the model, the relationships, and all your visuals.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
text:
6.13 What We Have Built
At the end of this chapter, your canvas should have:

3 KPI Cards across the top (Total Revenue, Transaction Count, Customer Count)
1 Table showing each customer’s name and total spend
1 Column Chart showing revenue by product category

This is a simple but functional analytical view. A stakeholder looking at this page immediately knows: how much money FreshMart made, how many transactions occurred, how many customers are in the program, and which product categories generate the most revenue.
In the next chapter, we will add slicers, maps, more chart types, DAX calculations, and wire everything together into a polished, interactive multi-page dashboard.

title: 6  Power BI: Getting Started
text:
6.14 Exercises
Exercise 1. Add a fourth KPI Card showing the average transaction value (TotalAmount, aggregation: Average). Format it to show two decimal places.






NoteSolution






Add a new Card visual to the canvas.
Drag TotalAmount from the transactions table into the Fields well.
In the Visualizations pane, click the dropdown next to TotalAmount and change the aggregation from Sum to Average.
To control decimal places: go to Format Visual → Callout value → Value decimal places → set to 2.

The card should display approximately $16.23 — the average amount spent per transaction.



Exercise 2. Create a new column chart showing total revenue by store (use StoreName from stores on the X-axis and TotalAmount from transactions on the Y-axis). Sort it descending. Which store has the highest revenue?






NoteSolution






Add a Clustered column chart.
Drag StoreName from stores to the X-axis field well.
Drag TotalAmount from transactions to the Y-axis field well.
Click the three dots → Sort axis → Sum of TotalAmount → Sort descending.

FreshMart Parramatta (StoreID 2) should come out on top — it serves the largest customer base in the dataset, including Amara Diallo and Jake Nguyen.
Note: FreshMart Darwin appears in the stores table but has no transactions (it opened in 2024 with no sales recorded). It may not appear in the chart at all — this is the same LEFT JOIN / RIGHT JOIN gap from the SQL chapters, now visible in Power BI.



Exercise 3. In the Table visual you built in Step 7, add the MembershipTier column from the customers table. Then sort by TotalAmount descending. What is the membership tier of the top two spenders?






NoteSolution






Click on the existing Table visual to select it.
From the customers table in the Data pane, drag MembershipTier into the Columns field well in the Visualizations pane.
Click the TotalAmount column header to sort descending.

Both Marcus Lee and Priya Sharma are Gold tier members — the highest tier. This makes sense: they are the two highest spenders. Gold tier members appear to be the most engaged with the FreshMart loyalty program.



Exercise 4. Go to Data view and inspect the transactions table. Verify that TransactionDate is stored as a Date type (not text). What is the earliest transaction date in the dataset?






NoteSolution






Click the Data view icon (table icon) on the left sidebar.
Select the transactions table from the panel.
Click the TransactionDate column header. Check that the column type icon shows a calendar (📅) not text (ABC).
Scroll to the top or sort ascending to find the earliest date.

The earliest transaction is 2024-01-05 — Priya Sharma’s first purchase of the year: 3 litres of Full Cream Milk.



Exercise 5. In Model view, double-click the relationship line between transactions and customers. What is the cardinality? What does it mean in plain English?






NoteSolution





The cardinality is **Many to one (*:1)**.
In plain English: one customer can appear in many transaction rows (because a customer makes multiple purchases), but each transaction row belongs to exactly one customer. This is the standard parent-child relationship in a relational database — the same concept as a foreign key in SQL.










NoteChapter Summary



In this chapter you:

Installed Power BI Desktop (free, Windows)
Navigated the five main areas of the Power BI interface
Loaded four CSV files (Customers, Products, Stores, Transactions)
Cleaned data types in Power Query Editor
Created a star-schema data model with three relationships
Built your first three visuals: Table, Card, and Column Chart
Applied basic formatting (titles, colours, data labels)

These are the foundations. Everything in the next chapter builds on what you have set up here.

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.1 Planning Your Dashboard
In the previous chapter, you set up Power BI, loaded the FreshMart data, defined relationships, and built three basic visuals. You now have a functioning data model and a canvas with something on it. Good. But a collection of charts is not yet a dashboard.
A dashboard tells a story. It has a clear audience, a defined purpose, and visuals that work together rather than sitting side by side ignoring each other. In this chapter, you will learn the features that transform a page of charts into a polished, interactive analytical tool: slicers, DAX measures, drill-down, maps, action buttons, and interactivity between visuals.
By the end of this chapter, you will have a complete two-page FreshMart Sales Dashboard.
Before touching Power BI, spend two minutes thinking about what your dashboard needs to answer. A dashboard without a question is just decoration.
Our FreshMart Sales Dashboard will answer three questions for a FreshMart regional manager:
These three questions map neatly to two pages:
Let us build it.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
How is overall sales performance tracking? (total revenue, transaction count, average order value)
Which stores and products are performing best? (revenue by store, revenue by category)
Who are our most valuable customers, and how are they distributed? (customer map, loyalty tier breakdown, top spenders)



Page 1 — Sales Overview: KPI cards + revenue by store + revenue by category + a slicer to filter by month
Page 2 — Customer Insights: Revenue by customer + membership tier chart + map visual + top products table
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.2 Setting Up Pages
At the bottom of the canvas, you will see a tab labelled Page 1. You can rename it and add more pages.
Step 1. Right-click the Page 1 tab at the bottom → Rename page → type Sales Overview.
Step 2. Click the + button next to the tab to add a new page. Right-click the new tab → Rename page → type Customer Insights.






Note📸 What you should see



Two tabs at the bottom of the canvas: “Sales Overview” and “Customer Insights”. The Sales Overview tab is active.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.3 Building Page 1: Sales Overview
Make sure you are on the Sales Overview page.

7.3.1 Step 1: Move Your Existing Visuals
If you still have the three KPI Cards, the Table, and the Column Chart from Chapter 1, move them to sensible positions. If you are starting fresh, you will build them now.
The recommended layout for Page 1:
[Card: Total Revenue] [Card: Transactions] [Card: Avg Order Value]   [Slicer: Month]
[Column Chart: Revenue by Store                                     ]
[Column Chart: Revenue by Category                                  ]
A wide chart takes up the bottom two-thirds, KPI cards sit across the top, and a slicer filters everything.



7.3.2 Step 2: Create a DAX Measure for Total Revenue
So far, when you drag TotalAmount onto the canvas, Power BI automatically sums it. This works, but a named measure gives you more control and is reusable across visuals.
What is DAX? DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) is Power BI’s formula language — similar to Excel formulas, but designed to work on entire columns and tables rather than individual cells. A measure is a DAX calculation you define once and then reuse anywhere.
Step 1. In the Data pane, right-click the transactions table → New measure.






Note📸 What you should see



A formula bar appears at the top of the screen (similar to the Excel formula bar), with a blinking cursor ready for your formula. The new measure is temporarily named “Measure” in the Data pane.


Step 2. Type the following formula. The existing text (“Measure =”) will be replaced:
Total Revenue = SUM(transactions[TotalAmount])
Step 3. Press Enter or click the tick (✓) button in the formula bar.






Note📸 What you should see



The measure “Total Revenue” now appears in the transactions table in the Data pane, with a small calculator icon (🧮) next to it — distinguishing it from a regular column.


Step 4. Create two more measures the same way (right-click transactions → New measure):
Transaction Count = COUNTROWS(transactions)
Avg Order Value = DIVIDE([Total Revenue], [Transaction Count], 0)






TipWhy DIVIDE instead of /



DIVIDE([Total Revenue], [Transaction Count], 0) safely handles division by zero — if Transaction Count is zero, it returns 0 instead of crashing. Plain division with / would return an error. Use DIVIDE for any division in DAX.


Step 5. Update your three KPI Cards to use these named measures instead of the raw columns:

Click each Card visual and replace the field in the Fields well with the corresponding measure from the Data pane (Total Revenue, Transaction Count, Avg Order Value).




7.3.3 Step 3: Add a Slicer for Month Filtering
A Slicer is a visual filter. When a user clicks a value in a slicer, every other visual on the page filters to show only data matching that selection. It is the most important interactivity tool in Power BI.
We want a slicer that lets the manager filter the entire page by month.
Step 1. First, create a new measure to extract the month name from the transaction date:
Right-click transactions → New measure:
Month = FORMAT(transactions[TransactionDate], "MMM YYYY")






TipActually, use a calculated column here



A Slicer works better with a column than a measure because slicers need to show a list of distinct values. Right-click transactions → New column instead:
MonthLabel = FORMAT(transactions[TransactionDate], "MMM YYYY")
Calculated columns are computed row-by-row and stored in the table — perfect for categories like month names.


Step 2. In the Visualizations pane, click the Slicer icon (it looks like a funnel).






Note📸 What you should see



A blank slicer visual appears on the canvas.


Step 3. From the transactions table in the Data pane, drag MonthLabel (the calculated column you just created) into the Field well of the slicer.






Note📸 What you should see



The slicer now shows a list of month-year labels: “Jan 2024”, “Feb 2024”, “Mar 2024”, etc.


Step 4. By default, the slicer shows as a vertical list. To change it to a dropdown (saves space): In the Format visual pane → Slicer settings → Options → Style → change from List to Dropdown.
Step 5. Test the slicer: click Jan 2024 in the slicer. Notice how every other visual on the page — the KPI cards, the column charts — all update to show only January data. Click it again to deselect, or click the eraser icon on the slicer to clear all filters.






Note📸 What you should see



After clicking “Jan 2024” in the slicer, the Total Revenue card shows January’s total only (~$103), the Transaction Count drops to 10, and the column charts show only January transactions.








TipMulti-select in slicers



Hold Ctrl and click multiple months to select them simultaneously. For example, Ctrl+click “Jan 2024” and “Feb 2024” to see Q1 data (first two months combined).





7.3.4 Step 4: Revenue by Store (Column Chart)
Step 1. Add a Clustered column chart.
Step 2. X-axis: StoreName from stores. Y-axis: Total Revenue (the measure you created).
Step 3. Add a data label so values appear above each bar: Format visual → Data labels → toggle On.
Step 4. Title: Revenue by Store. Set font size 14, bold.






Note📸 What you should see



A column chart showing 5 stores (FreshMart Darwin has no sales). FreshMart Parramatta is likely the tallest bar. FreshMart Darwin does not appear — it has zero transactions, so it is effectively invisible in an INNER JOIN style aggregation.





7.3.5 Step 5: Revenue by Product Category (Column Chart)
Step 1. Add another Clustered column chart below the store chart.
Step 2. X-axis: Category from products. Y-axis: Total Revenue.
Step 3. Sort descending (three dots → Sort axis → Sum of Total Revenue → Descending).
Step 4. Title: Revenue by Product Category.






Note📸 What you should see



A column chart with product categories on the X-axis. Dairy and Meat & Seafood should be among the highest — Marcus Lee’s bulk chicken purchases and Priya Sharma’s milk runs add up quickly.




7.3.6 Finishing Page 1
Your Sales Overview page should now have:

3 KPI Cards (Total Revenue, Transaction Count, Avg Order Value) across the top
A Slicer (Month dropdown) in the top-right corner
A Column Chart: Revenue by Store
A Column Chart: Revenue by Product Category

Add a Text Box to label the page: Home tab → Insert → Text box → type FreshMart Sales Overview — 2024. Format it as a page title (large, bold text at the very top of the canvas).






Note📸 What you should see



The completed Sales Overview page: a title text box at top, three KPI cards and a month slicer in a row, and two column charts below.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.4 Building Page 2: Customer Insights
Click the Customer Insights tab at the bottom to switch pages.


7.4.1 Step 6: Revenue by Customer (Bar Chart)
A horizontal bar chart is better than a column chart when you have long category labels — like full customer names.
Step 1. Add a Clustered bar chart (horizontal bars, not vertical columns — look for the icon with horizontal bars).
Step 2. Y-axis: drag FirstName from customers. Then also drag LastName from customers — Power BI will combine them.






TipCombining First and Last Name



Instead of dragging two separate columns, create a calculated column in the customers table:
Right-click customers → New column:
FullName = customers[FirstName] & " " & customers[LastName]
Now drag FullName to the Y-axis. Much cleaner.


Step 3. X-axis: Total Revenue (the measure).
Step 4. Sort descending.
Step 5. Title: Revenue by Customer.






Note📸 What you should see



A horizontal bar chart. Marcus Lee has the longest bar (highest spend), followed by Priya Sharma. Nina Patel appears at the bottom with $0 — she is present in the customers table but has zero transactions. She shows up in this bar chart because Power BI is performing an outer join between customers and the aggregated transactions data.





7.4.2 Step 7: Membership Tier Breakdown (Doughnut Chart)
A Doughnut Chart is ideal for showing proportions. We want to see the revenue split across membership tiers (Bronze, Silver, Gold).
Step 1. In the Visualizations pane, click the Doughnut chart icon (a circle with a hole in the middle).
Step 2. Drag MembershipTier from customers into the Legend field well.
Step 3. Drag Total Revenue into the Values field well.






Note📸 What you should see



A doughnut chart divided into three coloured segments: Gold, Silver, and Bronze. Gold should make up the largest segment — Marcus Lee and Priya Sharma are both Gold members and together account for the majority of revenue.


Step 4. Format: turn on Detail labels → set label style to Category, Percent of total. This shows each segment’s tier name and its percentage of total revenue.
Step 5. Title: Revenue by Membership Tier.



7.4.3 Step 8: Customer Map
Power BI has a Map visual that can plot data by city, state, country, or coordinates. We will plot revenue by state to show where FreshMart’s customers are concentrated.
Step 1. In the Visualizations pane, click the Map icon (it looks like a location pin on a globe).






Note📸 What you should see



A map visual appears with a greyed-out world map background. Bubble placeholders appear until you add location data.


Step 2. Drag State from the customers table into the Location field well.
Step 3. Drag Total Revenue into the Bubble size field well. Larger bubbles = more revenue.






Note📸 What you should see



The map zooms to Australia and shows bubbles over VIC, NSW, and QLD. Victoria (VIC) has the largest bubble because Marcus Lee and Priya Sharma are both from Victoria. There are no bubbles over SA or NT — Gary McSnarl (SA) has churned, and FreshMart Darwin has no customer transactions.








TipIf the map shows bubbles in the wrong country



Power BI’s geocoding can occasionally misidentify abbreviations. If “VIC” maps to somewhere in the USA, try: Format visual → Map settings → add the country context manually, or spell out “Victoria” by creating a mapping table. For this exercise, it should resolve to Australia automatically since Power BI recognises AU state abbreviations.


Step 4. Title: Revenue by State.



7.4.4 Step 9: Top Products Table
A detail table that shows which products are selling best — useful for the store manager’s product ordering decisions.
Step 1. Add a Table visual.
Step 2. Add the following columns: - ProductName from products - Category from products - Total Revenue (the measure) - Transaction Count (the measure)
Step 3. Add one more calculated measure for average quantity sold:
Right-click transactions → New measure:
Avg Quantity = AVERAGE(transactions[Quantity])
Add Avg Quantity as a fifth column in the table.
Step 4. Sort by Total Revenue descending.
Step 5. Title: Top Products.






Note📸 What you should see



A five-column table listing products by revenue. Chicken Breast 500g should rank near the top — it has a high unit price ($8.50) and appears frequently in Marcus Lee’s shopping. Full Cream Milk 2L will rank high in transaction count even if not the top revenue generator, because it is bought in small amounts very frequently.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.5 Step 10: Drill-Down on Column Charts
Drill-down lets users click into a chart to see more detail. On the Revenue by Category chart, a drill-down could reveal individual product names within each category.
Step 1. Go back to Sales Overview page (or add drill-down to the Revenue by Category chart on Customer Insights — either works).
Step 2. Click on the Revenue by Category column chart to select it.
Step 3. In the X-axis field well, you currently have Category. Now also drag ProductName from products into the X-axis field well below Category. (The order matters: Category is the top level, ProductName is the detail level.)






Note📸 What you should see



The X-axis field well now shows two items stacked: Category (top) and ProductName (below). The chart looks the same as before — drill-down mode is active but you are currently viewing the top level.


Step 4. Hover over the chart. In the top-right corner of the visual, small arrow icons appear — these are the drill-down controls:

↓ (single down arrow) — drill down on a specific bar when you click it
⇊ (double down arrow) — show the next level for all bars at once
↑ (up arrow) — drill back up
⊞ (expand all) — expand one level down while keeping the hierarchy

Step 5. Click the single down arrow (↓) to enable drill mode. Then click on the Dairy bar.






Note📸 What you should see



The chart zooms into the Dairy category and now shows individual products within Dairy: “Full Cream Milk 2L” and “Free Range Eggs 12pk” as separate bars. A breadcrumb at the top of the visual shows “Category > Dairy”.


Step 6. Click the up arrow (↑) to drill back up to the category view.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.6 Step 11: Small Multiples
Small Multiples splits a chart into a grid of identical charts — one per category. This is a very efficient way to compare the same metric across groups without requiring the user to filter manually.
Let us create a small multiples column chart showing monthly revenue, with one panel per store.
Step 1. Add a new Clustered column chart.
Step 2. X-axis: MonthLabel (the calculated column from the transactions table).
Step 3. Y-axis: Total Revenue.
Step 4. In the Visualizations pane, scroll down to find the Small multiples field well. Drag StoreName from stores into the Small multiples field well.






Note📸 What you should see



The single chart splits into a grid of 5 smaller charts (one per store that has data). Each mini-chart shows the same X-axis (month) and Y-axis (revenue), but filtered to one store. FreshMart Darwin is absent because it has no sales.


Step 5. Format the visual → Small multiples → adjust the number of rows and columns to arrange the panels neatly (e.g., 2 rows × 3 columns).
Step 6. Title: Monthly Revenue by Store.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
section: 7.7 Step 12: Action Buttons for Page Navigation
text:
7.7 Step 12: Action Buttons for Page Navigation
Action Buttons allow users to navigate between report pages with a click — like buttons on a website. This gives your dashboard a polished, app-like feel.
Step 1. Go to the Sales Overview page.
Step 2. In the ribbon, click Insert → Buttons → Navigator → Page navigator.






Note📸 What you should see



Power BI automatically creates a set of navigation buttons — one button per page — and places them on the canvas. They read “Sales Overview” and “Customer Insights.”








TipAlternatively: custom buttons



You can also make custom buttons. Insert → Buttons → Blank → then in Format visual, set the Action type to Page navigation and specify which page it goes to. This lets you design your own labelled navigation buttons.


Step 3. Move the navigation buttons to the top-right corner of the canvas.
Step 4. Test: click Customer Insights in the navigator. The report switches to Page 2.
Step 5. Go to the Customer Insights page and add the same page navigator there (Insert → Buttons → Navigator → Page navigator). This way the user can navigate from either page.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.8 Step 13: Interactivity Between Visuals
By default, clicking any visual on a page filters all other visuals on the same page. This is called cross-filtering. You can control this behaviour — useful when you want certain visuals to remain unaffected by user clicks.
Step 1. On the Sales Overview page, click the Revenue by Store column chart to select it.
Step 2. In the ribbon, click Format → Edit interactions.






Note📸 What you should see



Small icons appear in the top-right corner of every other visual on the page. Each icon has a filter icon (to enable filtering), a highlight icon, and an X (to disable interaction entirely).


Step 3. For each KPI Card, click the filter icon (funnel) above the card to confirm that clicking on a store bar will filter the KPI cards. This should already be on by default.
Step 4. To prevent a visual from being filtered — for example, if you want the KPI cards to always show the totals regardless of chart clicks — click the X (none) icon above each card instead.
Step 5. Click Edit interactions again in the ribbon to turn off edit mode.
Step 6. Test: click the FreshMart Parramatta bar in the Revenue by Store chart. All other visuals filter to show only Parramatta data — the Revenue by Category chart now shows only what Parramatta customers bought, and the KPI cards update to Parramatta-only totals.






Note📸 What you should see



After clicking the Parramatta bar, the Revenue by Category chart shows a different distribution — reflecting that Parramatta customers (Jake Nguyen and Amara Diallo) have different buying habits from, say, Carlton customers. The Parramatta KPI card shows lower revenue than Carlton’s Marcus Lee alone.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.9 Step 14: Final Polish
Before presenting your dashboard, apply consistent formatting across both pages.

7.9.1 Colour theme
Step 1. In the View tab of the ribbon, click Themes → browse the built-in themes. Choose a clean professional theme — Executive or Accessible default work well for business reports.






Note📸 What you should see



The ribbon shows the Themes dropdown. Hovering over a theme previews how it will change the colours of your visuals.




7.9.2 Page background
Step 2. Right-click the canvas → Format page → Wallpaper → set a light grey colour (e.g., #F5F5F5). This creates a subtle background that makes your white visuals pop.


7.9.3 Consistent titles
Step 3. Make sure every visual has a clear title. Use the same font, size, and colour for all titles. This is what separates an amateur dashboard from a professional one.


7.9.4 Alignment
Step 4. Select multiple visuals (hold Shift and click each one). In the Format tab → Align → align edges and distribute spacing evenly. A well-aligned dashboard looks intentional; a messy one looks rushed.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.10 The Completed FreshMart Dashboard
Page 1 — Sales Overview:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        FreshMart Sales Overview — 2024               │
│  [Total Revenue] [Transactions] [Avg Order Value]  [Month Slicer] │
│  [======== Revenue by Store ==================]     │
│  [======== Revenue by Category ===============]     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Page 2 — Customer Insights:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        FreshMart Customer Insights — 2024            │
│  [====== Revenue by Customer =====] [Doughnut: Tier] │
│  [========== Map: by State =========] [Top Products] │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
A regional manager can open this dashboard and immediately answer:

How much did we make in March? → Click March in the Month slicer
Which store is underperforming? → Revenue by Store chart
What do our Gold-tier customers buy? → Click “Gold” in the Doughnut chart, check Revenue by Category
Where are we losing customers geographically? → Map visual, compare with Churned column in the Customers table
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard
text:
7.11 Exercises
Exercise 1. Create a new DAX measure called Churned Customers that counts the number of customers where Churned = 1. Add it as a KPI Card on the Customer Insights page.






NoteSolution





Right-click the customers table → New measure:
Churned Customers = CALCULATE(COUNTROWS(customers), customers[Churned] = 1)
CALCULATE evaluates an expression (COUNTROWS) within a filter context (Churned = 1). It is one of the most powerful functions in DAX — the equivalent of WHERE in SQL.
Add a Card visual to the Customer Insights page and drag Churned Customers into the Fields well. The result should be 2 — Gary McSnarl and Bev O’Brien have both churned.



Exercise 2. The Revenue by Customer bar chart currently shows Nina Patel with $0 revenue. Create a filter to exclude customers with zero transactions from this visual (without removing Nina from the model entirely).






NoteSolution





Click the Revenue by Customer bar chart. In the Filters pane (right side, above Visualizations), expand Filters on this visual. Drag Total Revenue into this filter area. Change the filter type to is greater than and enter 0. Apply filter.
Nina Patel now disappears from this chart — she has no revenue, so she is excluded. She still exists in the customers table (and in the map, if you are filtering by state rather than revenue). This is a visual-level filter and does not affect any other visuals on the page.



Exercise 3. Add a second slicer to Page 1 for MembershipTier. Test it: select “Gold” tier — which stores serve Gold-tier customers?






NoteSolution






On the Sales Overview page, add a Slicer visual.
Drag MembershipTier from customers into the Field well.
The slicer shows three options: Bronze, Gold, Silver.
Click Gold.

The Revenue by Store chart filters to show only transactions by Gold-tier customers (Marcus Lee in Carlton, Priya Sharma in Fitzroy). FreshMart Carlton and FreshMart Fitzroy should be the only stores with revenue. All other stores disappear or show zero — none of the other customers are Gold tier.



Exercise 4. Create a Line Chart on Page 1 showing monthly revenue trend (X-axis: MonthLabel, Y-axis: Total Revenue). A line chart is better than a column chart for showing a trend over time. Add a trend line to the visual.






NoteSolution






Add a Line chart visual (the icon with a zigzag line).
X-axis: MonthLabel from transactions.
Y-axis: Total Revenue measure.
To add a trend line: Format visual → Analytics → Trend line → toggle On.

The line chart shows monthly revenue from January to June 2024. The trend line provides a statistical average direction. If revenue appears relatively flat, it reflects that the FreshMart dataset was constructed with consistent monthly spend — in a real business, you would expect seasonal variation.
Note: MonthLabel sorts alphabetically by default (Apr, Feb, Jan…) which looks wrong. To fix the sort order, you need a sort column. Create a new calculated column in transactions: MonthSort = FORMAT(transactions[TransactionDate], "YYYYMM") — then in the Data view, select MonthLabel column, go to Column tools in the ribbon → Sort by column → select MonthSort. The line chart will now display months chronologically.



Exercise 5. The Small Multiples chart shows monthly revenue by store. Currently FreshMart Darwin is missing. Explain in plain English why Darwin does not appear, and describe what SQL JOIN concept this mirrors.






NoteSolution





FreshMart Darwin does not appear because it has zero rows in the Transactions table. When Power BI aggregates revenue by store, it performs what is effectively an INNER JOIN between transactions and stores — only stores that appear in transactions are included in the result. Darwin exists in stores but has nothing in transactions to join on.
This is the same as the SQL chapter example: a RIGHT JOIN or LEFT JOIN was needed to surface Darwin as a store with no recorded sales. In Power BI, you can expose Darwin by:

Creating a measure that explicitly handles missing values
Using a visual that references the stores table directly (so all stores appear on the axis) rather than letting the aggregation drive which stores appear
Adding the StoreName from stores directly to the axis instead of letting it auto-derive from transactions

This is intentional — the FreshMart Darwin gap is designed to teach exactly this concept, whether you encounter it in SQL or in Power BI.










NoteChapter Summary



In this chapter you built a complete two-page interactive dashboard:

Created and named two Report Pages (Sales Overview, Customer Insights)
Wrote three DAX measures (Total Revenue, Transaction Count, Avg Order Value) and two calculated columns (FullName, MonthLabel)
Added a Slicer for month filtering with cross-page interactivity
Built a Doughnut Chart for membership tier breakdown
Created a Map visual showing revenue by state
Applied Drill-down to a column chart for category → product navigation
Created Small Multiples to compare monthly trends across stores
Added Action Buttons for page navigation
Configured Edit Interactions to control cross-filtering behaviour
Applied consistent formatting across the dashboard

You now have the skills to build production-quality Power BI dashboards. The specific charts and features covered here represent the core toolkit of any working data analyst. The next frontier — if you want to go further — is learning more DAX functions (particularly CALCULATE, FILTER, RANKX, and time intelligence functions like TOTALYTD) and connecting Power BI to live database sources rather than CSV files.
crumbs: 7  Power BI: Building Your Dashboard

title: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
8.1 6.1 Why R for Business Analytics?
R is a free, open-source programming language designed for statistical computing and data analysis. It is widely used by data analysts, statisticians, and data scientists across industries — from banking and retail to healthcare and government.
You might be wondering: I can already do calculations in Excel — why learn R?
That’s a fair question. Excel is a powerful tool, and you’ll keep using it. But R can do things that are either impossible or very tedious in Excel:
crumbs: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis

title: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
Repeatable analysis. An R script is a complete record of every step you took. Run it again tomorrow on fresh data and you get updated results in seconds. No clicking, no re-doing.
Handles large datasets easily. FreshMart processes thousands of transactions per day. R handles millions of rows without breaking a sweat.
Publication-quality graphics. The ggplot2 package produces graphics that belong in annual reports, not just in a spreadsheet.
Statistical modelling. Regression, classification, clustering — all built-in or one package away.


8.1.1 The tidyverse
The tidyverse is a collection of R packages designed to work together, all sharing a common philosophy: data in, insight out. The key packages we’ll use are:



Package
What it does




readr
Reads CSV and other data files into R


dplyr
Filters, groups, summarises, and transforms data


ggplot2
Creates graphics using a “grammar of graphics”


tidyr
Reshapes data between wide and long formats



All tidyverse packages are free, maintained by the Posit team (the company behind RStudio), and run in the interactive code blocks on this page via webR — no installation required.






NoteWhat is webR?



Every code block on this page is live and interactive. Click the ▶ Run button to execute it — R is running directly in your browser. You can edit any code block and re-run it to experiment. Your changes don’t affect anyone else.
crumbs: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis

title: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
8.2 6.2 The FreshMart Customer Dataset
Throughout the statistical modelling chapters we’ll work with a single dataset: FreshMart customers. This table was built by combining the Customers and Transactions database tables you worked with in the SQL chapters — each row now represents one customer, summarising their entire history with FreshMart.








Variable
Type
Description




CustomerID
Integer
Unique customer identifier


FirstName / LastName
Character
Customer name


City
Character
Customer’s city


JoinDate
Date
Date they registered


LoyaltyPoints
Integer
Accumulated loyalty points


TotalSpend
Numeric
Total $ spent at FreshMart (lifetime)


TotalTransactions
Integer
Number of purchase visits


AvgTransactionValue
Numeric
Average $ per visit


DaysSinceFirstPurchase
Integer
How long they’ve been active


IsHighValue
Logical
TRUE if TotalSpend > $500


SpendCategory
Character
“Low” (<$200), “Medium” ($200–$500), “High” (>$500)



You’ll notice some familiar faces in this dataset — Marcus Lee, Gary McSnarl, Jake Nguyen, and the others from the SQL chapters are all here.

8.2.1 Loading the data
Run this block first. It loads the tidyverse and reads in the FreshMart dataset. Every code block in this chapter depends on having this run first.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


8.2.2 Inspecting the structure
glimpse() gives you a compact summary of every column — its name, data type, and the first few values. Think of it as the first thing you run whenever you load a new dataset.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
head() shows the first few rows as a table.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Important✏️ Concept Check — Section 6.2



Q1. How many customers are in the dataset?
Q2. What does <dbl> mean next to a column name in glimpse() output?
Q3. Look at row 1. Is this customer high-value? How can you tell from the IsHighValue column?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. 500 customers (500 rows).
A2. <dbl> stands for “double” — a numeric variable that can have decimal places. You’ll also see <int> (whole numbers), <chr> (text/character), <lgl> (TRUE/FALSE logical), and <date> (dates).
A3. Check the IsHighValue column. If it reads TRUE, the customer has spent more than $500 lifetime. If FALSE, they haven’t reached that threshold yet.
crumbs: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis

title: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
8.3 6.3 Descriptive Statistics with dplyr
Before drawing any graphs or fitting any models, we always start with numbers. The dplyr package makes it easy to calculate summary statistics — even broken down by groups.

8.3.1 The pipe operator |>
In R, the pipe (|>) passes the result of one step into the next. Read it as “and then”:
freshmart |>         # start with freshmart, AND THEN
  filter(...) |>    # filter rows, AND THEN
  summarise(...)    # summarise
This style keeps code readable and matches how we think: “take the data, then do this, then do that.”


8.3.2 Summarising the whole dataset
summarise() computes a summary of the entire dataset. mean(), median(), sd(), min(), max(), and n() are the most useful functions inside it.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Notice that the mean ($414) is much higher than the median ($215). This happens when a small number of high-spending customers (like Marcus Lee with $12,300) pull the average upward. The median — the middle value — is not affected by extreme values and often better represents the “typical” customer.






Note📚 Recall: Mean vs Median



From first-year statistics: the mean is sensitive to outliers; the median is not. When a distribution is right-skewed (a long tail to the right), the mean > median. This is exactly what we see with customer spend data.




8.3.3 Summarising by group
group_by() splits the dataset into groups before summarising. This is one of the most powerful ideas in data analysis.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


8.3.4 Filtering rows
filter() keeps only the rows that match a condition.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Recognise Marcus Lee in the top spot? And Bev O’Brien in the top 5?






Important✏️ Concept Check — Section 6.3



Q1. The mean spend for Sydney is higher than for Melbourne. Does this mean every Sydney customer spends more than every Melbourne customer? Explain.
Q2. Why might the marketing team prefer to look at median spend by city rather than mean spend?
Q3. Write the dplyr code to find the 3 cities with the lowest median spend. (Hint: change arrange(desc(...)) to arrange(...))








Tip✅ Answers





A1. No. The mean is an average across all customers in that city. Individual customers in Melbourne may spend far more than some Sydney customers. The city average doesn’t tell us about any individual.
A2. A single very high-spending customer (like Marcus Lee in Sydney) can inflate the mean for that city, making it look like Sydney customers typically spend more when most of them might not.
A3.
freshmart |>
  group_by(City) |>
  summarise(median_spend = median(TotalSpend)) |>
  arrange(median_spend) |>
  slice_head(n = 3)
crumbs: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis

title: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
8.4 6.4 Visualising Distributions with ggplot2
Numbers tell us the centre and spread, but a graph tells us the shape. The ggplot2 package builds graphics using the grammar of graphics — a consistent layering system.
Every ggplot has three essential parts:
ggplot(data, aes(x = variable)) +   # 1. What data and which variable?
  geom_histogram() +                 # 2. What kind of geometry (shape)?
  labs(title = "My Plot")            # 3. Labels and styling

8.4.1 Histograms — seeing the shape of a distribution

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
What does this tell us?
The distribution is strongly right-skewed: most customers cluster in the $0–$500 range, but a long tail extends to the right where a small group of high-spending customers live (including Marcus Lee at $12,300). This is typical for retail spending data.






Note📚 Recall: Distribution Shape




Symmetric: mean ≈ median; bell-shaped
Right-skewed (positive skew): long tail to the right; mean > median
Left-skewed (negative skew): long tail to the left; mean < median





8.4.2 Log scale for skewed data
When data is this skewed, a log scale can make patterns clearer:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
On a log scale, the distribution looks much more symmetric — a useful reminder that the underlying purchasing patterns aren’t as extreme as the raw numbers suggest.


8.4.3 Boxplots — comparing groups
A boxplot summarises a distribution in five numbers: minimum, Q1, median, Q3, maximum. It’s ideal for comparing groups side by side.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Notice how the “High” category has much more spread (tall box, long whiskers) than “Low” — this tells us that high-value customers vary a lot in how much they spend, while low-value customers cluster tightly in a small range.






Note📦 R Practice — Exercise 6.4.1



Modify the histogram code above to show the distribution of TotalTransactions instead of TotalSpend. Change the title and axis labels appropriately. Does it have the same skewed shape?








Tip✅ Solution





ggplot(freshmart, aes(x = TotalTransactions)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#8FBFCA", colour = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Number of Transactions — FreshMart",
    x     = "Total Number of Transactions",
    y     = "Number of Customers"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
Yes — it has a similar right-skewed shape. Most customers visit FreshMart only a few times, but a small group are very frequent shoppers.









Note📦 R Practice — Exercise 6.4.2



Create a boxplot comparing AvgTransactionValue across cities. Which city has the highest typical transaction value? Are there any outliers?
(Hint: use City on the x-axis and add + coord_flip() at the end to make it easier to read the city names.)








Tip✅ Solution





ggplot(freshmart, aes(x = City, y = AvgTransactionValue, fill = City)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Transaction Value by City",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Average Transaction Value ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  coord_flip()









Important✏️ Concept Check — Section 6.4



Q1. The box in a boxplot spans from Q1 to Q3. What is this range called, and what fraction of the data does it contain?
Q2. In ggplot2, what does aes() stand for, and what kind of information goes inside it?
Q3. A histogram for customer ages looks like a bell curve (symmetric). Would the mean or median be a better description of the “typical” customer age? Why?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. The interquartile range (IQR). It contains the middle 50% of the data.
A2. aes() stands for aesthetics — the visual properties mapped to variables in the data: which variable goes on the x-axis, which on the y-axis, which controls colour, fill, size, etc.
A3. For a symmetric distribution, mean and median are approximately equal, so either works. In practice, analysts often report the mean for symmetric data and the median for skewed data.

title: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
8.5 6.5 Relationships Between Variables
So far we’ve looked at one variable at a time. But the interesting questions are usually about relationships: does spending more money earn more loyalty points? Do customers who visit more often also spend more per visit?

8.5.1 Scatter plots
A scatter plot shows the relationship between two numeric variables. Each dot is one customer.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
There’s a clear positive relationship: customers who visit more often tend to spend more overall. The trend isn’t perfect (there’s scatter around the line), but the direction is unmistakable. This is exactly the kind of relationship a regression model can quantify — something we’ll do in Chapter 7.






Note📚 Recall: Correlation



From first-year statistics: the correlation coefficient (r) measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables.

r ranges from −1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive)
r = 0 means no linear relationship
r = 0.7 is a strong positive relationship
r = −0.3 is a weak negative relationship

Correlation measures linear relationships only. A curved relationship may have r ≈ 0 even if the relationship is strong.




8.5.2 Correlation matrix with corrr
The corrr package (part of the tidymodels ecosystem) makes tidy correlation tables easy to read and visualise.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
What do we see?

TotalSpend and LoyaltyPoints have near-perfect correlation — they were both earned from the same purchasing behaviour.
TotalSpend and TotalTransactions are strongly correlated (r ≈ 0.7) — customers who visit more spend more.
DaysSinceFirstPurchase has a moderate positive correlation with spend — longer-tenured customers have had more time to accumulate spend.
AvgTransactionValue is less strongly related — some customers make fewer but bigger purchases.



8.5.3 Pairwise scatter plots with GGally
For a quick visual overview of all pairwise relationships at once, GGally::ggpairs() is invaluable:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The diagonal shows each variable’s distribution; the lower panels show scatter plots; the upper panels show correlation coefficients. This is the analyst’s “first look” chart — run it before every modelling project.






Note📦 R Practice — Exercise 6.5.1



The scatter plot above coloured points by SpendCategory. Try colouring by City instead. Which cities seem to have the most high-spending customers?








Tip✅ Solution





ggplot(freshmart, aes(x = TotalTransactions, y = TotalSpend, colour = City)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(
    title  = "Total Transactions vs Total Spend, coloured by City",
    x      = "Number of Transactions",
    y      = "Total Spend ($AUD)",
    colour = "City"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
Sydney and Melbourne customers appear across the full range of spend levels because they make up the largest share of customers.









Important✏️ Concept Check — Section 6.5



Q1. The correlation between TotalSpend and LoyaltyPoints is very close to 1.0. Does this mean LoyaltyPoints caused the high spend? Explain.
Q2. A scatter plot shows that customers with higher DaysSinceFirstPurchase tend to have higher TotalSpend, but there’s a lot of scatter. What does “a lot of scatter” tell you about the strength of the relationship?
Q3. FreshMart’s CEO says: “Customers who have more loyalty points spend more, so if we give everyone 1000 free points, they’ll spend more.” What’s the flaw in this reasoning?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. No. Correlation tells us two variables move together — but it says nothing about cause and effect. In this case, both variables are driven by the same underlying behaviour (purchasing). Loyalty points are earned by spending, so they naturally correlate — but points didn’t cause the spending.
A2. A lot of scatter means the relationship is not very tight — knowing someone’s tenure doesn’t precisely predict their spend. There’s a general tendency, but many individual exceptions exist.
A3. This is a classic correlation ≠ causation error. High points and high spend are correlated because they both come from the same purchasing behaviour. Giving away free points doesn’t change underlying purchasing habits.

title: 8  Introduction to R & Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
8.6 6.6 Chapter Exercises
Work through these exercises independently. Each builds on the code from this chapter — you can copy, modify, and run it in the webR blocks above.







NoteExercise 6.1 — Exploring a Different Variable



Using summarise() and group_by(), calculate the mean, median, and standard deviation of TotalTransactions broken down by City. Which city has the most variable number of transactions per customer?








Tip✅ Solution





freshmart |>
  group_by(City) |>
  summarise(
    mean_txns   = round(mean(TotalTransactions), 1),
    median_txns = median(TotalTransactions),
    sd_txns     = round(sd(TotalTransactions), 1)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(sd_txns))
The city with the highest sd_txns has the most variable number of transactions — a large standard deviation means customers there range widely from very infrequent to very frequent shoppers.










NoteExercise 6.2 — Finding Our Characters



Use filter() to find the rows for Marcus Lee, Jake Nguyen, and Nina Patel. Display their FirstName, City, TotalSpend, TotalTransactions, and IsHighValue.








Tip✅ Solution





freshmart |>
  filter(FirstName %in% c("Marcus", "Jake", "Nina")) |>
  select(FirstName, LastName, City, TotalSpend, TotalTransactions, IsHighValue)
You should see Marcus Lee at $12,300 (high value), Jake Nguyen at $340 (not high value), and Nina Patel at $0 (never purchased).










NoteExercise 6.3 — Histogram Choice



Create a histogram of DaysSinceFirstPurchase. Describe its shape. Is this what you’d expect — why or why not?








Tip✅ Solution





ggplot(freshmart, aes(x = DaysSinceFirstPurchase)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#8FBFCA", colour = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "How Long Have Customers Been with FreshMart?",
    x     = "Days Since First Purchase",
    y     = "Number of Customers"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
The distribution is roughly uniform (flat) — customers joined FreshMart at all different times, so there’s no particular “peak.” This makes sense for a dataset representing active customers across several years.










NoteExercise 6.4 — Counting Categories



Using count(), find how many customers are in each combination of City and SpendCategory. Which city-category combination has the most customers?
(Hint: count(City, SpendCategory) then arrange(desc(n)))








Tip✅ Solution





freshmart |>
  count(City, SpendCategory) |>
  arrange(desc(n))
Sydney and Melbourne dominate the top rows, as expected given their larger population weights in the dataset.










NoteExercise 6.5 — Outlier Identification



Use filter() to find all customers with TotalSpend greater than $3,000. How many are there? List their names and cities.








Tip✅ Solution





freshmart |>
  filter(TotalSpend > 3000) |>
  select(FirstName, LastName, City, TotalSpend) |>
  arrange(desc(TotalSpend))
There are very few customers above $3,000 — this is the extreme right tail of the distribution. Marcus Lee ($12,300) and Bev O’Brien ($3,240) will appear here.










NoteExercise 6.6 — A Better Scatter Plot



Recreate the TotalTransactions vs TotalSpend scatter plot, but this time exclude Nina Patel (who has 0 transactions and 0 spend) and add axis labels that include dollar signs on the y-axis. Does removing Nina change the trend line noticeably?








Tip✅ Solution





freshmart |>
  filter(TotalSpend > 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = TotalTransactions, y = TotalSpend)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, colour = "#8FBFCA", size = 1.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", colour = "#9B8EC7") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(
    title = "Transactions vs Spend (Nina Patel excluded)",
    x     = "Total Transactions",
    y     = "Total Spend ($AUD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
The trend line should be very similar — one data point at (0, 0) has minimal effect on a 500-row dataset.










NoteExercise 6.7 — Correlation Interpretation



The correlation matrix shows r = 0.705 between TotalSpend and TotalTransactions. Write 2–3 sentences interpreting this value for a non-technical FreshMart manager. Avoid the word “correlation” — explain it in plain business language.








Tip✅ Solution





Sample answer: “Customers who visit FreshMart more often tend to spend more money overall — but this relationship isn’t perfectly predictable. A customer who visits twice as often doesn’t necessarily spend exactly twice as much; some infrequent visitors spend a lot per trip, while some frequent visitors only pop in for small purchases. Overall though, visit frequency is a reliable indicator of total customer value.”










NoteExercise 6.8 — Putting It Together



Write a short paragraph (4–6 sentences) summarising the key findings from your exploratory analysis of the FreshMart customer dataset. Imagine you are presenting to the Head of Marketing before the modelling team begins building predictive models. What do they need to know about the data?








Tip✅ Sample Answer





“The FreshMart customer dataset contains 500 customers from seven Australian cities, with Sydney and Melbourne making up the majority. Customer spending is highly right-skewed: most customers spend less than $500 lifetime, but a small group of high-value customers (about 25% of the base) drive a disproportionate share of revenue — Marcus Lee alone accounts for over $12,000 in lifetime spend. The number of transactions a customer makes is the strongest predictor of their total spend (r ≈ 0.70), suggesting that visit frequency is a key driver of customer value. Loyalty points are almost perfectly correlated with total spend, meaning they contain very similar information. One customer (Nina Patel) has registered but never purchased — she may represent a segment worth targeting with a first-purchase incentive.”





  ✅ Week 6 Complete
  You've loaded, explored, and visualised the FreshMart dataset. In Week 7 we'll take the next step: building a regression model to predict how much a customer is likely to spend — moving from description to prediction.
