title: 9  Week 7: Linear Regression
text:
9.1 7.1 From Description to Prediction
In Chapter 6 we described the FreshMart customers: we found that customers who visit more often tend to spend more. But description only tells us about the past. Prediction asks: given what we know about a new customer today, what will they likely do in the future?
A regression model is a mathematical equation that uses one or more input variables (called predictors or features) to produce a predicted numeric output (called the outcome or target).
For FreshMart, we want to predict:
\[\text{TotalSpend} = f(\text{TotalTransactions}, \text{DaysSinceFirstPurchase}, \text{City}, \ldots)\]
The simplest version of this is linear regression — which assumes the relationship between predictors and outcome is a straight line.

title: 9  Week 7: Linear Regression
text:
9.2 7.2 Simple Linear Regression

9.2.1 The model equation
Simple linear regression uses one predictor to predict the outcome. The model is:
\[\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x\]
Where:

\(\hat{y}\) is the predicted value of the outcome (TotalSpend)
\(x\) is the predictor (e.g., TotalTransactions)
\(\beta_0\) is the intercept — the predicted \(\hat{y}\) when \(x = 0\)
\(\beta_1\) is the slope — how much \(\hat{y}\) changes for each 1-unit increase in \(x\)

The model finds the values of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) that make the line fit the data as closely as possible. “As closely as possible” means minimising the sum of squared residuals — the squared differences between actual and predicted values.






Note📚 Recall: The Line of Best Fit



In first-year statistics you encountered lines of best fit in the context of scatter plots. Linear regression is exactly that — but now we use it to make numerical predictions and we measure how good the line is formally.
The residual for observation \(i\) is: \(e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\) (actual minus predicted). A good model has small residuals.


The animation below sweeps through many candidate lines. Each orange segment is a residual. Watch how the SSE (sum of squared residuals) in the title changes — the green line at the minimum SSE is exactly what linear regression finds automatically.








9.2.2 Setting up — load packages and data
Run this block first. It loads tidymodels and the FreshMart dataset, and creates the training/test split we’ll need throughout the chapter.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






NoteWhy split the data?



We split the dataset into a training set and a test set. The model is fitted on the training set only. The test set is held out until the very end — it represents “new customers the model has never seen” and gives us an honest estimate of how well the model will perform in the real world.
set.seed(42) makes the random split reproducible — you’ll get the same split every time you run this code.




9.2.3 The tidymodels workflow
tidymodels uses a consistent three-step recipe for building any model:
1. Specify   →  What type of model? What engine?
2. Fit       →  Train the model on the training data
3. Evaluate  →  Check performance on the test data
This might seem like extra work compared to base R’s lm(), but the big advantage is that every model in this course — logistic regression, trees, KNN — follows exactly the same three steps. Change one line and you change the model type.


9.2.4 Fitting a simple linear regression

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The tidy() function from the broom package (part of tidymodels) returns the model results as a clean tibble. Let’s read the output:



Column
Meaning




term
Which coefficient (intercept, or the predictor name)


estimate
The estimated value of \(\beta_0\) or \(\beta_1\)


std.error
How uncertain we are about that estimate


statistic
The t-statistic for the hypothesis test


p.value
Is this coefficient statistically different from zero?



Reading the output:

(Intercept) ≈ some value — this is the predicted spend for a customer with zero transactions
TotalTransactions coefficient ≈ some positive value — for each additional transaction, this is how much more we predict the customer will spend







Note📚 Recall: p-values



From first-year statistics: a p-value measures the probability of seeing a result as extreme as we observed, if there were truly no relationship. Convention: if p < 0.05, we say the result is “statistically significant” — i.e., the predictor appears to genuinely influence the outcome (rather than just by random chance).




9.2.5 The model equation and predictions

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Important✏️ Concept Check — Section 7.2



Q1. In the fitted equation TotalSpend = 25.60 + 18.40 × TotalTransactions, what does the slope of 18.40 mean in plain English?
Q2. A customer has made 10 transactions. Using the equation above, what is their predicted total spend?
Q3. The p-value for TotalTransactions is 0.000 (effectively zero). What does this tell you?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. For every additional transaction a customer makes, their predicted total lifetime spend increases by $18.40, on average — holding everything else constant.
A2. $25.60 + 18.40 × 10 = $25.60 + $184.00 = $209.60
A3. A p-value of essentially zero means the probability of seeing a slope this large just by chance (if there were no real relationship) is effectively zero. We have very strong statistical evidence that TotalTransactions genuinely predicts TotalSpend.

title: 9  Week 7: Linear Regression
section: 9.3 7.3 Model Fit: How Good Is Our Line?
text:
9.3 7.3 Model Fit: How Good Is Our Line?

9.3.1 R² — the proportion of variance explained
glance() gives overall model statistics:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The key statistic is R² (r-squared): the proportion of variation in TotalSpend that is explained by the model. R² ranges from 0 to 1:

R² = 0: the model explains nothing (no better than guessing the mean every time)
R² = 1: the model explains everything perfectly (every prediction is exact)
R² = 0.50: the model explains half the variation in TotalSpend



9.3.2 RMSE — how big are our errors?
R² tells us about relative fit. The Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) tells us the average size of our errors in the same units as the outcome ($AUD):
\[\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}\]

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
An RMSE of, say, $150 means our predictions are off by about $150 on average. Whether that’s acceptable depends on the business context — good enough to prioritise customer outreach, perhaps not good enough to set individual credit limits.


9.3.3 Residual plot
A residual plot checks whether the errors are random (good) or have a pattern (bad). We want residuals scattered randomly around zero with no trend.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Note



What to look for: If the residuals fan out as predictions increase (a “funnel” shape), this suggests the model is less accurate for high-spending customers. This is common with right-skewed outcome variables like TotalSpend.








Important✏️ Concept Check — Section 7.3



Q1. R² = 0.52 on the test set. Describe what this means in plain English.
Q2. RMSE = $210 and the average TotalSpend is $414. Is this a good result? How would you decide?
Q3. You look at the residual plot and see that all the large positive residuals (model under-predicted) are from Melbourne customers. What does this suggest?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. The model explains 52% of the variation in customer total spend. The remaining 48% is driven by things our single predictor doesn’t capture — city, average transaction value, tenure, or purely random differences between customers.
A2. This is contextual. RMSE of $210 on an average spend of $414 means a typical error of about 50% of the average value — quite large. However, for a model with just one predictor, it might be a reasonable starting point. We’d compare against a baseline (e.g., “what if we just always predicted the mean spend?”) and against a model with more predictors.
A3. It suggests our model is systematically under-predicting spend for Melbourne customers — there’s something about Melbourne that our predictor doesn’t capture. Adding City as a predictor (which we’ll do next) should fix this.

title: 9  Week 7: Linear Regression
text:
9.4 7.4 Multiple Linear Regression
A model with just one predictor often misses important information. Multiple regression uses several predictors simultaneously. The equation becomes:
\[\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_3 x_3 + \ldots\]
Each coefficient \(\beta_j\) shows the effect of predictor \(x_j\) on the outcome while holding all other predictors constant.

9.4.1 The recipe approach
When we have multiple predictors — especially categorical ones like City — we use a recipe to preprocess the data before fitting. A recipe describes transformations to apply to the predictors.
City is a categorical variable (text). Most statistical models need numeric inputs, so we convert it to dummy variables: one binary column per city (1 = that city, 0 = not).

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Notice that City has been replaced by several binary columns: City_Darwin, City_Melbourne, etc. (one city is omitted as the “baseline” to avoid redundancy — this is called the reference category).


9.4.2 Building the workflow

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Interpreting the coefficients:

TotalTransactions: for each additional visit, predicted spend increases by $X — holding all other predictors constant
DaysSinceFirstPurchase: for each additional day of tenure, spend increases by $Y
City_Melbourne: customers in Melbourne spend $Z more (or less if negative) than the reference city — on average, given the same transactions and tenure







Note📦 R Practice — Exercise 7.4.1



The fm_recipe uses TotalTransactions, AvgTransactionValue, DaysSinceFirstPurchase, and City as predictors. Try removing AvgTransactionValue from the recipe. Does the model’s R² increase or decrease? What does this tell you?








Tip✅ Solution





fm_recipe_v2 <- recipe(
  TotalSpend ~ TotalTransactions + DaysSinceFirstPurchase + City,
  data = fm_train
) |>
  step_dummy(City)

lm_v2 <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(fm_recipe_v2) |>
  add_model(lm_spec) |>
  fit(data = fm_train)

glance(lm_v2)
If R² decreases, it means AvgTransactionValue was contributing useful predictive information. If it barely changes, the variable wasn’t adding much beyond what TotalTransactions already captures.









Important✏️ Concept Check — Section 7.4



Q1. In multiple regression, the coefficient for TotalTransactions is interpreted “holding all other predictors constant.” Why is this phrase important?
Q2. After adding City to the model, R² increases from 0.52 to 0.58. Is this extra complexity worth it?
Q3. Why do we use dummy variables for City rather than coding it as 1=Sydney, 2=Melbourne, 3=Brisbane, etc.?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. Without this phrase, we might confuse the individual effect of transactions with the combined effect of transactions and other variables. In multiple regression, each coefficient isolates the effect of one predictor, assuming the others don’t change. This is what makes the coefficients interpretable.
A2. It depends. An increase of 0.06 in R² is modest — the model explains 6% more variation. Whether this is worth the added complexity (and harder-to-explain coefficients) is a judgement call. In practice, we’d also check whether the improvement holds on the test set, not just training.
A3. If we coded Sydney = 1, Melbourne = 2, Brisbane = 3, the model would treat this as a numeric variable — implying that Melbourne is “twice Sydney” and Brisbane is “three times Sydney.” That makes no mathematical sense. Dummy variables avoid this by treating each city as a separate binary yes/no.

title: 9  Week 7: Linear Regression
text:
9.5 7.5 Making Predictions
Once a model is fitted, we can use it to predict outcomes for new, unseen customers.

9.5.1 Evaluating on the test set
This is the most important evaluation — the test set simulates real “new customers” the model has never seen.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Points close to the diagonal line are accurate predictions. Points far from the line are cases where our model struggles — notice the high-spending customers (top-right area) where the model tends to under-predict.


9.5.2 Predicting for new customers
Imagine three new FreshMart sign-ups. What is the model’s prediction for each?

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Note📦 R Practice — Exercise 7.5.1



Add a fourth “new customer” to the new_customers tibble: someone from Darwin who has made 25 transactions with an average of $80, and has been a customer for 500 days. What does the model predict their total spend to be?








Tip✅ Solution





new_customers_v2 <- tibble(
  FirstName              = c("Alice", "Bob", "Chloe", "Dan"),
  TotalTransactions      = c(3, 18, 8, 25),
  AvgTransactionValue    = c(45, 60, 120, 80),
  DaysSinceFirstPurchase = c(60, 400, 180, 500),
  City                   = c("Brisbane", "Sydney", "Melbourne", "Darwin")
)

predict(lm_multi, new_data = new_customers_v2) |>
  bind_cols(new_customers_v2 |> select(FirstName, City)) |>
  rename(PredictedSpend = .pred)









Important✏️ Concept Check — Section 7.5



Q1. Why do we evaluate model performance on the test set rather than the training set?
Q2. The actual-vs-predicted plot shows the model consistently under-predicts for customers with spend above $2,000. What could explain this?
Q3. A colleague suggests: “Since the model was trained on all our existing customers, why not just retrain it every week with the latest data?” What is the benefit of this approach?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. Because the model was fitted on the training data — it has “seen” those rows. Evaluating on training data would be like giving a student the exam before the test and then measuring how well they do. The test set is genuinely unseen data, giving us an honest estimate of real-world performance.
A2. Several possibilities: (1) High spenders may have predictors (like very high transaction frequency or very high avg value) that are outside the range of the training data — the model is “extrapolating.” (2) The linear assumption may break down at high spend levels — the relationship may be non-linear. (3) There may be predictor variables we haven’t included (like product category preferences) that differentiate high spenders.
A3. The benefit is that the model stays up to date with changing customer behaviour. If customer patterns shift (e.g., post-COVID shopping habits change), a model trained on old data will drift — regular retraining keeps it current.

title: 9  Week 7: Linear Regression
text:
9.6 7.6 A Word on Model Assumptions
Linear regression makes several assumptions. Violating them doesn’t always break the model, but it can make the predictions unreliable or the p-values misleading. Here are the key ones — with FreshMart context:
1. Linearity. The relationship between each predictor and the outcome should be (approximately) linear. Our residual plot can reveal non-linearity (a curved pattern in the residuals).
2. Independence. Observations should be independent of each other. For FreshMart, this is reasonable — one customer’s spend doesn’t directly affect another’s.
3. Constant variance (homoscedasticity). The spread of residuals should be similar across all predicted values. If residuals fan out as predicted values increase (a funnel shape), this is a problem.
4. Normality of residuals. The residuals should be approximately normally distributed. This matters most for small samples and for the reliability of p-values.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: 9  Week 7: Linear Regression
text:
9.7 7.7 Chapter Exercises







NoteExercise 7.1 — Reading Coefficient Output



Run the multiple regression and look at the tidy() output. Find the coefficient for DaysSinceFirstPurchase. Write one sentence interpreting it in plain English for a FreshMart manager.








Tip✅ Solution





tidy(lm_multi) |> filter(term == "DaysSinceFirstPurchase")
Sample interpretation: “For each additional day that a customer has been with FreshMart, their predicted total lifetime spend increases by approximately $[coefficient value] — holding their visit frequency, average transaction value, and city constant.”










NoteExercise 7.2 — Comparing Simple vs Multiple Regression



Compare the test-set RMSE and R² of the simple regression (TotalSpend ~ TotalTransactions) to the multiple regression. How much did adding more predictors improve performance?
(Hint: run metrics() on the predictions from both models.)








Tip✅ Solution





# Simple model on test set
lm_simple_preds <- augment(lm_simple, new_data = fm_test)
metrics(lm_simple_preds, truth = TotalSpend, estimate = .pred)

# Multiple model on test set
lm_multi_preds <- augment(lm_multi, new_data = fm_test)
metrics(lm_multi_preds, truth = TotalSpend, estimate = .pred)
Compare R² and RMSE between the two. The multiple regression should have higher R² and lower RMSE, showing that the additional predictors genuinely improve predictions.










NoteExercise 7.3 — A New Recipe



Build a new recipe that uses LoyaltyPoints and DaysSinceFirstPurchase as predictors (no City, no dummy variables needed). Fit the model and evaluate on the test set. Is it better or worse than the multiple regression from Section 7.4?








Tip✅ Solution





simple_recipe <- recipe(
  TotalSpend ~ LoyaltyPoints + DaysSinceFirstPurchase,
  data = fm_train
)

simple_wf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(simple_recipe) |>
  add_model(lm_spec) |>
  fit(data = fm_train)

augment(simple_wf, new_data = fm_test) |>
  metrics(truth = TotalSpend, estimate = .pred)
Because LoyaltyPoints is nearly perfectly correlated with TotalSpend (it’s earned from spending), this model will appear to perform extremely well — but be careful: this is almost “cheating” because loyalty points are a direct consequence of spend, not something you’d know before the customer spends. Discuss this with your class — it illustrates the importance of thinking about what you can actually know at prediction time.










NoteExercise 7.4 — Predicting for FreshMart Characters



Use the multiple regression model to predict total spend for:

Gary McSnarl: 1 transaction, $34.90 avg value, 1,026 days tenure, Melbourne
Jake Nguyen: 7 transactions, $48.57 avg value, 795 days tenure, Melbourne
Amara Diallo: 14 transactions, $62.86 avg value, 146 days tenure, Perth

Compare the predictions to their actual spend (from Chapter 6). Is the model accurate for these customers?








Tip✅ Solution





characters <- tibble(
  FirstName              = c("Gary", "Jake", "Amara"),
  TotalTransactions      = c(1, 7, 14),
  AvgTransactionValue    = c(34.90, 48.57, 62.86),
  DaysSinceFirstPurchase = c(1026, 795, 146),
  City                   = c("Melbourne", "Melbourne", "Perth")
)

predict(lm_multi, new_data = characters) |>
  bind_cols(characters |> select(FirstName)) |>
  mutate(
    ActualSpend = c(34.90, 340.00, 880.00),
    Error       = round(.pred - ActualSpend, 2)
  )
The model may under-predict Gary (very atypical, churned customer) and Amara (recent high-spender). This illustrates that linear regression captures average behaviour — it can struggle with unusual customers.










NoteExercise 7.5 — Overfitting Check



You’ve been asked to compare a “simple” model (TotalTransactions only) vs a “complex” model (all predictors). Calculate both models’ R² on the training set and the test set. Present the results in a small table. Which model overfits more, and how can you tell?








Tip✅ Solution





# Simple model
s_train <- augment(lm_simple, new_data = fm_train) |>
  metrics(truth = TotalSpend, estimate = .pred) |>
  filter(.metric == "rsq") |>
  mutate(model = "Simple", set = "Train")

s_test <- augment(lm_simple, new_data = fm_test) |>
  metrics(truth = TotalSpend, estimate = .pred) |>
  filter(.metric == "rsq") |>
  mutate(model = "Simple", set = "Test")

# Multiple model
m_train <- augment(lm_multi, new_data = fm_train) |>
  metrics(truth = TotalSpend, estimate = .pred) |>
  filter(.metric == "rsq") |>
  mutate(model = "Multiple", set = "Train")

m_test <- augment(lm_multi, new_data = fm_test) |>
  metrics(truth = TotalSpend, estimate = .pred) |>
  filter(.metric == "rsq") |>
  mutate(model = "Multiple", set = "Test")

bind_rows(s_train, s_test, m_train, m_test) |>
  select(model, set, .estimate) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = set, values_from = .estimate)
A model that overfits shows a large gap between training and test R². If both gaps are small, the models are generalising well.










NoteExercise 7.6 — Business Communication



Imagine you’ve just built the multiple regression model and you’re presenting to FreshMart’s Head of Marketing. She doesn’t know what RMSE or R² means.
Write 3–4 sentences explaining: (1) what the model does, (2) how accurate it is, and (3) one important limitation she should be aware of.
(There’s no single correct answer — aim for clarity and honesty.)








Tip✅ Sample Answer





“The model uses a customer’s visit frequency, average spend per visit, how long they’ve been a customer, and their city to estimate their total lifetime spend. In testing on customers the model had never seen, it predicted spend to within about $[RMSE] on average — meaning predictions for most customers will be in the right ballpark, but they won’t be exact. The model works best for typical customers; it tends to underestimate spend for our very highest-value customers like Marcus Lee, who behave differently from the average. We’d recommend using it as a guide for customer prioritisation, rather than for setting individual credit limits.”





  ✅ Week 7 Complete
  You've built your first predictive model and learned to evaluate it honestly on unseen data. In Week 8 we tackle a different kind of prediction problem: when the outcome is yes or no rather than a number. That's the world of logistic regression and classification.

title: 10  Logistic Regression
text:
10.1 8.1 When the Outcome Is a Category
The Head of FreshMart’s loyalty team has a new question: “Can we predict whether a new customer will become high-value, based on their early behaviour?”
If we can flag likely high-value customers early, we can send them a targeted welcome offer before they lose interest. The answer isn’t a dollar amount — it’s one of two options: Yes (High Value) or No. This is a classification problem.
Our target variable is IsHighValue — TRUE if lifetime TotalSpend > $500, FALSE otherwise.

title: 10  Logistic Regression
text:
10.1.1 Why not use linear regression for this?
You might think: fit linear regression where the outcome is 1 (TRUE) or 0 (FALSE). The problem is that a straight line can produce predictions outside the 0–1 range — such as a “probability” of −0.3 or 1.7 — which is mathematically meaningless.
Logistic regression solves this by using a special S-shaped curve called the sigmoid function that squeezes all outputs into the range 0–1.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The model outputs a probability for each customer (e.g., 78% likely to be high value). We then apply a decision threshold — by default 0.5 — to convert that probability into a class label: ≥ 0.5 → predict TRUE, < 0.5 → predict FALSE.


10.1.2 The model equation and log-odds
The sigmoid is not an arbitrary choice — it emerges from the model structure. Logistic regression assumes the log-odds of the outcome is a linear function of the predictors:
\[\log\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \cdots\]
The left side is the logit (log of the odds ratio). Solving for \(p\) gives the sigmoid directly:
\[p = P(\text{High Value} = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \cdots)}}\]
So the same linear combination as linear regression is happening inside the exponent — logistic regression is a linear model in disguise, transformed to keep outputs between 0 and 1.
Reading the coefficients: Each \(\beta_j\) is on the log-odds scale. The more practical interpretation uses the odds ratio \(e^{\beta_j}\):



\(\beta_j\)
Odds ratio \(e^{\beta_j}\)
Meaning




Positive
> 1
Higher \(x_j\) increases the probability of TRUE


Negative
< 1
Higher \(x_j\) decreases the probability of TRUE


≈ 0
≈ 1
\(x_j\) has negligible effect



For example, if the coefficient for TotalTransactions is 0.40, each additional transaction multiplies the odds of being High Value by \(e^{0.40} \approx 1.49\) — a 49% increase in odds, holding all other predictors constant.
How are coefficients estimated? Logistic regression uses maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), not OLS. The algorithm finds the \(\beta\) values that make the observed outcomes most probable:
\[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \arg\max_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \prod_{i:\,y_i=1} p_i \;\times\; \prod_{i:\,y_i=0}(1 - p_i)\]
In plain English: choose coefficients so the model assigns high probability to customers who actually were High Value, and low probability to those who weren’t. There is no closed-form solution — R’s glm() solves this iteratively via Newton-Raphson.
The animation below shows how shifting the threshold changes which customers are classified as High Value — and how accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity trade off against each other.












Important✏️ Concept Check — 8.1



Q1. A logistic model outputs 0.72 for a customer. Using the default threshold, what class do we predict?
Q2. Why can’t linear regression be used directly when the outcome is TRUE/FALSE?
Q3. Looking at the sigmoid curve: as the linear combination gets very large (far right), what does the predicted probability approach?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. 0.72 > 0.5, so we predict TRUE (High Value).
A2. A straight line can produce predictions outside 0–1 (e.g., −0.3 or 1.4). These cannot be interpreted as probabilities. The logistic function guarantees valid probability outputs.
A3. It approaches 1.0 (100%) — the model becomes increasingly confident the customer is high-value as the predictors indicate stronger purchasing behaviour.

10.2 8.2 Fitting Logistic Regression in R
The tidymodels workflow is nearly identical to Chapter 7 — we swap linear_reg() for logistic_reg() and add set_mode("classification").

10.2.1 Setup

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Notestrata = IsHighValue



Stratified splitting ensures the training and test sets have roughly the same proportion of high-value customers. Without it, random chance might leave almost all high-value customers in one set.




10.2.2 Specify → Recipe → Workflow → Fit

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Reading the output: The estimate column shows log-odds coefficients. You don’t need to work with log-odds directly — what matters is:

Positive coefficient → higher values of that predictor increase the probability of TRUE (High Value)
Negative coefficient → higher values decrease it
Small p-value (< 0.05) → that predictor is statistically significant







Note📦 R Practice — Exercise 8.2.1



Look at the tidy() output. Which predictor has the largest positive coefficient? What does this tell you about which variable most strongly predicts whether a customer becomes high-value?








Tip✅ Solution





Find the row with the largest positive estimate. Because we used step_normalize(), the coefficients are on a comparable scale — the largest coefficient corresponds to the most influential predictor. Typically TotalTransactions or AvgTransactionValue will rank highest, meaning these best distinguish high-value from regular customers.









Important✏️ Concept Check — 8.2



Q1. What is the only line that changed between the Chapter 7 linear regression workflow and this logistic regression workflow?
Q2. We used step_normalize() in the recipe. What does this do, and why does it matter here?
Q3. The coefficient for DaysSinceFirstPurchase is positive. What does this mean in plain English?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. The model specification changed from linear_reg() to logistic_reg() |> set_mode("classification"). The recipe, workflow, and fit() are structurally identical. This is the key benefit of tidymodels — swap one line to change the model type.
A2. step_normalize() rescales each numeric variable to have mean = 0 and SD = 1. This puts predictors measured in different units (e.g., days vs. dollars) on a comparable scale, so their coefficients can be meaningfully compared.
A3. Customers who have been with FreshMart longer are more likely to be classified as High Value — holding other predictors constant. This makes intuitive sense: longer tenure gives more time to accumulate spend.

title: 10  Logistic Regression
section: 10.3 8.3 Evaluating a Classifier: The Confusion Matrix
text:
10.3 8.3 Evaluating a Classifier: The Confusion Matrix
A single accuracy number can be deceiving. If 75% of customers are not high-value, a model that always predicts FALSE achieves 75% accuracy — without learning anything. We need to look inside the errors.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The four cells:




Predicted: Not High Value
Predicted: High Value




Actual: Not High Value
✅ True Negative
❌ False Positive


Actual: High Value
❌ False Negative
✅ True Positive



For FreshMart’s goal (nurture high-value customers early), False Negatives are the costlier error — we miss a valuable customer. We’d prefer to occasionally send an offer to a regular customer (False Positive) than miss a future loyal one.

10.3.1 Accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.







Metric
Meaning




Accuracy
Overall fraction of correct predictions


Sensitivity
Of all actual High-Value customers, what fraction did we catch?


Specificity
Of all actual regular customers, what fraction did we correctly identify?





10.3.2 Adjusting the threshold and the ROC curve
By lowering the threshold below 0.5 we catch more high-value customers (higher sensitivity), at the cost of more false positives (lower specificity). The ROC curve shows this trade-off across every possible threshold at once.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
AUC of 1.0 is perfect; 0.5 is random. Values above 0.80 are generally considered good.






Important✏️ Concept Check — 8.3



Q1. Our model has accuracy = 0.84 and the test set is 75% negative. What is the “naive baseline” accuracy, and how much better is our model?
Q2. We lower the threshold from 0.5 to 0.3 to catch more high-value customers. What happens to the number of False Positives?
Q3. What does AUC = 0.50 tell you about a model?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. The naive baseline (always predict FALSE) achieves 75% accuracy. Our model at 84% beats it by 9 percentage points. Context makes a difference — 84% isn’t as impressive if we forget what the baseline is.
A2. False Positives increase. Lowering the threshold means more customers are flagged as High Value — including some regular customers who are misclassified. This is the sensitivity-specificity trade-off in action.
A3. A model with AUC = 0.50 is no better than random guessing — it has learned nothing useful from the predictors.

title: 10  Logistic Regression
11  Week 9: Regression Trees
text:
11.1 9.1 How a Decision Tree Thinks
A decision tree makes predictions by asking a series of yes/no questions about the data. It’s the model equivalent of the flowcharts you might draw on a whiteboard.
Here’s a simple example of what a FreshMart spending tree might look like:
Each path from the top to a leaf gives the model’s prediction for that group of customers. The prediction at each leaf is the average TotalSpend of the training customers who fell into that group.

title: 11  Week 9: Regression Trees
text:
Is TotalTransactions > 12?
    ├─ Yes → Is AvgTransactionValue > $80?
    │    ├─ Yes → Predict: $1,450
    │    └─ No   → Predict: $640
    └─ No   → Is DaysSinceFirstPurchase > 400?
         ├─ Yes → Predict: $195
         └─ No   → Predict: $72



11.1.1 How does the tree choose where to split?
At each node, the tree tries every possible split on every predictor and picks the one that most reduces the variance of the outcome within each resulting group. Splitting on “TotalTransactions > 12” is useful if it separates high-spending from low-spending customers more cleanly than any other cut. The goal is to create groups (leaves) where customers are as similar to each other as possible in terms of spend.






Important✏️ Concept Check — 9.1



Q1. In a regression tree, what value does the model predict at each leaf?
Q2. A tree splits on “TotalTransactions > 5.” After the split, one group has very similar spend values and the other has very diverse spend values. Was this a good split? Why?
Q3. What is one advantage a decision tree has over a linear regression equation, from a business communication standpoint?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. The mean of the outcome (TotalSpend) for all training observations that fell into that leaf.
A2. Not ideal — we’d prefer both groups to have low variance after the split (each group’s customers are similar to each other). A good split creates two homogeneous groups. If one group is still very diverse, the tree will need more splits to capture it.
A3. A decision tree produces a set of explicit if-then rules that any manager can read and understand without statistical training. An equation like TotalSpend = 25 + 18×Transactions + 0.4×Days... requires interpretation; a tree flowchart is self-explanatory.



The animation below shows how tree predictions (green/orange/red curve) change as depth increases. A shallow tree underfits — it’s too simple. A deep tree overfits — it memorises training noise and performs worse on test data. Watch the test RMSE (title) rise even as the curve gets more complex.

11.2.2 Specify and fit

11.2.3 Visualising the tree

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Reading the tree: start at the top and follow the yes/no branches. Each internal node shows the split condition. Each leaf (terminal node) shows the predicted spend and the percentage of training data that ended up there.






Note📦 R Practice — Exercise 9.2.1



Look at the tree diagram. Find the leaf that predicts the highest average spend. What path of yes/no decisions leads to that leaf? Write it out as a business rule in plain English (e.g., “Customers who visited more than X times AND had average transaction value above $Y are predicted to spend…”).








Tip✅ Solution





Trace the path from the root to the rightmost high-value leaf. It will typically be something like:
“Customers who made more than [X] visits and whose average transaction value exceeds $[Y] are predicted to spend approximately $[Z] lifetime.”
The exact numbers come from your tree diagram.









Important✏️ Concept Check — 9.2



Q1. tree_depth = 4 means the tree can ask at most 4 questions in sequence. What does tree_depth = 1 produce?
Q2. min_n = 10 means a node must have at least 10 observations to split further. What would happen if we set min_n = 1?
Q3. Compare the top split in your tree to the strongest predictor from the linear regression (Chapter 7). Are they the same variable?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. A single yes/no question — the simplest possible tree with just two leaves. Sometimes called a “stump.”
A2. The tree would keep splitting until every leaf contained only a single observation. It would perfectly predict the training data but would be terrible on new data — pure overfitting.
A3. Typically yes — the variable the tree first splits on is the most powerful predictor of spend, just as the largest coefficient in linear regression indicates the most important predictor. Both methods tend to agree on which variables matter most.

title: 11  Week 9: Regression Trees
text:
11.3 9.3 Overfitting and Tree Depth
A deep tree can memorise the training data almost perfectly — but fail badly on new data. This is called overfitting: the model has learned the noise in the training data, not the true underlying pattern.
We can see this by comparing training vs test RMSE across different tree depths.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
What to look for:

As depth increases, training RMSE always falls (the tree memorises more detail)
Test RMSE falls at first, then levels off or rises — this is where overfitting begins
The “sweet spot” is the depth where test RMSE is lowest before it starts increasing







Important✏️ Concept Check — 9.3



Q1. At depth = 10, training RMSE is much lower than test RMSE. What is happening?
Q2. At depth = 1, both training and test RMSE are high. What does this mean?
Q3. How is the overfitting problem in trees similar to the concept of using the test set (not the training set) for evaluation that we discussed in Chapter 7?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. The tree has memorised the training data — it knows exactly which leaf each training customer lands in. But those very specific rules don’t generalise; new customers don’t match those precise patterns, so test errors are much larger. This is overfitting.
A2. A depth-1 tree asks only one question. It’s too simple to capture the real pattern in the data — both training and test errors are high. This is underfitting — the opposite problem.
A3. Both reflect the same core idea: evaluating a model on the data it was trained on gives an overly optimistic picture. We always need to test on held-out data to see how the model truly performs on new, unseen observations.

title: 11  Week 9: Regression Trees
text:
11.4 9.4 Trees vs Linear Regression
Now let’s compare our best decision tree directly against the linear regression model from Chapter 7, using the same test set.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Key difference in predictions: The tree produces predictions that cluster at distinct values (the leaf averages), creating horizontal bands in the actual-vs-predicted plot. Linear regression produces a smooth spread of predictions. Trees tend to struggle more with very high-spend outliers like Marcus Lee — they can only predict within the range of their training leaf averages.






Important✏️ Concept Check — 9.4



Q1. In the actual-vs-predicted plot, why does the decision tree show horizontal “stripes” while linear regression shows a smoother diagonal?
Q2. Linear regression gives slightly better RMSE. Does this mean we should always use linear regression? What might favour choosing a tree?
Q3. Name one type of FreshMart business audience that would prefer seeing a decision tree over a regression equation.








Tip✅ Answers





A1. Because a tree can only predict the average spend of the training customers in each leaf — there are a limited number of distinct prediction values (one per leaf). Linear regression can output a unique value for every set of inputs.
A2. Not necessarily. A tree is far easier for non-technical stakeholders to understand and audit. If FreshMart needs to explain to store managers why a customer is being targeted, a tree’s rules are much clearer than a regression equation. Interpretability has real business value.
A3. Store managers, field sales teams, or customer service staff who need to apply rules manually would strongly prefer a tree. They can follow the flowchart without understanding regression.

text:
11.5 9.5 Chapter Exercises







NoteExercise 9.1 — Reading the Tree



Look at the rpart.plot diagram from Section 9.2. Without running any code, answer:

What is the very first question the tree asks (the root node split)?
What is the predicted spend for a customer who has made 5 transactions and has been with FreshMart for 200 days?
What percentage of training customers end up in the highest-spend leaf?









Tip✅ Solution





Trace through the tree manually from the root. Your tree diagram shows all the information needed:

The root split is shown at the top node.
Follow the path: Is TotalTransactions ≤ [split value]? → Yes. Then check DaysSinceFirstPurchase. Follow each branch based on the values given.
Read the percentage shown in the leaf node with the highest predicted value.

This exercise trains the skill of reading a model output without running code — an important practical skill when reviewing someone else’s model.










NoteExercise 9.2 — Depth Tuning



From the overfitting plot in Section 9.3, identify the optimal tree depth (lowest test RMSE). Fit a final tree at that depth and report its test RMSE and R².








Tip✅ Solution





# Identify best depth from the depth_results tibble
best_depth <- depth_results |>
  filter(set == "Test") |>
  slice_min(.estimate) |>
  pull(depth)

cat("Optimal depth:", best_depth, "\n")

# Fit final tree at optimal depth
final_tree <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(tree_recipe) |>
  add_model(decision_tree(tree_depth = best_depth, min_n = 10) |>
              set_engine("rpart") |> set_mode("regression")) |>
  fit(data = fm_train)

augment(final_tree, new_data = fm_test) |>
  metrics(truth = TotalSpend, estimate = .pred) |>
  filter(.metric %in% c("rmse", "rsq"))










NoteExercise 9.3 — Predicting with the Tree



Use your fitted tree to predict the total spend for Marcus Lee, Gary McSnarl, and Amara Diallo (same customer tibble as Exercise 8.1). Compare the predictions to the linear regression predictions from Chapter 7. Which model is closer to the actual spend for each character?








Tip✅ Solution





characters <- tibble(
  TotalTransactions      = c(48,    1,     14),
  AvgTransactionValue    = c(256.25, 34.90, 62.86),
  DaysSinceFirstPurchase = c(1868,  1026,  146),
  City                   = c("Sydney", "Melbourne", "Perth")
)

tree_preds <- predict(best_tree_fit, new_data = characters)
lm_preds   <- predict(lm_fit, new_data = characters)

tibble(
  Name   = c("Marcus Lee", "Gary McSnarl", "Amara Diallo"),
  Actual = c(12300, 34.90, 880),
  Tree   = round(tree_preds$.pred, 2),
  LM     = round(lm_preds$.pred, 2)
)
Marcus Lee ($12,300 actual) will likely be under-predicted by both models — he’s an extreme outlier. But compare which model gets closer and discuss why.










NoteExercise 9.4 — When to Use Each Model



For each scenario below, decide whether you’d recommend a linear regression or a decision tree, and briefly justify your choice:

FreshMart wants to predict customer lifetime spend for internal forecasting. The predictions will be used in a financial spreadsheet.
FreshMart’s store managers want to understand which customers to approach for a loyalty upgrade. The rules need to be explainable in a 30-second conversation.
FreshMart is building an automated email targeting system where the model runs 10,000 predictions per day with no human review.









Tip✅ Sample Answers






Linear regression — the predictions are continuous and the finance team cares about accuracy. R² and RMSE are familiar metrics in that context.
Decision tree — store managers need rules they can explain to customers. “We’re reaching out because you’ve visited more than 10 times and your average spend is over $50” is a clear tree-derived rule.
Either — in an automated system, interpretability matters less. Choose based on which has lower test RMSE. If both are similar, the linear regression may be preferable for its speed and simplicity.






  ✅ Week 9 Complete
  You can now compare two approaches to the same problem — equations vs rules. In Week 10 we return to classification (Chapter 8's problem: predicting High Value customers), but with a fundamentally different philosophy: instead of learning a decision boundary, KNN simply asks "who are the most similar customers to this person, and what did they do?"

title: 12  Week 10: K-Nearest Neighbours
text:
12.1 10.1 The Nearest Neighbours Idea
KNN is one of the most intuitive algorithms in machine learning. When asked to classify a new customer, it does this:
That’s it. No equations to fit, no parameters to estimate. KNN doesn’t learn a model from training data in the traditional sense — it memorises the training data and uses it directly at prediction time.
A concrete example:
Imagine plotting every customer on a chart with TotalTransactions on the x-axis and AvgTransactionValue on the y-axis. A new customer (Chloe) appears at the point (10, $65). We look at her 5 nearest neighbours (K=5):
KNN with K=5 predicts: TRUE (High Value) — majority vote.
The animation below makes the voting process concrete. Watch K expand from 1 to 15 — the red circle shows the search radius, highlighted points are the current neighbours, and the title shows the live majority vote.

title: 12  Week 10: K-Nearest Neighbours
text:
Measure the distance from the new customer to every customer in the training data
Find the K nearest customers (the most similar ones)
Take a majority vote — if most of those K neighbours are High Value, predict High Value; otherwise predict Not High Value





3 of them are High Value (TRUE)
2 of them are Not High Value (FALSE)



Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



12.1.1 Choosing K
The number of neighbours K is a hyperparameter — a setting we choose before training.

K = 1: predict based on just the single nearest neighbour. Very sensitive to noise — if that one neighbour is an outlier, the prediction will be wrong.
Large K: predict based on many neighbours — smoother, less sensitive to outliers, but may blur real boundaries.
Best K: found through cross-validation (covered in Section 10.3).







Important✏️ Concept Check — 10.1



Q1. A new customer has 5 nearest neighbours: TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE. With K=5, what does KNN predict?
Q2. With K=1, training accuracy is always 100%. Why is this not a useful result?
Q3. KNN is sometimes called a “lazy learner.” What do you think this means?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. TRUE — 3 out of 5 neighbours are TRUE, so the majority vote predicts High Value.
A2. With K=1, every training point’s nearest neighbour is itself — so it always predicts correctly on training data. But this tells us nothing about how it will perform on new, unseen customers. K=1 massively overfits.
A3. KNN doesn’t build a model during training — it just stores the data. All the “work” happens at prediction time (finding neighbours). Compare this to linear regression which does all its work up front (fitting coefficients) and then predicts instantly. KNN is “lazy” because it defers the computation until you actually need a prediction.

title: 12  Week 10: K-Nearest Neighbours
section: 12.2 10.2 Distance and the Critical Role of Scaling
text:
12.2 10.2 Distance and the Critical Role of Scaling
KNN uses distance to measure similarity. The most common choice is Euclidean distance — the straight-line distance between two points in multi-dimensional space.
For two customers with predictors \((x_1, x_2)\) and \((y_1, y_2)\):
\[d = \sqrt{(x_1 - y_1)^2 + (x_2 - y_2)^2}\]
The scaling problem: Imagine we use TotalTransactions (range: 1–65) and DaysSinceFirstPurchase (range: 14–1,460). Without scaling, a difference of 1 transaction contributes the same as a difference of 1 day — but \(1,460 days\) worth of range dwarfs \(64 transactions\) worth, dominating the distance calculation entirely. A predictor with a large range will overwhelm one with a small range.
The fix: step_normalize() rescales every predictor to have mean = 0 and SD = 1 before computing distances. After normalisation, a 1-unit difference is always 1 standard deviation — every variable is on the same footing.

After normalisation: every variable has mean ≈ 0 and SD ≈ 1. Now a 1-unit change in each variable represents the same relative shift.






Important✏️ Concept Check — 10.2



Q1. Without scaling, DaysSinceFirstPurchase has a much larger range than TotalTransactions. Which variable will dominate the distance calculation — and is this desirable?
Q2. After step_normalize(), what are the mean and standard deviation of each numeric predictor?
Q3. Does step_normalize() change the shape of each variable’s distribution? Explain.








Tip✅ Answers





A1. DaysSinceFirstPurchase will dominate — a 100-day difference will produce a large distance even if the customers have identical transaction behaviour. This is not desirable if we think transactions and days should have roughly equal importance.
A2. Mean ≈ 0 and SD ≈ 1 for every numeric predictor.
A3. No — normalisation shifts and stretches each variable, but the shape of the distribution (skewness, modality) remains exactly the same. It only changes the scale, not the distribution’s form.

title: 12  Week 10: K-Nearest Neighbours
section: 12.3 10.3 Fitting KNN and Choosing K with Cross-Validation
text:
12.3 10.3 Fitting KNN and Choosing K with Cross-Validation
We can’t use the test set to choose K — that would be “peeking” at the test data, making our final evaluation dishonest. Instead we use k-fold cross-validation on the training data.

12.3.1 What is cross-validation?
The training data is split into V equal-sized “folds.” The model is trained V times — each time using V−1 folds for training and 1 fold for validation. The final metric is the average across all V folds.

  5-Fold Cross-Validation (V = 5)
  
  Round 1: [VAL] [train] [train] [train] [train] → accuracy₁
  Round 2: [train] [VAL] [train] [train] [train] → accuracy₂
  Round 3: [train] [train] [VAL] [train] [train] → accuracy₃
  Round 4: [train] [train] [train] [VAL] [train] → accuracy₄
  Round 5: [train] [train] [train] [train] [VAL] → accuracy₅
  CV accuracy = mean(accuracy₁ … accuracy₅)
  



12.3.2 Tuning K from 1 to 20

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


12.3.3 Fit the final model with the best K

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Important✏️ Concept Check — 10.3



Q1. Why do we use cross-validation to choose K rather than using the test set?
Q2. In 5-fold CV, each data point appears in the validation set exactly once. How many times does each point appear in a training set?
Q3. The CV accuracy plot peaks at K=7, then slowly decreases. What does this tell us about K=7 vs K=15?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. Using the test set to choose K is a form of “data leakage” — we’d be optimising based on the test data, making our final test-set evaluation dishonest. Cross-validation uses only the training data, keeping the test set truly unseen.
A2. Each data point appears in a training set 4 times (in V-1 = 4 of the 5 rounds).
A3. K=7 achieves the best balance between bias and variance on the training folds. K=15 averages over too many neighbours, blurring the decision boundary and reducing accuracy. K=7 is the “sweet spot” for this dataset.

title: 12  Week 10: K-Nearest Neighbours
section: 12.4 10.4 KNN vs Logistic Regression — A Direct Comparison
text:
12.4 10.4 KNN vs Logistic Regression — A Direct Comparison
We’ve now solved the same problem (classify IsHighValue) with two different methods. Let’s compare them directly on the test set.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Discussion: The two models may perform similarly — this is common with well-tuned KNN vs logistic regression on clean data. The key question is not just performance, but also:









Logistic Regression
KNN




Interpretability
High — coefficients explain the model
Low — no equation, just “look at neighbours”


Prediction speed
Fast — just apply the equation
Slower — scan all training data


Handles non-linearity
Poor (assumes linear boundary)
Well (flexible boundary)


Needs scaling
Helpful but not critical
Essential









Important✏️ Concept Check — 10.4



Q1. KNN achieves higher accuracy than logistic regression on this dataset. A colleague says “then we should always use KNN.” What’s wrong with this reasoning?
Q2. A FreshMart manager asks: “Why did the model predict that this customer would be high-value?” How would you answer this question for KNN vs logistic regression?
Q3. We didn’t include City as a predictor in the KNN recipe. Why might including categorical variables be more complicated for KNN?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. Accuracy on one dataset doesn’t mean “always best.” KNN may be slower on large datasets, harder to explain to stakeholders, and may not generalise as well if the data changes. Model choice depends on accuracy, speed, interpretability, and business context.
A2. For logistic regression: “The model assigned higher probability because the customer has made 18 transactions and their coefficient for TotalTransactions is positive — frequent visitors are more likely to be high-value.” For KNN: “The 7 most similar customers in our training data were mostly high-value. Similarity is measured by visit frequency and average spend per trip.” The logistic regression answer is more satisfying for audit purposes.
A3. KNN relies on distance — and distance isn’t naturally defined for categorical variables (e.g., what is the “distance” between Sydney and Melbourne?). We need to either encode categories as dummy variables and be careful about their scale, or use a different distance metric. For this reason, simpler KNN implementations often work best with numeric predictors only.

The table below shows five training customers. Using only TotalTransactions and AvgTransactionValue (already normalised), calculate the Euclidean distance from a new customer with TotalTransactions = 0.5 and AvgTransactionValue = 0.8 to each training customer. Predict the class using K=3.

Tip✅ Solution





Euclidean distance from new customer (0.5, 0.8):

To A: √((0.5−(−1.2))² + (0.8−(−0.9))²) = √(1.7² + 1.7²) = √(2.89 + 2.89) = √5.78 ≈ 2.40
To B: √((0.5−0.4)² + (0.8−0.7)²) = √(0.01 + 0.01) = √0.02 ≈ 0.14
To C: √((0.5−1.8)² + (0.8−1.5)²) = √(1.69 + 0.49) = √2.18 ≈ 1.48
To D: √((0.5−(−0.5))² + (0.8−0.2)²) = √(1.00 + 0.36) = √1.36 ≈ 1.17
To E: √((0.5−0.6)² + (0.8−0.9)²) = √(0.01 + 0.01) = √0.02 ≈ 0.14

3 nearest: B (0.14), E (0.14), D (1.17) → Labels: TRUE, TRUE, FALSE → Predict: TRUE










NoteExercise 10.2 — The Effect of K



Using the knn_tune results object from Section 10.3, extract and display the cross-validated sensitivity (not just accuracy) for K = 1, 5, 10, 15, and 20. Which K maximises sensitivity? Is it the same K that maximises accuracy?








Tip✅ Solution





collect_metrics(knn_tune) |>
  filter(.metric == "sensitivity", neighbors %in% c(1, 5, 10, 15, 20)) |>
  select(neighbors, mean, std_err) |>
  arrange(neighbors)
The K that maximises accuracy and the K that maximises sensitivity may differ. For FreshMart’s loyalty campaign, sensitivity matters most — flag as many genuine high-value customers as possible, even at the cost of some false positives.










NoteExercise 10.3 — Predict the Characters



Use the final KNN model to classify Marcus Lee, Gary McSnarl, and Amara Diallo. Compare to the logistic regression predictions from Chapter 8. Do the models agree?
characters <- tibble(
  TotalTransactions      = c(48,    1,     14),
  AvgTransactionValue    = c(256.25, 34.90, 62.86),
  DaysSinceFirstPurchase = c(1868,  1026,  146)
)








Tip✅ Solution





knn_char <- augment(final_knn, new_data = characters)
lr_char  <- augment(lr_fit,   new_data =
  characters |> mutate(City = c("Sydney","Melbourne","Perth")))

tibble(
  Name  = c("Marcus Lee", "Gary McSnarl", "Amara Diallo"),
  KNN   = knn_char$.pred_class,
  LR    = lr_char$.pred_class,
  Actual = c("TRUE", "FALSE", "TRUE")
)
Both models should agree on Marcus Lee (clear TRUE) and Gary McSnarl (clear FALSE). Amara Diallo is more interesting — her short tenure but high activity makes her harder to classify.










NoteExercise 10.4 — Model Selection Memo



You are presenting to FreshMart’s Head of Analytics. Write a short recommendation (3–4 sentences) on whether to deploy logistic regression or KNN for the high-value customer identification task. Justify your choice using at least two criteria (e.g., accuracy, interpretability, speed, ease of retraining).








Tip✅ Sample Answer





“Both models achieve similar accuracy on the test set. We recommend logistic regression for production deployment for two reasons: first, when a customer or manager asks why a particular customer was flagged, we can explain it clearly using the coefficients (e.g., high visit frequency is the strongest signal). Second, logistic regression predicts almost instantly because it’s just a formula — KNN requires scanning all training customers at prediction time, which becomes slow as the customer base grows. If interpretability is ever challenged by customers or regulators, logistic regression is easier to defend.”





  ✅ Week 10 Complete
  Weeks 7–10 all shared one thing: a labelled target variable to predict. In Week 11 we remove that requirement entirely. Clustering finds natural groups in the data with no labels — purely from the patterns of similarity between customers. It's how FreshMart discovers customer segments it didn't even know existed.

title: 13  Clustering
text:
13.1 11.1 Supervised vs Unsupervised Learning
Supervised learning (Chapters 7–10): We have a labelled dataset where the “right answer” is known for each training observation. The model learns to predict that answer for new observations.
Unsupervised learning (this chapter): There is no right answer. We’re not predicting — we’re discovering. We want to find structure, patterns, or natural groupings in the data that weren’t defined in advance.
FreshMart’s business question today: The CMO calls you into a meeting and says: “I don’t think all our customers are the same. I feel like we have different types of customers — frequent shoppers, occasional big spenders, people who signed up but barely come back. Can you show me the data on this?”
This is a clustering problem. We’re not predicting whether someone is “High Value” — we’re discovering what types of customers actually exist in the data.

title: 13  Clustering
text:
Important✏️ Concept Check — 11.1



Q1. In supervised learning, we use a target variable to train the model. What is used to guide clustering instead?
Q2. After running a clustering algorithm and finding 3 groups, a colleague says “these are the only 3 types of FreshMart customers.” Is this a fair statement? Why not?
Q3. Name one real-world business application of clustering other than customer segmentation.








Tip✅ Answers





A1. Clustering is guided purely by similarity (distance) between observations. Points that are close together in the feature space end up in the same cluster. There is no external label or correct answer — only the structure of the data itself.
A2. No — clustering is a simplification. The number of clusters K is chosen by the analyst, and the resulting groups are the algorithm’s best approximation of natural groupings. With K=3 we find 3 groups, with K=5 we’d find 5. The “true” number of customer types is unknowable; clustering gives us a useful approximation.
A3. Examples: grouping news articles by topic (text clustering), grouping geographic areas by demographic similarity (for store location decisions), detecting unusual transactions (anomaly detection), grouping genes by expression pattern in biology.

title: 13  Clustering
text:
13.2 11.2 The K-Means Algorithm
K-means is the most widely used clustering algorithm. It works by repeatedly improving a set of K cluster centres (called centroids):

  K-Means: Step by Step
  
    Initialise: Randomly place K centroids in the data space
    Assign: Each data point is assigned to its nearest centroid
    Update: Each centroid moves to the mean of the points assigned to it
    Repeat steps 2–3 until cluster assignments stop changing
  
  The algorithm converges when no points change cluster — usually within 10–50 iterations.


13.2.1 K-means measures within-cluster distance
The quality of a clustering solution is measured by Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WSS): the total squared distance of every point from its centroid. A lower WSS means tighter, more compact clusters.
K-means tries to minimise WSS — it’s looking for tight, well-separated groups.
Important: Like KNN, K-means uses distance — so scaling is essential before running it.






Important✏️ Concept Check — 11.2



Q1. K-means requires you to specify K before running. What is a drawback of this requirement?
Q2. K-means uses distance to assign points to clusters. Why must we scale variables before running it?
Q3. K-means is sensitive to the random initial placement of centroids. Two runs of K-means on the same data with different random seeds can produce different results. How does set.seed() help with this?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. We must guess or search for the right number of clusters before we know the answer. In practice we run K-means for several values of K and use metrics (like the elbow method) to choose.
A2. A variable with a large range (e.g., TotalSpend: $0–$12,000) will dominate the distance calculation over a variable with a small range (e.g., TotalTransactions: 0–65). Scaling ensures every variable contributes equally.
A3. set.seed() fixes the random number generator, so the algorithm always starts with the same initial centroids. This makes the results reproducible — you and a colleague running the same code get the same clusters.



The animation below shows K-means converging from randomly placed starting centroids (✕ markers). Each iteration, points are reassigned to their nearest centroid and the centroids shift to the new cluster means. Watch them home in on the true cluster centres.

13.3.3 Profiling the clusters
Knowing which cluster each customer is in is only the starting point. The real insight comes from understanding what makes each cluster different.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Note📦 R Practice — Exercise 11.3.1



Find Marcus Lee, Gary McSnarl, Bev O’Brien, and Nina Patel in freshmart_clustered. Which cluster is each person assigned to? Does the assignment match your intuition about their spending behaviour?








Tip✅ Solution





freshmart_clustered |>
  filter(FirstName %in% c("Marcus", "Gary", "Bev", "Nina")) |>
  select(FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend, TotalTransactions, .cluster)
Expect Marcus Lee and Bev O’Brien in the high-spend cluster; Gary McSnarl and Nina Patel in the low-spend/inactive cluster. If two different characters end up in the same cluster, discuss what they have in common.









Important✏️ Concept Check — 11.3



Q1. The recipe uses ~ TotalSpend + TotalTransactions + ... with no outcome on the left side of ~. Why is there no outcome variable here?
Q2. Two customers are assigned to the same cluster. Does this mean they are identical? What does it mean?
Q3. We chose K=3 for this initial fit. What information would you use to decide whether 3 is the right choice?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. Clustering is unsupervised — there is no variable we’re trying to predict. The ~ notation with predictors on the right side tells the recipe which variables to use for measuring distance. Nothing appears on the left because there is no “target.”
A2. Not identical — they are the most similar group the algorithm could construct given K=3. Within a cluster, customers will vary; they’re just more similar to each other than to customers in other clusters. The cluster is a simplification.
A3. The elbow method — plot WSS (within-cluster sum of squares) against K and look for the “elbow” where adding more clusters gives diminishing returns. We cover this in Section 11.4.

title: 13  Clustering
text:
13.4 11.4 Choosing K: The Elbow Method
There is no single “correct” K. We use the elbow method to find a K where adding more clusters stops providing much improvement.
WSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares) measures how compact the clusters are. As K increases, WSS always decreases — with K equal to the number of data points, every point is its own cluster and WSS = 0. We want the K beyond which WSS barely improves.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Interpreting the plot: Find the “elbow” — the point where the curve bends and the improvement starts to flatten. If the WSS drops sharply from K=1 to K=3 but barely changes from K=3 to K=4, then K=3 is likely a good choice. Beyond the elbow, extra clusters add complexity without much gain in quality.






NoteThere’s no perfect answer



The elbow is often a judgement call, not a precise point. Two analysts looking at the same plot might choose K=3 or K=4. That’s normal — clustering is exploratory, and the “best” solution is the one that tells the most useful business story. Always ask: do these clusters make sense? Can we name them and use them?








Important✏️ Concept Check — 11.4



Q1. WSS always decreases as K increases. Does a smaller WSS always mean a better clustering? Why not?
Q2. The elbow plot shows a sharp drop from K=1 to K=2, a moderate drop to K=3, and tiny drops from K=4 onwards. What K would you choose?
Q3. Two colleagues both look at the same elbow plot — one suggests K=3, the other K=4. How would you decide between them?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. No. With K equal to the number of data points, WSS = 0 — but 500 clusters of 1 customer each is meaningless for business purposes. We want the fewest clusters that still capture meaningful differences.
A2. K=3 — the point of diminishing returns begins at K=4. Adding a 4th cluster only slightly reduces WSS, meaning the data doesn’t naturally support a 4th distinct group.
A3. Fit both K=3 and K=4, profile each cluster’s characteristics, and ask: does the 4th cluster in K=4 tell a meaningfully different business story, or is it just splitting an existing group? If the extra cluster doesn’t have a clear interpretation or actionable implication, K=3 is preferable.

title: 13  Clustering
section: 13.5 11.5 Naming the Clusters and Business Recommendations
text:
13.5 11.5 Naming the Clusters and Business Recommendations
Numbers are not a story. “Cluster 2” means nothing to a CMO. After profiling, we give each cluster a name that describes its business meaning — and then propose an action.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Based on the profile output, we can name the clusters. A typical K=3 result on FreshMart data produces something like:

  
    🌟 Loyal Champions
    High spend, frequent visits, long tenure. FreshMart's most valuable customers.
    → Action: reward & retain
  
  
    💛 Growing Shoppers
    Moderate spend, building visit frequency, mixed tenure.
    → Action: upsell & grow
  
  
    💤 Dormant / At Risk
    Low spend, few visits, some newly joined and some long-inactive.
    → Action: re-engage
  


13.5.1 Visualise with segment labels

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Important✏️ Concept Check — 11.5



Q1. Cluster names like “Loyal Champions” and “Dormant / At Risk” are invented by the analyst — the algorithm only produces numbers. Why is naming the clusters an important step?
Q2. The “Dormant / At Risk” cluster contains both new customers (few transactions, recently joined) and older inactive ones (few transactions, joined years ago). Is this a problem? What does it suggest?
Q3. FreshMart’s CMO asks: “Can we add a fourth segment for high-spending but infrequent customers?” How would you respond?








Tip✅ Answers





A1. Names connect the statistical output to business action. “Cluster_2” cannot be acted on; “Growing Shoppers — upsell & grow” can. Naming is also how you communicate findings to non-technical stakeholders and make the analysis actually useful.
A2. It suggests these two groups may actually be distinct in the data — they just got merged because K=3 isn’t granular enough. Running K=4 or K=5 and re-profiling might separate “recently joined, not yet engaged” from “long-term inactive, at risk of churning” — which would call for different marketing responses.
A3. “Yes — that’s a testable idea. If we run K=4 or K=5 and one of the new clusters matches the profile you’re describing (high spend + low transaction count, i.e., high AvgTransactionValue), then it exists in the data. Let’s try it and see.”

title: 13  Clustering
text:
13.6 11.6 Chapter Exercises







NoteExercise 11.1 — Interpreting the Elbow Plot



From the elbow plot in Section 11.4, identify your chosen K and explain the reasoning in two sentences. Then re-run the K-means with K=4 and produce a profile table. Does the extra cluster add a meaningful new story, or does it just split an existing cluster?








Tip✅ Solution





# Fit K = 4
set.seed(42)
km_fit_4 <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(km_recipe) |>
  add_model(k_means(num_clusters = 4)) |>
  fit(data = fm_cluster)

fm_cluster |>
  bind_cols(extract_cluster_assignment(km_fit_4)) |>
  group_by(.cluster) |>
  summarise(
    n             = n(),
    avg_spend     = round(mean(TotalSpend), 0),
    avg_txns      = round(mean(TotalTransactions), 1),
    avg_tenure    = round(mean(DaysSinceFirstPurchase), 0)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_spend))
Compare the K=4 profile to K=3. Does the fourth cluster represent a genuinely new customer type (e.g., separating “high spend, long tenure” from “high spend, short tenure”)? If yes, it adds value. If it just splits an existing cluster roughly in half with similar profiles, K=3 was sufficient.










NoteExercise 11.2 — Finding the Characters



Using freshmart_clustered from Section 11.3, find all 7 named FreshMart characters (Marcus Lee, Gary McSnarl, Nina Patel, Jake Nguyen, Bev O’Brien, Amara Diallo, Priya Sharma). For each one, state which cluster they are in and whether the assignment makes intuitive sense given what we know about them from the SQL chapters.








Tip✅ Solution





freshmart_clustered |>
  filter(FirstName %in% c("Marcus","Gary","Nina","Jake","Bev","Amara","Priya")) |>
  select(FirstName, LastName, TotalSpend, TotalTransactions, DaysSinceFirstPurchase, .cluster) |>
  arrange(desc(TotalSpend))
Expected assignments: - Marcus Lee → Loyal Champions (highest spend, 48 transactions) - Bev O’Brien → Loyal Champions (long tenure, consistent shopper) - Amara Diallo → Loyal Champions or Growing Shoppers (high recent spend) - Priya Sharma → Growing Shoppers (medium-tier profile) - Jake Nguyen → Growing Shoppers (moderate spend, regular visits) - Gary McSnarl → Dormant / At Risk (1 transaction, very low spend) - Nina Patel → Dormant / At Risk (0 transactions, never purchased)
Discussion point: Gary McSnarl and Nina Patel are both “low engagement” but for different reasons — Nina never bought anything, Gary bought once and left. K=3 puts them together; K=4 might separate them.










NoteExercise 11.3 — Connecting to Earlier Chapters



We spent four chapters (7–10) building supervised models that classified customers into IsHighValue = TRUE/FALSE. Now in Chapter 11 we’ve clustered them without using that label.
Answer these two questions:

Does the “Loyal Champions” cluster roughly correspond to the IsHighValue = TRUE group from Chapter 8? Calculate the percentage of each cluster that is High Value.
Is clustering doing the same job as logistic regression? What is the fundamental difference?









Tip✅ Solution





# Add IsHighValue back to the clustered data
freshmart_clustered |>
  left_join(freshmart |> select(CustomerID, IsHighValue), by = "CustomerID") |>
  group_by(.cluster) |>
  summarise(
    pct_high_value = round(mean(IsHighValue) * 100, 1)
  )
The Loyal Champions cluster should have a very high % of High Value customers. But the clusters are NOT the same as IsHighValue:

Clustering finds groups that are internally similar across all four variables simultaneously — spend, transactions, value, and tenure together.
Logistic regression only cares about predicting IsHighValue — it optimises for that single boundary.
A customer could be in the “Growing Shoppers” cluster despite being technically “High Value” (if their spend just crossed $500 but their visit pattern is still developing).

They answer different questions: logistic regression asks “is this customer high value?”; clustering asks “what type of customer is this?”










NoteExercise 11.4 — The CMO Report



Write a one-page summary (4–5 short paragraphs) that you would present to FreshMart’s CMO. Include:

What question you were trying to answer
What method you used (in plain language — no technical jargon)
What the three customer segments look like
One specific marketing recommendation for each segment
One limitation of the analysis the CMO should be aware of

This is the final exercise of the statistical modelling module — it ties together everything from Chapters 6–11.








Tip✅ Sample Answer





Question: We wanted to understand whether FreshMart’s 500 customers naturally group into distinct types — without assuming in advance what those types should be.
Method: We used a technique called K-means clustering, which places each customer into a group based on how similar they are to their groupmates across four measures: total lifetime spend, number of store visits, average spend per visit, and length of time as a customer. We tested several numbers of groups and found that three was the natural sweet spot.
What we found:

Loyal Champions (approx. X customers): Long-term, frequent visitors who consistently spend more per trip. Marcus Lee and Bev O’Brien are in this group. They drive a disproportionate share of FreshMart’s revenue.
Growing Shoppers (approx. Y customers): Moderately active customers who visit regularly but haven’t yet hit the highest spend tiers. Priya Sharma and Jake Nguyen typify this segment.
Dormant / At Risk (approx. Z customers): Customers with very few transactions and low spend — some newly registered and not yet engaged, others inactive for a long time. Gary McSnarl and Nina Patel are here.

Recommendations:

Loyal Champions: Protect and reward. Offer exclusive loyalty tier benefits — early access to specials, personalised “thank you” messages. Losing one of these customers costs far more than keeping them.
Growing Shoppers: Invest to grow. Targeted offers on slightly higher-value product categories, gentle prompts to reach the next loyalty tier.
Dormant / At Risk: Re-engage or accept churn. A simple “We miss you — here’s $5 off” campaign. If unresponsive after two touch points, focus resources elsewhere.

Limitation: The three segments are a simplification of reality — customers exist on a continuous spectrum. Two customers in the same segment may have very different stories (e.g., Nina Patel never bought; Gary McSnarl tried and left). The model is a useful starting point, not a complete picture.
